results.title_inferred: Table of Contents: Medicinal Book, Chapters on Betonica (Bishopwort) and Waybread (Plantago)
results.original_text:
CHAPTERS OF THE MEDICINAL BOOK.

1. Name of wort betonica, that is, bishopwort.

1. For monstrous nocturnal visitors and frightful sights and dreams.
2. If a mans head be broken.
3. For sore of eyes.
4. For sore of ears.
5. For dimness of eyes.
6. For strong blood-running from the nose.
7. For bleared eyes.
8. For tooth ache.
9. For sore of side.
10. For sore of the broad of the loins.
11. For sore of belly.
12. In case a mans inwards be too costive.
13. In case blood gush up through a mans mouth.
14. In case a man have a mind not to be drunken.
15. In case a pustule [or carbuncle] is going to settle on a man.
16. In case a man be inwardly ruptured.
17. In case a man become tired with much riding or walking.
18. In case a man be out of health or feel nausea.
19. That a mans meat may easily digest.
20. In case a man cannot retain his meat.
21. For sore of inwards, or if they be swollen.
22. For taking of poison.
23. For bite of snake.
24. Again, for bite of snake.
25. For bite of mad dog.
26. In case a mans throat be sore or any part of his neck.
27. For sore of loins, and if a mans thighs ache.
28. For the hot fever.
29. For foot disease.

II. The herb ἀρνόγλωσσος, that is, waybread.

1. For head ache.
2. For sore of wamb or belly.
3. For sore of inwards.
4. Again, in case a man be ill grown in wamb.
5. In case a man have a running of blood from anus.
6. In case a man is badly wounded.
7. In case one wishes to make a mans wamb dwindle.
8. For rend of adder.
9. Again, for rend of adder.
10. For inward worms.
11. In case a mans body be hardened.
12. In case a man have a fever of four days.
13. For sick disease and for [...]
results.translation:
CHAPTERS OF THE MEDICINAL BOOK.

I. Name of wort betonica [Betony], that is, bishopwort [Betonica officinalis].

Chapter headings for betonica:
1. For monstrous nocturnal visitors [nightmares/demonic visitations] and frightful sights and dreams.
2. If a man's head be broken [head wound/fracture].
3. For soreness of the eyes.
4. For soreness of the ears.
5. For dimness of the eyes.
6. For heavy bleeding from the nose.
7. For bleared [rheumy/inflamed] eyes.
8. For toothache.
9. For soreness of the side.
10. For soreness of the broad of the loins.
11. For soreness of the belly.
12. In case a man's inwards be too costive [constipated].
13. In case blood gush up through a man's mouth [haemoptysis or haematemesis].
14. In case a man wishes not to become drunk.
15. In case a pustule [or carbuncle] is going to settle upon a man.
16. In case a man be inwardly ruptured [hernia or internal rupture].
17. In case a man become tired with much riding or walking.
18. In case a man be out of health or feel nausea.
19. That a man's food may easily digest.
20. In case a man cannot retain his food.
21. For soreness of the inwards, or if they be swollen.
22. For taking of poison [ingested poison].
23. For bite of snake.
24. Again, for bite of snake.
25. For bite of a mad dog [rabid dog].
26. In case a man's throat be sore or any part of his neck.
27. For soreness of the loins, and if a man's thighs ache.
28. For the hot fever [as distinguished from the cold fever or ague].
29. For disease of the foot.

II. The herb ἀρνόγλωσσος [arnoglōssos], that is, waybread [Plantago major].

Chapter headings for waybread:
1. For headache.
2. For soreness of the wamb [belly/womb].
3. For soreness of the inwards.
4. Again, in case a man be ill grown in the wamb [abdominal growth/swelling].
5. In case a man have a running of blood from the anus.
6. In case a man is badly wounded.
7. In case one wishes to make a man's wamb dwindle [reduce abdominal swelling].
8. For the bite [rend] of an adder.
9. Again, for the bite of an adder.
10. For inward worms [intestinal worms].
11. In case a man's body be hardened [stiffness/paralysis].
12. In case a man have a quartan fever [fever recurring every four days].
13. For sick disease and for [text incomplete/continues].

entities_mentioned.name: Betonica / Bishopwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Betonica officinalis (syn. Stachys betonica), Wood Betony
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly labelled 'B. officinalis' in the source margin. One of the most widely used medicinal herbs in early medieval European herbalism. Indications listed include head wounds, eye and ear complaints, nosebleed, toothache, digestive problems, fever, snakebite, poison, nightmares, fatigue, and more.

entities_mentioned.name: Waybread / ἀρνόγλωσσος (arnoglōssos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly labelled 'Plantago major' in the source margin. The Greek name ἀρνόγλωσσος ('lamb's tongue') was the classical designation. Indications listed include headache, abdominal disorders, wounds, snakebite, intestinal worms, fever, and haemorrhage.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a table of contents (chapter list) from the Old English Herbarium (Herbarium Apuleii Platonici), here in Cockayne's 19th-century edition (vol. 1). The Old English chapter headings in the lower portion of the page are partially transcribed in the original source alongside Cockayne's English translations; both are incorporated here. The footnote variants (B., H., V. manuscript sigla) are textual witnesses to different manuscript traditions and are not translated but noted. The word 'wamb' is retained in translation where Cockayne uses it, as it carries ambiguity between 'belly' and 'womb' in Old English. 'Pustule or carbuncle' reflects the editorial footnote in the original. The Greek term is rendered in standard Unicode. Chapter 13 of the waybread section is cut off in the source text and marked as incomplete.

results.title_inferred: Anglo-Saxon Wound Salves and Wound Drinks (Sections 9–10)
results.original_text:
9. A wound salve: pound very thoroughly, goutweed, mingle with butter, seethe thoroughly, and boil, and wring through a cloth, skim off the foam, salt it very well; if the wound get foul, chew strailwort upon it and yarrow. A wound salve: take ribwort and yarrow, and the netherward part of pellitory, and dock, and goose dung, and a little pitch, and honey, boil in butter, apply it to the wound, then it cleanseth and healeth. A wound salve: take yarrow and leechwort, boil in butter.

10. A salve to the end that a wound may not foul: take briar, on which hips wax, that is, dog rose, chew the rind and let it drop on the wound, then it will not foul. A wound salve: the netherward part of meadow wort, lustmock, hove, everfern, boil in honey, add thick mashwort among them. A drink for wounds: the netherward part of everthroat, that is, cavline thistle, and meadow sweet, so also the nether and upward part of agrimony, boil the worts in ale, barm them with yeast, that is, introduce fermentation with yeast, administer to drink.
results.translation:
9. A wound salve: pound very thoroughly goutweed [Aegopodium podagraria], mix with butter, simmer thoroughly and boil, then strain through a cloth, skim off the foam, and salt it well. If the wound becomes infected [foul], chew strailwort [identity uncertain] and yarrow upon it. A wound salve: take ribwort [Plantago lanceolata] and yarrow [Achillea millefolium], and the lower [root] part of pellitory [Anacyclus pyrethrum or Parietaria officinalis], and dock [Rumex sp.], and goose dung, and a little pitch, and honey; boil in butter and apply to the wound — it will cleanse and heal. A wound salve: take yarrow and leechwort [identity uncertain], and boil in butter.

10. A salve to prevent a wound from becoming infected: take briar upon which hips grow — that is, dog rose [Rosa canina] — chew the rind and let it drop onto the wound; it will not then become infected [foul]. A wound salve: take the lower [root] part of meadow wort [possibly Filipendula ulmaria or a related species], lustmock [identity uncertain], hove [ground ivy, Glechoma hederacea], and everfern [possibly polypody, Polypodium vulgare]; boil in honey and add thick mashwort [wort mash, i.e., a thick herbal infusion or malt-wort residue] among them. A drink for wounds: take the lower [root] part of everthroat — that is, carline thistle [Carlina vulgaris] — and meadow sweet [Filipendula ulmaria], and likewise the lower and upper parts of agrimony [Agrimonia eupatoria]; boil the plants in ale, ferment with yeast (that is, introduce fermentation by adding yeast), and administer as a drink.

entities_mentioned.name: goutweed
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as primary ingredient in a wound salve; pounded and boiled in butter.

entities_mentioned.name: strailwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied by chewing onto a foul (infected) wound. Identity not confidently established; possibly a corruption of an Old English plant name.

entities_mentioned.name: yarrow
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in multiple recipes: chewed onto infected wounds, boiled in butter with ribwort, and boiled in butter with leechwort.

entities_mentioned.name: ribwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound salve; boiled in butter with other ingredients.

entities_mentioned.name: pellitory
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'netherward part' (root or lower stem) used in wound salve. Both species are recorded under this name in Anglo-Saxon herbalism.

entities_mentioned.name: dock
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound salve boiled in butter with other ingredients.

entities_mentioned.name: goose dung
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal-derived ingredient added to wound salve; not a plant.

entities_mentioned.name: pitch
entities_mentioned.notes: Small amount added to wound salve as a binding or antiseptic agent.

entities_mentioned.name: leechwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in butter with yarrow for a wound salve. The Old English name literally means 'physician's plant' or 'leech-plant,' but the specific species is disputed.

entities_mentioned.name: dog rose / briar
entities_mentioned.notes: Text explicitly glosses 'briar on which hips wax' as dog rose. Rind chewed and dripped onto wound to prevent infection.

entities_mentioned.name: meadow wort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Filipendula ulmaria (meadowsweet) or related Spiraea/Filipendula sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Lower (root) part boiled in honey for wound salve.

entities_mentioned.name: lustmock
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity uncertain; appears in wound salve recipe boiled in honey.

entities_mentioned.name: hove
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'hofe'; used in wound salve boiled in honey.

entities_mentioned.name: everfern
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polypodium vulgare (polypody fern) or possibly Dryopteris sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound salve boiled in honey. The 'ever-' prefix may suggest an evergreen fern.

entities_mentioned.name: mashwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely a thick herbal mash or malt-wort residue added to wound salve for consistency or medicinal effect.

entities_mentioned.name: everthroat / carline thistle
entities_mentioned.notes: Text explicitly glosses 'everthroat' as 'cavline thistle' (carline thistle). Lower root part used in wound drink boiled in ale.

entities_mentioned.name: meadow sweet
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound drink boiled in ale with carline thistle and agrimony.

entities_mentioned.name: agrimony
entities_mentioned.notes: Both upper and lower parts used in wound drink boiled in ale and fermented with yeast.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Thomas Oswald Cockayne's 'Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England' (Vol. 2), preserving his mid-19th-century English rendering of Old English medical texts. The source is Anglo-Saxon in origin (likely from the 'Lacnunga' or a related leechbook). Several plant names — 'strailwort,' 'lustmock,' 'leechwort,' 'everfern,' 'mashwort' — remain difficult or impossible to identify with confidence; they are retained as Cockayne rendered them. The OCR error 'thoroughl}^' (section 9) has been silently corrected to 'thoroughly.' The phrase 'netherward part' consistently refers to the root or lower portion of the plant. The brewing/fermentation instruction in section 10 ('barm them with yeast') is an unusual wound treatment indicating internal administration of a fermented ale decoction. The gloss 'cavline thistle' for 'everthroat' almost certainly represents 'carline thistle' (Carlina), and has been so identified.

results.title_inferred: Wound Drinks and Wound Salve (Leech Book I, Recipes 38–39)
results.original_text: 11. A wound drink: pound small, cuckoo sour, wild cunila, cockle, the netherward part of carline thistle, ashthroat, put them into cold water, rub between the hands, strain through a cloth, administer to drink a full draught to the sick [patient] his nights fasting. A wound drink: pound small the netherward and upward part of ribwort, carline thistle, and the netherward part of ashthroat, put them into boiling water, rub between the hands, and strain through a cloth, administer to drink. A salve for every wound: collect cow dung, cow stale [urine], work up a large kettle full into a batter as a man worketh soap, then take appletree rind, and ash rind, sloethorn rind, and myrtle rind, and elm rind, and holly rind, and withy rind, and the rind of a young oak, sallow rind, put them all in a mickle kettle, pour the batter upon them, boil very long, then remove [the rinds], boil the batter [down] until it be thick, set always on [a fire of] four [or] two logs with a kettle as low as it may be, pour into a vessel when it be thick enough, then heat chalk-stone very [hot] and gather [the residue] and sift through a cloth and [put] the chalk-stone also into the batter, smear with that [the] bowl [of a wound]. Again for the same: take hops and gladdon [iris] and brown wort and lusmore [foxglove] twigs and hare's spreckle [spotted plant], boil in butter and then wring off the plants, add others: ribwort, bishopwort, yarrow, attorlothe [viper's bugloss or similar], put these in the same butter, boil again, wring well off — that is a good wound salve.
results.translation:
Recipe 11. A wound drink: pound finely cuckoo-sorrel [wood sorrel], wild cunila [a savory-type herb], cockle, the lower part of carline thistle, and ashthroat [probably cleavers or a similar plant]; put them into cold water, rub between the hands, strain through a cloth, and administer a full draught to the sick patient after a night's fasting.

A second wound drink: pound finely both the lower and upper parts of ribwort [plantain], carline thistle, and the lower part of ashthroat; put them into boiling water, rub between the hands, strain through a cloth, and administer to drink.

A salve for every kind of wound: collect cow dung and cow urine, work a large kettleful up into a batter as one works soap. Then take appletree bark, ash bark, sloe-thorn bark, myrtle bark, elm bark, holly bark, willow bark, the bark of a young oak, and sallow bark; put them all into a large kettle, pour the batter over them, and boil for a very long time. Then remove the bark pieces and boil the batter [down] until it is thick, keeping it always on a low fire of four or two logs with the kettle set as low as possible. Pour it into a vessel when it is sufficiently thick. Then heat a chalk-stone very hot, collect [the powder], sift it through a cloth, and add the chalk-stone into the batter as well; apply this to the bowl [interior] of the wound.

Again, for the same purpose: take hops, gladdon [a species of iris], brownwort, lusmore [foxglove] twigs, and hare's spreckle [a spotted plant]; boil in butter and then wring out the plants. Add a further set of herbs: ribwort, bishopwort [betony], yarrow, and attorlothe [a plant used against poison, possibly viper's bugloss]; put these into the same butter, boil again, and wring out thoroughly. This is a good wound salve.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuckoo sour (cuckoo-sorrel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'cuccusure'; named for its sour taste and association with the cuckoo's season.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild cunila
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Satureja hortensis or related Lamiaceae savory
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny XX.63 reference given in footnote; may refer to a wild thyme or savory; exact Anglo-Saxon equivalent uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Cockle
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly identified as corn cockle in Anglo-Saxon herbal contexts; used here in a wound drink, which is notable given its toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: Carline thistle
entities_mentioned.notes: Both lower and upper parts specified in the second recipe; a well-attested wound herb in medieval European medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Ashthroat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Galium aparine (cleavers) or an unidentified plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'æscþrote'; literal meaning is 'ash-throat'; identification remains disputed in scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: Ribwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Very common wound herb in Anglo-Saxon medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Appletree rind
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malus sylvestris or cultivated Malus species (bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound salve preparation as tannin source.

entities_mentioned.name: Myrtle rind
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrica gale (bog myrtle) or Myrtus communis
entities_mentioned.notes: In Anglo-Saxon contexts 'myrtle' more likely refers to Myrica gale than Mediterranean Myrtus.

entities_mentioned.name: Elm rind
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ulmus glabra or Ulmus procera (elm bark)

entities_mentioned.name: Withy rind
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Salix viminalis or related willow species (bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Willow bark; rich in salicylates.

entities_mentioned.name: Young oak rind
entities_mentioned.notes: High tannin content; standard wound astringent.

entities_mentioned.name: Gladdon (geolodwyrt)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iris foetidissima (stinking iris) or Iris pseudacorus
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'geolodwyrt' or 'glædene'; typically identified as a wild iris species.

entities_mentioned.name: Brown wort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Prunella vulgaris (selfheal) or Galeopsis species
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'brune wyrt'; identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Lusmore (foxglove twigs)
entities_mentioned.notes: Irish/Anglo-Saxon 'lusmore' is often associated with foxglove; use in a wound salve rather than internal remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: Hare's spreckle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Pulmonaria officinalis (lungwort) or an unidentified spotted-leaf plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'haran sprǣcel'; 'spreckle' refers to spots/markings on the plant; exact identification unclear.

entities_mentioned.name: Bishopwort
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most frequently cited wound herbs in Anglo-Saxon medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Yarrow
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-attested vulnerary herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Attorlothe
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Echium vulgare (viper's bugloss) or Centaurea species
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon compound meaning 'poison-loather' or 'antidote'; used against venom and wounds; exact botanical identity debated.

entities_mentioned.name: Cow dung and cow stale (urine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the base medium for the salve, worked like soap; a common ingredient in Anglo-Saxon wound treatments.

entities_mentioned.name: Chalk-stone
entities_mentioned.notes: Heated and powdered, sifted into the salve; likely as a desiccant or alkalizing agent.

results.translator_notes: The source text combines a printed English translation (Cockayne's edition of the Leech Book) with the original Old English (Anglo-Saxon) manuscript text in a parallel layout. The segment covers two wound drink recipes and a complex wound salve recipe. The Old English contains several OCR artifacts (e.g., 'f' for 's', runic-influenced characters) that are unambiguous in context and have been silently normalized. The footnote reference to Pliny XX.63 relates to 'wild cunila.' The phrase 'sick caffcer' in the printed text is an OCR error for 'sick [patient]' — the Old English reads 'þam seocam' (to the sick one). The instruction to boil with 'four or two logs' (OE '.i. b.' = a manuscript abbreviation likely for 'feower oþþe twa') refers to regulating the heat of the fire. The salve preparation is elaborate and unique, combining animal waste, multiple tree barks, and a chalk-stone finishing step, suggesting a complex tannin-rich astringent compound.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Erysipelas and Erysipelatous Swellings (Leech Book I, Chapter 2 continued)
results.original_text:
2. Take leaves of green marche, rub or bruise them with the lees of vinegar, smear with that the sore places. For erysipelas which hath broken into blains, take sour curds, work them to a chalder, and foment with the chalder. For erysipelatous inflammations again, take dregs of beer, and soap, and the white of an egg, and old groats, lay this on against erysipelatous swellings. Against bursting of erysipelatous inflammations, let the man sit in cold water till the sore becometh numbed, then get him up, then strike four scarifying slashes about the pocks on the outside, and let the lymph run as it will. Work thyself a salve thus: take brown wort, and marsh gall, or marsh gentian, and red nettle, boil in butter, and smear and bathe with the same worts.

results.translation:
2. Take leaves of green marche [marsh celery / smallage], rub or bruise them with the lees [dregs] of vinegar, and smear the sore places with that. For erysipelas which has broken out into blains [blisters/pustules], take sour curds, work them into a whey [chalder], and foment [apply warm] with the whey. For erysipelatous inflammations again, take dregs of beer, and soap, and the white of an egg, and old groats [crushed grain], lay this on against erysipelatous swellings. Against the bursting of erysipelatous inflammations, let the man sit in cold water until the sore limb becomes numbed; then raise him up, then make four scarifying slashes around the pocks [pustules] on the outside, and let the lymph [fluid] run as it will. Prepare yourself a salve thus: take brownwort, and marsh gall [marsh gentian], and red nettle, boil in butter, and smear and bathe with the same worts.

For the same condition [erysipelas/swelling], take agrimony, rub it well, add vinegar to it, bind it on, and smear with it. For the same, take lupin, grind to dust, mix with honey, and smear with it. For the same, take scrambled [beaten] eggs, mix with oil, lay on, and bathe well with beet leaves. Also, take calf's dung, or that of an old ox, warm, and lay on. Again for the same, take shavings of a hart's [stag's] horn, scraped from the skin with pumice, wet with vinegar, and smear with it. Also, take boar's gall; if you have none, take other swine's [pig's gall], rub, and smear with it where it is sore. For the same, take a swallow's nest, break it all apart with the eggs, and burn it together with the dung, grind to dust, mix with vinegar, and smear with it. For the same, heat cold water with a hot iron, and foment frequently with it. For hot tumours [Latin: 'latum omum', i.e., hot swellings]: take betony, and wormwood, and fennel, rub in ale, and give him radish to drink. For hot tumours, for him who has swellings, and the small burdocks, boil in goat's milk and whey. For hot tumours, take…

entities_mentioned.name: marche (green marche)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens (smallage / wild celery) or Sium latifolium (greater water parsnip / marsh celery)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'merce' commonly refers to smallage or marsh celery. Used rubbed with vinegar dregs and applied topically.

entities_mentioned.name: lees of vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle/excipient for topical application.

entities_mentioned.name: sour curds / chalder
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Chalder' is interpreted as a whey or liquid preparation from curds, used as a fomentation.

entities_mentioned.name: dregs of beer
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as part of a poultice with soap, egg white, and groats.

entities_mentioned.name: soap
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of topical poultice for erysipelatous swellings.

entities_mentioned.name: white of an egg
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a binding/emollient in the poultice.

entities_mentioned.name: old groats
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avena sativa or similar cereal, coarsely ground (old/stale hulled grain)
entities_mentioned.notes: Stale groats used as a poultice base.

entities_mentioned.name: brownwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Prunella vulgaris (self-heal) or Scrophularia nodosa (figwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'brune wyrt'; identity uncertain. Boiled in butter for salve.

entities_mentioned.name: marsh gall / marsh gentian
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gentiana pneumonanthe (marsh gentian) or Menyanthes trifoliata (bogbean)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'mearsc neap geallan'; the text equates it with marsh gentian. Boiled in butter.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Cockayne's edition of the Old English 'Leech Book' (Bald's Leechbook), Book I. The primary English translation is Cockayne's own; the Old English source text (fol. 38a–38b) is provided in parallel. The term 'chalder' appears to denote a liquid preparation or whey derived from curds; its exact meaning is contextually inferred. 'Latum omum' in the Latin gloss likely represents a corrupt or abbreviated Latin phrase for hot swellings or tumours ('calor' / 'tumor calidus'); the footnote cites Plinius Valerianus as a source for a portion of the text (fol. 76d, eight lines). Several plant names in the Old English are partially illegible due to OCR degradation of the manuscript script (particularly 'anjolcp^eccean' for agrimony). The scarification procedure described (four slashes around pustules to release lymph after cold-water numbing) is a notable surgical/procedural entry. The segment is not a continuous single remedy but a series of discrete recipes for erysipelas and related hot inflammatory conditions.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Erysipelas and Related Hot Humours (Leechbook I, Ch. 39)
results.original_text: 3. For the same, take an earthworm, rub it thoroughly fine, add vinegar to it, bind it on and smear therewith. For the same, take savine, rub to dust, and mingle with honey and smear therewith. For the same, take roasted eggs, mingle with oil, lay on, and foment freely with leaves of beet. Again, take a calf's sharn, that is clung, or an old bullock's, still warm, and lay it on. Again for this same, take hart's shavings, shaven off the fell or skin with pumice, and wash, that is macerate, with vinegar and smear therewith. Again, take a boar's gall, if thou have not that, take gall of another swine, rub and smear with that where it is sore. For that ilk, take a swallow's nest, break it away altogether, and burn it with its dung and all, and rub it to dust, mingle with vinegar and smear therewith. For the same, heat cold water with a hot iron, and bathe frequently with that. For hot erysipelatous humours, take betony, and wormwood, and fennel, rub them into ale, and radish with them, give the mixture to the sick man to drink. For hot erysipelatous humours, take fen ompre, that is water dock, and the small clote, that is cleavers, boil in goat's milk and sup. Against hot erysipelatous humours, [continues in Old English:] take innate and ephelebra [feverfew] and alexanders and betony and celandine and cress, use in wine. Boil the blossoms of elder and the cluster in butter and smear therewith; if it will fester, smear with egg yolk, smear over again with that, and dry to the weals until it be hard, then wash away and smear again with the salve. For hot erysipelas, take wine's barm [yeast], mingle with raw resin and strike on with a feather, and do not wash before it be healed. For cold erysipelas, take kneeholen [butcher's broom], long before other food, sometimes for the sore; and a bull's gall, honey, foot [perhaps: juice], put together, treat therewith. For that same which is a pitch-black [condition], take smoke-dried [or clustered] bistort for bathing, and burn to a salve wolf's cheek [jawbone], the wine-soaked and the teeth ground separately, mingle with honey and smear therewith, and lay on pressed cheese; mingle the other with milk, sup three mornings, nine sups. For cancor [cancer/canker], that is oman [erysipelas/inflammation], take nineteen shreds of elecampane and nine antlers and eleven red sedges, put in ale and drink greatly before thou eat; and the elecampane [take as directed].
results.translation:
3. For the same [erysipelas]: take an earthworm, rub it thoroughly fine, add vinegar to it, bind it on and smear therewith. For the same, take savine [Juniperus sabina], rub to dust, and mingle with honey and smear therewith. For the same, take roasted eggs, mingle with oil, lay on, and foment freely with leaves of beet. Again, take a calf's dung [sharn = dung], that is fresh-dropped, or an old bullock's, still warm, and lay it on. Again for this same, take scrapings from a hart [male deer], shaved from the skin with pumice, and macerate with vinegar and smear therewith. Again, take a boar's gall [bile]; if thou have not that, take gall of another swine, rub and smear with that where it is sore. For that same, take a swallow's nest, break it away altogether, and burn it with its dung and all, rub it to dust, mingle with vinegar and smear therewith. For the same, heat cold water with a hot iron and bathe frequently with that.

For hot erysipelatous humours: take betony, wormwood, and fennel, rub them into ale, and [add] radish with them; give the mixture to the sick man to drink. For hot erysipelatous humours: take fen ompre [water dock] and the small clote [cleavers], boil in goat's milk and drink as a sup.

Against hot erysipelatous humours [from the Old English]: take elecampane and feverfew and alexanders and betony and celandine and cress; use in wine. Boil the blossoms of elder and the flower-cluster in butter and smear therewith. If it will fester, smear with egg yolk; smear over again, and dry it to the welts until it be hard; then wash away and smear again with the salve.

For hot erysipelas: take wine's barm [yeast], mingle with raw resin, strike on with a feather, and do not wash before it be healed.

For cold erysipelas: take butcher's broom, well before other food, sometimes applied to the sore; and bull's gall [and] honey; put together and treat therewith. For that same, which presents black [and malignant]: take smoke-dried bistort for a bath, and burn wolf's jawbone to a salve — the wine-soaked [portions] and the teeth ground separately — mingle with honey and smear therewith; and lay on pressed cheese; mingle the other with milk and sup three mornings, nine sups.

For cancor [canker/ulcer], that is erysipelatous inflammation: take nineteen shreds of elecampane root and nine antler [shavings] and eleven red sedges, put in ale and drink copiously before eating; and [use] the elecampane [as directed].

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Cockayne's edition of the Old English Leechbook (Læce Boc), Book I, Chapter 39. The text blends an English translation of the Latin-derived recipes with the original Old English. 'Sharn' is a period term for dung. 'Clung' likely means fresh-dropped or clinging. 'Oman' in Old English denotes erysipelas or a related inflammatory condition. 'Fen ompre' and 'small clote' are identified within the text itself. 'Ephelebra' is uncertain — possibly feverfew or another plant beginning with that sound; rendered tentatively. The footnote by Bjorn Haldorson citing the Icelandic 'A'mumadkr' (earthworm) connects the Old English 'Ome' to Scandinavian folk medicine. The Old English passage beginning 'Imnan...' is translated interpretively from the diplomatic text; some readings are uncertain due to OCR artifacts in the Old English. 'Cancor' (canker) and 'oman' appear to be used near-synonymously in this context for severe inflammatory or ulcerative skin conditions. The instruction to apply wolf's jawbone burned to salve is a notable zootherapeutic element.

results.title_inferred: Drinks and leechdoms against poison, adder-bite, and snake-bite
results.original_text: 1. Drinks or potions and leechdoms against poison. Pound in ale betony, marche, wormwood, fennel, radish; administer this to drink. Against poison; put in holy water betony and the small atterlothe, drink the water and eat the worts. Against any poison; eat ere the danger cometh radish and clote; no man may then do thee a mischief with poison. Against any poison; boil the netherward part of bishopwort and lupin, and the netherward part of springwort, everthroat, and clote in ale; give to drink frequently. If an adder strike a man, or for whatever of that which cometh of shots, wash the black snail in holy water, give to the sick to drink. Again, rub waybroad thoroughly fine, drink it in wine. For bite of snake, put so much of betony as may weigh three pennies into three bowls full of wine, give it the man to drink.
results.translation: 1. Drinks [potions] and leechdoms [medicinal remedies] against poison. Pound betony, marche [smallage/wild celery], wormwood, fennel, and radish in ale; administer this as a drink. Against poison: place betony and the small atterlothe [a plant identified as lesser burdock or possibly black hellebore] in holy water, drink the water and eat the plants. Against any poison: eat radish and clote [burdock] before the danger arrives; no man may then harm you with poison. Against any poison: boil the lower part of bishopwort [bishop's wort/wood betony] and lupin, and the lower part of springwort [dwarf spurge or caper spurge], everthroat [possibly vervain or a related plant], and clote [burdock] in ale; give to drink frequently. If an adder bites a man, or for whatever ailment arising from [elf-]shots, wash the black snail in holy water and give to the sick person to drink. Again, grind waybroad [plantain, Plantago major] thoroughly fine and drink it in wine. For the bite of a snake: put as much betony as weighs three pennies into three bowls full of wine and give it to the man to drink.

entities_mentioned.name: betony
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Betonica officinalis (syn. Stachys betonica), wood betony
entities_mentioned.notes: Used repeatedly throughout this passage as a general antidote to poison and snake/adder bite.

entities_mentioned.name: marche
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens or Smyrnium olusatrum (smallage or alexanders)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'mearce'; commonly identified as smallage or wild celery in Anglo-Saxon herbals.

entities_mentioned.name: radish
entities_mentioned.notes: Also recommended prophylactically eaten before anticipated poisoning.

entities_mentioned.name: atterlothe (small)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Chrysanthemum vulgare, black hellebore, or lesser burdock; identification disputed
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'attorlaðe' (lit. 'poison-hater'); identity much debated in scholarship, proposed candidates include betony itself, Chrysanthemum, or Centaurea species.

entities_mentioned.name: clote
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'clate'; appears twice in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: bishopwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stachys betonica (wood betony) or Aegopodium podagraria (ground elder)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'biscopwyrt'; most commonly identified as wood betony, though some editors assign it to ground elder.

entities_mentioned.name: springwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euphorbia lathyris or Euphorbia peplus (caper spurge / dwarf spurge)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'springwyrt'; identification uncertain, caper spurge is a common guess.

entities_mentioned.name: everthroat
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'efenheorte' or similar; identification remains uncertain in scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: black snail
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arion ater or similar black land slug/snail species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a folk remedy washed in holy water for adder-bite and 'elf-shots'; zootherapeutic use.

entities_mentioned.name: waybroad
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'wegbrade'; one of the nine sacred herbs of Anglo-Saxon medicine.

results.translator_notes: This passage is from Cockayne's 'Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England' (Vol. 2), preserving Old English vernacular medical texts. The word 'leechdoms' means medicinal remedies or prescriptions. 'Shots' likely refers to 'elf-shots' (Old English 'ylfa gescot'), a common Anglo-Saxon folk explanation for sudden internal pains or illness attributed to invisible projectiles shot by elves or hostile spirits. The original title text contains a clear OCR artifact ('potio7is' for 'potions') which has been silently corrected. The measurement 'three pennies' weight is a period apothecary weight reference. Holy water appears as both a vehicle and an active component in two remedies, reflecting the intertwining of Christian and pre-Christian healing traditions in Anglo-Saxon medicine.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Swallowed Poison and Snake Bite, with Latin Charm and Charm against Flying Venom
results.original_text:
3. In case a man swallow poison, take then horehound, work up a mickle deal of it, and adderwort, pound them together and wring the juice, pour thereon three measures of wine and give this to the poisoned man to drink. For hurt from snake; take waybroad, and agrimony, and adderwort, administer them rubbed up in wine to be drunk; and work up a salve of the same worts, and then take agrimony, form a ring around the incision on the outside so it overflows not further, and bind the worts again over the wound. For adder's venom, put of thine ear-wax [lit. 'that which thou cheerest'] and smear with it around, and sing thrice the prayer of St. John and this charm.

[Latin charm:] From the beginning: Father, and Son, and Holy Spirit. The Assumption of St. John, to whom all things are subject. To whom all creation renders service and all powers are subject, and the snake and the dragon flee, and the viper and the toad which is called 'rana quieta' stops, and the scorpion shrinks, and the phalangius [spider] and the asp shrink, and the basilisk is tamed, and the cave-dweller [spelaeus] works no harm, and all venomous and yet more dangerous creeping things and noxious animals flee before thee, and all adversaries to human health are driven away. Thou, Lord, extinguish this venomous poison, extinguish its deadly workings, and empty the power which it has within itself, and give before thy sight to all whom thou hast created eyes that they may see, ears that they may hear, a heart that they may understand thy greatness. And when he had said this, he armoured himself wholly with the sign of the cross and drank all that was in the cup. By the sign of the Holy Cross, and by thee, Christ Jesus, and God the most high Father, one Saviour of the world, in the unity of the Holy Spirit, through all ages of ages, Amen.

[Old English charm against flying venom and every contagious eruption:] On a Friday, take butter that has been melted from the milk of one cow or hind, and let it not be mixed with water; sing over it nine times the Litany, and nine times the Paternoster, and nine times this charm: Acre, aercrae, siernem, nabre, aercuna hel, siernem, nithsern, aer, afan, buirme, adcrice, siernem, meodre, siernem, gegenu, siernem, allu, honor, ucus, idar, adcert, cunolaju, raticamo, helse, icar xrita, haele, tobaerc, teia, rueh, cui, rodaer, plana, mil — and dab on.
results.translation:
3. In case a man swallow poison, take then horehound, work up a great amount of it, and adderwort [bistort], pound them together and wring out the juice, pour thereon three measures of wine and give this to the poisoned man to drink. For hurt from a snake: take plantain [waybroad], and agrimony, and adderwort, administer them rubbed up in wine to be drunk; and work up a salve of the same plants, and then take agrimony, form a ring around the incision on the outside so that it does not overflow further, and bind the plants again over the wound. For adder's venom, apply ear-wax and smear it around the wound, and sing thrice the prayer of St. John and this charm.

[Latin charm (prayer in the tradition of the legend of St. John the Apostle):] In the name of the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Spirit. The Assumption of St. John the Apostle, to whom all things are subject, to whom all creation renders service, and all powers are subject, and the snake and the dragon flee, and the viper and the toad which is called 'rana quieta' [the quiet frog] is stilled, and the scorpion shrinks back, and the phalangius [a venomous spider] and the asp draw back, and the basilisk is tamed, and the cave-spider [spelaeus] works no harm, and all venomous and still more dangerous creeping things and noxious animals flee before thee, and all adversaries of human health are driven away. Thou, Lord, extinguish this venomous poison, extinguish its deadly workings, and drain away the powers it holds within itself, and grant before thy sight to all whom thou hast created: eyes that they may see, ears that they may hear, and a heart that they may understand thy greatness. And when he had said this, he armoured himself entirely with the sign of the cross and drank all that was in the chalice. By the sign of the Holy Cross, and by thee, Christ Jesus, and God the most high Father, one Saviour of the world, in the unity of the Holy Spirit, through all ages of ages, Amen.

[Old English charm against flying venom and every contagious pustule:] On a Friday, take butter that has been melted from the milk of one cow or hind, and let it not be mixed with water; sing over it nine times the Litany, and nine times the Lord's Prayer, and nine times this charm [a sequence of magico-ritual words of uncertain meaning, possibly corrupt Latin, Celtic, or glossolalic origin]: Acre, aercrae, siernem, nabre, aercuna hel, siernem, nithsern, aer, afan, buirme, adcrice, siernem, meodre, siernem, gegenu, siernem, allu, honor, ucus, idar, adcert, cunolaju, raticamo, helse, icar xrita, haele, tobaerc, teia, rueh, cui, rodaer, plana, mil — and then apply.

entities_mentioned.name: horehound
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as primary ingredient for remedy against swallowed poison; juice extracted and mixed with wine.

entities_mentioned.name: adderwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in both the poison remedy and the snake-bite remedy; the Old English name reflects its association with snakes.

entities_mentioned.name: waybroad
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Old English term for plantain; used in snake-bite remedy, rubbed up in wine.

entities_mentioned.name: agrimony
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in snake-bite remedy both internally (in wine) and as a topical ring-dressing around the wound incision.

entities_mentioned.name: ear-wax
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied topically around an adder-bite wound; a folk remedy ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: butter (from single cow or hind)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Animal butter/fat, specifically from one lactating cow or female deer
entities_mentioned.notes: Base for salve in the charm against flying venom; ritual purity specified (unmixed with water, single source animal).

entities_mentioned.name: phalangius
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a venomous spider, perhaps Latrodectus or Loxosceles species, or a general term for dangerous arthropods
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin term appearing in the charm; classical sources use it for a venomous spider.

entities_mentioned.name: basilisk (basiliscus / regulus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named in the Latin charm as a creature that is tamed by the power of St. John.

entities_mentioned.name: spelaeus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a cave spider or cave-dwelling venomous creature; identity uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in the Latin charm; the term means 'cave-dweller.'

entities_mentioned.name: rana quieta (the quiet frog / toad)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a toad species; likely Bufo bufo or similar, believed venomous
entities_mentioned.notes: Named in the Latin charm as a creature that is stilled by holy power.

results.translator_notes: This segment comes from the Old English Leech Book (Bald's Leechbook or Leechbook III), as edited by Cockayne (Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft, vol. 2). The passage is trilingual: Old English for the herbal remedies and the flying-venom charm, and Latin for the St. John charm. The Latin charm is based on a legend of St. John the Apostle drinking poison unharmed; it circulated widely as an anti-venom prayer in medieval medicine. The Old English charm words (Acre, aercrae, etc.) are largely opaque — scholars have proposed corrupt Latin, Old Irish/Celtic, or purely glossolalic origins; they are preserved untranslated here as ritual incantation. The manuscript folio reference (fol. 43a) and the textual footnotes (noting MS variants such as 'reventje,' 'tenebancur,' 'abierre Salutis') are from Cockayne's edition and are preserved in the original_text for scholarly context. OCR artifacts in the Old English passage (e.g., special characters representing thorn and wynn) have been silently normalized. The segment is cut off at the end ('so it overflows not further') due to source truncation, but context is supplied from the English header line.

results.title_inferred: Remedies against accidental ingestion of a creeping thing and against being 'withheld' or 'restrained' by worts
results.original_text: 6. If one drink a creeping thing in water, let him cut into a sheep instantly, let him drink the sheep's blood hot. If a man be 'restrained' with worts, give him springwort for him to eat, and let him sup up holy water. In case that a man be 'withheld'; if he hath on him Scottish wax, and the small atterlothe; or let him drink it in boiled ale, he may not be 'restrained' by worts.
results.translation: 6. If someone accidentally swallows a creeping creature [insect, worm, or similar small animal] in water, let him immediately slaughter a sheep and drink the sheep's blood while it is still hot. If a man is 'restrained' [bewitched or physically bound/obstructed] by herbs [worts], give him springwort to eat, and let him drink holy water. In the case that a man is 'withheld' [similarly afflicted or magically bound]: if he carries upon him Scottish wax and the small atterlothe [a plant], or alternatively let him drink it in boiled ale — he cannot then be 'restrained' by herbs [worts].

entities_mentioned.name: creeping thing
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: generic small invertebrate — insect, worm, or similar creature
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English medical texts frequently describe accidental ingestion of small creatures in drinking water as a medical emergency requiring immediate treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: sheep's blood
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an emetic or antidotal drink, consumed hot immediately after accidental ingestion of a creature.

entities_mentioned.name: worts
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: generic term for herbs or plants
entities_mentioned.notes: In Anglo-Saxon medicine, 'worts' (wyrt) is a broad term for plants/herbs; here used in context of magical 'restraint' or bewitchment by means of herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: springwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euphorbia lathyris (caper spurge) or possibly Bryonia dioica (white bryony); identification disputed
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'springwyrt' is difficult to identify with certainty; it appears in several Old English herbals as a powerful purgative plant with magical protective properties.

entities_mentioned.name: holy water
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: consecrated water (ritual/religious substance, not a botanical)
entities_mentioned.notes: Holy water appears frequently in Anglo-Saxon leechdoms as a vehicle or adjunct to herbal remedies, reflecting the blending of Christian ritual with folk medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Scottish wax
entities_mentioned.notes: The precise nature of 'Scottish wax' (Old English 'Scottisc weax') is unclear; it may refer to a specific prepared wax or resin product associated with Scotland or the Picts, possibly used as an amulet or topical application.

entities_mentioned.name: small atterlothe
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: possibly Betonica officinalis (betony) or a species of Solanum; highly uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Atterlothe' (also 'atterlathe') appears in Old English herbals and is associated with counteracting poison or magical harm; the 'small' variety suggests a size-based distinction between two related plants. Cockayne and later scholars have debated the identification without consensus.

entities_mentioned.name: boiled ale
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: heated fermented malt beverage used as a medicinal vehicle
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled or warm ale is a very common preparation medium in Anglo-Saxon leechdoms for dissolving and administering herbal remedies.

results.translator_notes: The terms 'restrained' and 'withheld' (placed in quotation marks in the source) are technical Old English concepts — likely 'gebunden' (bound) — referring to magical or supernatural affliction caused by malevolent use of herbs or witchcraft (wyrtgaldor), not purely physical symptoms. The segment is from Cockayne's 'Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England,' Vol. 2, and the chapter number xlvi follows. OCR rendered 'hot' as 'liot' in the original; this has been silently corrected to 'hot' as the intended word is unambiguous in context.

results.title_inferred: Remedies and Drinks for Fever and Worms, with Charms and Plasters
results.original_text:
2. A drink; administer in ale cinquefoil, that is five leaved grass, or 'potentilla, to drink for thirty nights. A drink for that; rub down into ale or into wine seed of radish and of colewort, let the man drink that long and frequently against ons worm, till that his case be bettered. A plaster for the same: pound to dust a red tile or brick, mingle with groats, bake a cake, lay it on the wound; work another plaster if need be.

[Old English (Lacnunga / Leechbook) text:]
Læcebomas wið þeorablum · reycnub · seþwan þrinh · elm þrub · cwicþrub · fio micle forþig uerlse moþopeard · ferniod · Lindhioloþe · beforaba þa winda ealle utan · þecnua swiþe wið tofomne · bo ealra empela op geot und hlutore ealof wex scandan þone drenc nihterne on wate sær mon hine drincan wille · drince on morgenne scenc fulne þisses drences · to middes merigenef scande east weard þ bebeode hine gode þeowlice · þine gerenige cypre hine sunngonges ymb þæfter þam drences gonge wiþþan · þ stande sume hwile ær he hine reste geoc swa micel on swa he þær of bo · drince þirne drenc nion niht þ þicge swilcne mete swa he wille.
Brenc wiþ þeorable · sund cincrian ymb help sing þriwa pater noster · brad up þonne þu cweþe set · libera nos a malo · genim þære wið fifsiðba þ seofon pipor corna gecnua togadere · þ þonne þu þ wyrce sing xii. siþum þone psalm · miserere mei deus · þ gloria In excelsis deo · þ pater noster · ofgeoc þonne mid wine þonne dæg þ niht sceadde · drince þonne þone drenc þ bebreoþ þe weorþme. Genim þonne hind hiolopan ane eþgeot mid wetere drince oþre morgen scenc fulne þonne oþre siðe seofon siðeba þ nijon piporcorn · þriddan siðe nijon sniðeba þ xi. piporcorn. Drince wiþþan swiþne drenc þe wille up þrinan þ of bine · þet þonne blod under ancleof.
results.translation:
Remedies for fever [þeorablum]: [the following herbs are compounded]: [various named plants — see entities], all the herbs bruised well together; take equal quantities of all, pour over with clear ale; let the drink stand overnight in a vessel where a man wishes to drink it. Let him drink in the morning a cup full of this drink. At midday let him stand facing east and commend himself devoutly to God, and let him walk around [sunwise] after drinking; and let him stand for some while before he rests. Let him receive as much [food] as he takes away from that; let him drink this drink for nine nights and eat such food as he will.

A drink for fever: go around [the patient] singing the Paternoster three times; raise [the cup] when you say 'set · libera nos a malo' [deliver us from evil]; take five seeds [of the herb] and seven grains of pepper, pound together; when you work this, sing twelve times the psalm 'Miserere mei Deus' [Have mercy upon me, O God] and 'Gloria in Excelsis Deo' and 'Pater Noster'; then pour over with wine at the division of day and night [i.e., at twilight]; then let him drink the drink and warm himself. Then take one [portion of] hind-heolope [black hellebore or a related plant], cook with water; let him drink the next morning a cup full; then a second time seven [seeds] and nine grains of pepper; a third time nine [seeds] and eleven grains of pepper. Drink afterwards a strong drink [of his choosing] to draw [the evil] upward and out; then let blood below the ankle.

[Also, from the earlier passage:]
Administer in ale cinquefoil — that is, five-leaved grass, or potentilla — to drink for thirty nights. A drink for that [ailment]: rub down into ale or into wine seed of radish and of colewort; let the man drink that frequently and for a long time against intestinal worm [ons worm], until his case is bettered. A plaster for the same: pound to dust a red tile or brick, mingle with groats [crushed grain], bake a cake [of it], lay it on the wound; work another plaster if need be.

entities_mentioned.name: Cinquefoil / five-leaved grass / potentilla
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly glossed in the source text as 'five leaved grass' and 'potentilla'. Administered in ale for thirty nights.

entities_mentioned.name: Radish seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Rubbed into ale or wine; used against intestinal worms.

entities_mentioned.name: Colewort seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with radish seed in ale or wine against intestinal worms.

entities_mentioned.name: Red tile / brick
entities_mentioned.notes: Pounded to dust, mixed with groats, baked into a cake for use as a topical plaster on a wound.

entities_mentioned.name: Hind-heolope / hind hiolopan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Helleborus niger (black hellebore) or Mercurialis perennis; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: A plant used in the drink against fever; cooked in water and drunk over successive mornings with increasing doses of pepper. Old English 'hindheolope' is debated in scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: Pepper (pipor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in escalating quantities (7, 9, 11 grains) combined with seeds in the fever remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: Elm (elm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed among the compound herbs for the fever remedy in the Old English text.

entities_mentioned.name: Fernio / ferniod
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Filipendula ulmaria (meadowsweet) or a fern species; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Named in the compound fever remedy in the Old English text.

entities_mentioned.name: Lindhioloþe / Lindhiologe
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Convolvulus or a related climbing plant; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Named in the compound fever remedy in the Old English text.

results.translator_notes: This segment combines two distinct textual layers: (1) Cockayne's editorial English paraphrase/translation of remedies (cinquefoil drink, radish-colewort drink, brick plaster) and (2) the underlying Old English text (Leechbook / Lacnunga), which includes a more elaborate fever compound remedy involving liturgical charms (Paternoster, Miserere mei Deus, Gloria in Excelsis), directional ritual (standing east, walking sunwise), and phlebotomy (bleeding below the ankle). The Old English text contains several OCR corruptions and some apparent scribal omissions noted by Cockayne himself (footnote: 'Some words are here, it seems, omitted'). The term 'ons worm' in the English paraphrase likely reflects 'intestinal worm' or a specific parasitic condition. 'Þeorablum' is glossed as 'fever' following standard scholarly interpretation. Escalating pepper doses across three mornings suggest a progressive dosing regimen. The ritual elements (psalms, Paternoster, cardinal orientation) are characteristic of Anglo-Saxon magico-medical practice. Footnote reference to Pliny lib. xxviii.5 concerns driving away scorpions with an herb ('duo'), likely cinquefoil.

results.title_inferred: Leechdoms for 'Dry' Diseases: Compound Herbal Drinks with Ritual Observances
results.original_text:
1. Leechdoms for 'dry' diseases; ash rind, aspen rind, elm rind, quickbeam rind, the netherward part of the mickle highway nettle, wormwood, hindheal, that is, water agrimony, empurple all the rinds on the outside, and pound them thoroughly, boil them together, apply equal quantities of all, souse them with clear ale, then let the drink stand for the space of a night in a vessel, before a man shall choose to drink it. Let him in the morning drink a cup full of this drink; in the middle of the morning hours, let him stand towards the east, let him address himself to God earnestly, and let him sign himself with the sign of the cross, let him also turn himself about as the sun goeth from east to south and west; after the drink let him next go and stand some while ere he repose himself; let him pour as much liquid into the vessel as he removes from it: let him drink this potion for nine nights and eat what meat he will. A drink for the 'dry' disease; delve about sour ompre, that is, sorrel dock, sing thrice the Pater noster, jerk it up, then while thou sayest sed libera nos a malo, take five slices of it and seven pepper corns, bray them together, and while thou be working it, sing twelve times the psalm Miserere mei, deus, and Gloria in excelsis deo, and the Pater noster, then pour the stuff all over with wine, when day and night divide, then drink the dose and wrap thyself up warm. Then take hindheal alone, souse it with water, drink the next morning a cup full, then the next time seven slices and nine pepper corns, the third time nine slices and eleven pepper corns; afterwards drink a strong potion which will run up and adown; then let blood below the ancle.

[Old English (Lacnunga/Leechbook)]: bpenc þis þeopable mnie healfpubu 7 bulenþfan þa linalan · hninon þync · habiþpexan moþopeapb · þealpynt moþopeapbe þecnua þonne ealle topomne pypce him to bjience bo on pyliþt ealo · oþþe on beop hat þtanban nihterne · bpince þonne þpilce nijon mopjenaþ · nime þy teoþan mopjne þæp bpincef tpa bleba pulle · bepylle on ane 7 þa pyjita þien mib aþeol þuph cþaþ apete tip þæpе hio eoþþan hpiman ne mæje oþ þæt hit mon bpincan mæje · þonne þu hit jebjuincen hæbbe beþpeoh [fol. 45a]
results.translation:
1. Leechdoms [remedies] for 'dry' diseases [likely a category of wasting or febrile illness]: [take] ash rind, aspen rind, elm rind, quickbeam [rowan] rind, the lower part of the great wayside nettle, wormwood, hindheal — that is, water agrimony — purple [bruise/char] all the rinds on the outside, and pound them thoroughly, boil them together, apply equal quantities of all, steep them in clear ale, then let the drink stand overnight in a vessel before a man shall drink it. Let him drink a cupful in the morning; in the middle of the morning hours, let him stand towards the east, address himself to God earnestly, sign himself with the sign of the cross, and turn himself about as the sun goes from east to south and west; after the drink, let him stand a while before he lies down; let him pour as much liquid back into the vessel as he takes out. Let him drink this potion for nine nights and eat whatever food he wishes.

A drink for the 'dry' disease: dig around sour ompre — that is, sorrel dock — sing the Pater noster thrice, pull it up while saying 'sed libera nos a malo' [but deliver us from evil]; take five slices of it and seven pepper corns, pound them together, and while working them, sing twelve times the psalm Miserere mei Deus [Have mercy on me, O God], and Gloria in excelsis Deo, and the Pater noster; then pour wine over the whole preparation at the moment when day and night divide [i.e., at twilight or midnight]; then drink the dose and wrap yourself up warmly. Then take hindheal alone, steep it in water, drink a cupful the next morning; the next time [take] seven slices and nine pepper corns; the third time nine slices and eleven pepper corns; afterwards drink a strong potion that will work both upward and downward [i.e., an emetic and purgative]; then let blood below the ankle.

[Old English continuation, fol. 45a]: [Directions continue, indicating the preparation of a similar compound drink with related herbs — including what appear to be related bark medicines — boiled together and made into a drink with light ale or beer, left to stand overnight; the patient is to drink it for nine mornings; on the tenth morning take two full cups of the drink, boil it down to one, and strain the herbs through a cloth; set it to stand until it cools enough to drink; when you have drunk it, [continue treatment — text breaks off at fol. 45a].]

entities_mentioned.name: Ash rind
entities_mentioned.notes: Bark of ash tree used in compound decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: Aspen rind
entities_mentioned.notes: Bark of aspen used in compound decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: Elm rind
entities_mentioned.notes: Elm bark used in compound decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: Quickbeam rind
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'cwicbeam'; rowan bark used in compound decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: mickle highway nettle (netherward part)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urtica dioica (stinging nettle), root or lower stem
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Mickle highway nettle' likely refers to the large stinging nettle found by roadsides; the 'netherward part' suggests root or lower stem/root portion.

entities_mentioned.name: Wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Old English 'wermod'; bitter herb with well-attested medicinal use.

entities_mentioned.name: Hindheal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tanacetum parthenium (feverfew) or possibly Tanacetum vulgare (tansy); text equates it with water agrimony
entities_mentioned.notes: The text itself glosses 'hindheal' as 'water agrimony' (Bidens tripartita or Eupatorium cannabinum). Old English 'hindheoloþe' is disputed; the text's own gloss suggests water agrimony (Bidens/Eupatorium) in this context.

entities_mentioned.name: Water agrimony (gloss for hindheal)
entities_mentioned.notes: The manuscript explicitly glosses hindheal as water agrimony.

entities_mentioned.name: Sour ompre / sorrel dock
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rumex acetosa (common sorrel) or Rumex acetosella (sheep's sorrel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'surompre'; the text itself glosses it as sorrel dock. Used in the second preparation, taken with pepper.

entities_mentioned.name: Pepper corns
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in escalating doses (7, 9, 11 corns) combined with sorrel dock in the second remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: Wine
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the menstrum [solvent/vehicle] for the second preparation, added at twilight/midnight.

entities_mentioned.name: Clear ale
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the menstrum for the first compound bark decoction.

results.translator_notes:
1. The term 'dry disease' (Old English 'drȳ'-related illness or possibly 'drige adle') likely refers to a category of wasting, febrile, or dessicating illness in Anglo-Saxon medicine, possibly encompassing what modern medicine might call fevers, consumption, or similar systemic diseases. The precise nosological meaning remains debated among scholars.
2. The ritual elements (Pater noster, Miserere, Gloria, eastward orientation, sun-following rotation, sign of the cross) are characteristic of the Christianized magical-medical blend found throughout the Leechbook and Lacnunga; they are translated faithfully here.
3. 'Empurple all the rinds on the outside' likely means to scorch or char the exterior of the bark, possibly to aid extraction of active compounds or as part of a ritual preparation.
4. 'A strong potion which will run up and adown' is glossed in the original footnote as a purgative and emetic — this interpretation is retained.
5. The Old English continuation at the bottom of the segment (fol. 45a) is partially legible but the OCR is poor; the translation of that section is an approximate rendering based on context and parallel Leechbook passages, marked accordingly.
6. 'Hindheal' is a disputed plant identification; the manuscript's own gloss ('water agrimony') has been followed, though other scholars identify hindheal as feverfew or tansy.
7. The nine-night/nine-morning regimen is a recurring feature of Old English leechdoms, likely carrying both practical and ritual significance.
8. 'Let blood below the ancle' refers to therapeutic phlebotomy at the ankle veins, a standard humoral medicine practice.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for the 'Dry' Disease: drinks, salves, and topical treatments
results.original_text:
2. A drink against the "dry" disease; take liekl [some] balm and the small bulentse, thunderwort, the nether part of Calaminth, the nether leaf of woodwax, the netherward part of wallwort, nepeta. Then pound all together, work it for him (the patient) for a drink, put it into foreign ale or beer, let it stand for the space of a night, then let him drink such drink for nine mornings, take on the tenth morning two cups full of the drink, boil them both in one, and let the worts be therewith, strain through a cloth, set it up where it may not touch the earth, till that a man may drink it; when thou have drunken it, wrap thee up warm; lie on the side to which the pain is incident, if it be in the inwards, then this drink will drive it out. A salve against the "dry" disease; take garlic and great wort, wormwood, a plant of nettle, pound small, and along with it harts grease, that it may be such as dough is, place it then on a linen cloth, then warm both the body and the salve at the fire; when thou wilt smear the body or the spot where the disease may be, follow up the patient with this salve and with this drink. A drink for the "dry" disease; dry wormwood, radish, wallwort [Sambucus ebulus], of all these equal quantities, put into ale, rub the herbs down well, the man should have the liquid stand at first for three nights before he drink it, and subsequently let him drink it for about seven nights, let him let blood under the ancle, let him drink the drink straight on for fourteen nights; let him next let blood under the other ancle. Drink the dose for thirty nights in all, a good cup full at nine A.M. or when thou wilt go to bed. For a "dry" worm in the foot; take the red nettle, pound it, add water to it, lay it on a hot stone, make it froth, bind it on the foot for the space of a night. Again, a salve; pound oats, lay on. For the "dry" rot in the foot, triturate wallwort into sweetened wine, and mastic and pepper; let him drink that.

[Old English text:] One herb for nine ailments: take wallwort and clufsung and kneehollow and egolaspe and camomile and lungwort, nine leaves of bishopwort, and attorlathe and red nettle, and red rose and wormwood and yarrow, and horehound and bolemart, and brewer's ale; do all these worts into wylisc [Welsh/foreign] ale and drink then nine days and let blood. Against severe swelling, work for a drink: alexanders, fennel, wormwood, two of kneehollow.
results.translation:
2. A drink against the 'dry' disease [a wasting or drying condition, possibly dropsy, arthritis, or a rheumatic ailment]: take liekl [balm, possibly lemon balm, Melissa officinalis] and the small bulentse [identity uncertain], thunderwort [Sempervivum tectorum, houseleek], the lower part of calamint [Calamintha spp.], the lower leaf of woodwax [Genista tinctoria, dyer's greenweed], the lower part of wallwort [Sambucus ebulus, dwarf elder], and nepeta [catmint, Nepeta cataria]. Pound all together, prepare it as a drink for the patient, put it into foreign [imported] ale or beer, let it stand overnight, then let him drink such drink for nine mornings. On the tenth morning take two cups full of the drink, boil them together in one vessel, with the plant material included, strain through a cloth, set it somewhere it does not touch the earth until it is cool enough to drink. When you have drunk it, wrap yourself up warmly; lie on the side on which the pain is present. If the disease is in the internal organs, this drink will drive it out.

A salve against the 'dry' disease: take garlic and great wort [possibly burdock or plantain], wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], and a plant of nettle [Urtica spp.]; pound small, and mix with it hart's grease [rendered fat of a male deer], until it is like dough. Spread it on a linen cloth, then warm both the body and the salve at the fire. When you wish to apply it to the body or to the spot where the disease is, treat the patient with this salve together with the drink described above.

Another drink for the 'dry' disease: dry wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], radish [Raphanus sativus], wallwort [Sambucus ebulus] — equal quantities of all these — put into ale and rub the herbs down well. The liquid should stand for three nights before he drinks it; then let him drink it for about seven nights. Let him let blood from beneath the ankle, and drink the drink continuously for fourteen nights; then let him next let blood from beneath the other ankle. Drink the full dose for thirty nights in total, a good cupful at nine in the morning or when going to bed.

For a 'dry' worm [parasitic lesion or ulcer] in the foot: take red nettle [Urtica dioica or Lamium purpureum], pound it, add water, lay it on a hot stone, make it froth, bind it on the foot overnight.

Again, a salve [for the same]: pound oats, apply.

For 'dry' rot in the foot: triturate wallwort [Sambucus ebulus] into sweetened wine with mastic and pepper; let him drink that.

[From the Old English:] One herb for nine ailments: take wallwort and clufsung [clove-like herb, possibly clove-wort or similar], kneehollow [Ruscus aculeatus, butcher's broom], egolaspe [identity uncertain], camomile [Anthemis/Chamaemelum nobile], lungwort [Pulmonaria officinalis or Sticta pulmonaria], nine leaves of bishopwort [Stachys betonica, betony], attorlathe [Plantago spp. or Conyza, a 'poison-loather'], red nettle, red rose [Rosa spp.], wormwood, yarrow [Achillea millefolium], horehound [Marrubium vulgare], bolemart [identity uncertain], and brewer's ale. Put all these worts into wylisc [Welsh or foreign] ale, drink for nine days, and let blood.

Against severe swelling, prepare a drink: alexanders [Smyrnium olusatrum], fennel [Foeniculum vulgare], wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], two [parts] of kneehollow [Ruscus aculeatus].

entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'liekl' is unclear; the footnote gloss 'balm' suggests Melissa officinalis.

entities_mentioned.name: bulentse (small)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity uncertain; possibly a dialectal or scribal variant of a known herb.

entities_mentioned.name: thunderwort

entities_mentioned.name: woodwax

entities_mentioned.name: wallwort

entities_mentioned.name: great wort
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly Arctium lappa (burdock) or Plantago major (greater plantain); insufficient context.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears multiple times throughout the remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: nettle
entities_mentioned.notes: Both 'nettle' and 'red nettle' mentioned; red nettle may be Lamium purpureum (red dead-nettle) or simply a reddish variety of Urtica.

entities_mentioned.name: hart's grease
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rendered fat of Cervus elaphus (red deer)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base/excipient for the salve.

entities_mentioned.name: oats
entities_mentioned.notes: Pounded and applied topically.

entities_mentioned.name: clufsung
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English term; possibly related to clove or a bulbous herb. Identity uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: kneehollow / cneowholen
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'cneowholen' is commonly identified with Ruscus aculeatus.

entities_mentioned.name: egolaspe
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity uncertain; possibly a scribal corruption.

entities_mentioned.name: bishopwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'biscopwyrt' is standardly identified as betony.

entities_mentioned.name: attorlathe
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'attorlathe' means 'poison-loather'; exact botanical identity debated.

entities_mentioned.name: yarrow
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'gearwe'.

entities_mentioned.name: bolemart
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity uncertain; possibly a scribal form of a known herb.

entities_mentioned.name: alexanders
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'alexandre'; a common medieval pot-herb and medicinal plant.

results.translator_notes: The 'dry' disease (Old English 'drȳge adl' or similar) is a recurring diagnostic category in Anglo-Saxon medicine whose precise modern equivalent is uncertain; it may encompass wasting diseases, dropsy, rheumatism, or similar chronic conditions. The instruction to 'not touch the earth' when setting the prepared drink is a ritual/magical precaution common in Lacnunga and Leechbook texts. The bloodletting instructions (under the ankle) reflect humoral medical practice. Footnote marginalia in the original ('Sedum tectorum', 'Sambucus ebulus') appear to be editorial identifications by Cockayne. The Old English passage at the end of the segment (beginning 'Oxa liejibe...') is from the Leechbook and has been transcribed with OCR errors; the translation reflects a best-effort reconstruction. 'Wylisc ealo' (Welsh ale or foreign ale) is a specific type of ale referenced in several Old English medical texts. The term 'bolemart' and 'egolaspe' remain unidentified and may be corrupt forms.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for intestinal worms and the small (hair) worm
results.original_text:
2. For worms again; mingle and rub up with honey a hard and very dry goats tord, administer it to be drunk, that will drive them away. Against worms which ail a man within, again; seethe in water radish to the third part, mingle with wine, give to drink. Again for that; put goats gall on wool, lay and bind it on the navel. For that ilk; mingle with honey, mint well triturated, work it into a little bolus, make him swallow it. Again, give for three days to drink oil and of vinegar an equal quantity. Again, everthroat, marehe, betony, nepeta, githcorn; boil them in wine. For worms which are troublesome within; give to drink a wort drink of contre and of parsnip. A salve; let him eat celandine; let him boil brownwort in inspissated wine, then add thereto ship tar and sulfur; smear therewith.

xlix.
For the small worm; the forepart of a twig of withe wind, and the tallow dock, not the red one, and this coarse salt beaten together very small and little butter.
results.translation:
2. For worms again: mix and rub up with honey a hard and very dry goat's dung [tord = dung/excrement], administer it to be drunk; that will drive them away. Against worms which afflict a man internally, again: boil radish in water down to one-third, mix with wine, give to drink. Again for that: put goat's gall [bile] on wool, lay and bind it on the navel. For that same ailment: mix with honey mint well ground [triturated], work it into a small bolus [pill], make the patient swallow it. Again, give for three days to drink equal quantities of oil and vinegar. Again: everthroat [a plant, possibly cleavers/Galium aparine], marehe [marsh plant, possibly water-parsley or similar], betony, nepeta [catnip/catmint], githcorn [corn cockle or similar]; boil them in wine. For worms which are troublesome within: give to drink an infusion [wort drink] of contre [identity uncertain] and of parsnip. A salve: let the patient eat celandine; let him boil brownwort in thickened [inspissated] wine, then add thereto ship's tar and sulfur; smear therewith.

Chapter xlix.
For the small worm [hair worm]: the tip [forepart] of a twig of withe wind [bindweed], and tallow dock [possibly Rumex obtusifolius, the broad-leaved dock, not the red dock], and coarse salt beaten together very fine, and a little butter.

results.translator_notes: Several plant names remain uncertain: 'everthroat,' 'marehe,' and 'contre' are obscure Anglo-Saxon or possibly corrupt terms. 'Tord' is an Anglo-Saxon/Old English word for dung or excrement. The marginal annotation 'Apa lustris' adjacent to the xlix chapter likely refers to Rumex palustris (marsh dock), suggesting the editor or copyist attempted a Latin identification of 'tallow dock.' The footnote marker for 'Hair worm' in the original indicates the editor's identification of the small worm as a hair worm (Gordius/Nematomorpha). 'Inspissated wine' means wine boiled down to a thick consistency. 'Wort drink' is a standard Anglo-Saxon term for an herbal infusion. The OCR rendering 'contre' is likely a corruption of a plant name (possibly 'centaury' = Centaurium erythraea, or 'coriander'); marked uncertain. 'Tallow dock' is an unusual compound not in standard botanical references and may refer to the pale/yellow-flowered dock rather than red sorrel.
