results.title_inferred: Remedies for Hand-Worms, Dew-Worms, Flesh-Worms, and Internal Worms (Leech Book I, chapters 50–52)
results.original_text:
1. For hand worms and dew worms; take dock or clotes, such as would swim, mingle the roots with cream and with salt, let it stand for three nights, and on the fourth day smear therewith the sore places.

[Old English, fol. 47a–47b:]
Wið pyrum hond ete: genim mersc meær geallan ⁊ reade netlan ⁊ reade boccan ⁊ smele clifan; wyrc on cu buteran þonne fio sealf gelbben sie, wipþum; nim þonne sealtes þrys men læad on hweor coldum e, ⁊ inne und; wyrc mid laþan ymb niht smire mid. Wið bearwyrme læppe on þar col cele und wætre læppe on swa hat swa he hardost mæge. Wið bearwyrme: sume nimaþ wearm cwead monnes þynne dribað neahterne on; sume swines lungen wearme. Wið hond wyrme: nim scirsceare ⁊ swefl ⁊ pipor ⁊ hwit sealt; meng tosomne smire und. Weax sealf wið wyrme: weax sealf; butere pipor hwit sealt; meng tosomne smire und.

.LI.
Wið wyrmum þe mannes flæsc etaþ: genim geallan þone wagan; cnua on niþe ealo ær þon hit asripen wæ; fol. 47b. wele þ ofer pyllo drincan þreo niht. Eft genim grundswelgan þe on eorþan weaxeþ ⁊ sceapes smeru meng tosomne gelice fela lege on. Eft genim beren ear besenj lege on swa hat ⁊ hat wætres laþa on. Wið flæsc wyrmum genim monnes snian þa leap gerel togedre gebrisid on þærfe gecuna þonne lege on swa þu hatost mæge aþresnan.

.LII.
Wið lusum acriud ⁊ hwon wermod gecnua on ealu wele drincan. Wið lusum cwic seolfor ⁊ eald butere an penig seolfres ⁊ tu penig wæge butran meng on aþrot eal tosomne.
results.translation:
**Chapter 50 (remedy 1). For hand-worms and dew-worms [skin parasites or eruptions]:**
Take dock or burdock [clotes], such as would float [i.e., fresh/light roots], mingle the roots with cream and with salt; let it stand for three nights, and on the fourth day smear the sore places therewith.

**For hand-worms that eat [i.e., cause itching/eruption on] the hand:**
Take marsh-mallow gall [or: marsh-mallow bile], red nettle, red dock, and small cleavers; work [these] into cow-butter; when the salve is prepared, add [pepper?]; then take three men's measure of salt in cold water [i.e., a cold brine], and apply inwardly [?]; make [the salve] with lye; smear on at night. For a bear-worm [intestinal parasite]: apply cold to cool it, and pour on water as hot as the patient can bear. For a bear-worm: some take warm human excrement and drip it on overnight; some [use] warm swine's lung. For hand-worm: take scissors-herb [scirsceare, possibly germander or a cutting herb], sulphur, pepper, and white salt; mingle together and smear on the wound. Wax salve against worms: wax salve — butter, pepper, white salt; mingle together and smear on.

**Chapter 51. Against worms that eat a man's flesh [flesh-worms]:**
Take gall of the wagon [? — possibly ox-gall or a plant identifier]; pound it into weak ale before it has fermented; give that mixture to drink for three nights over a pillow [i.e., at bedtime]. Again: take groundsel that grows in the earth and sheep's fat; mingle together in equal parts and lay on. Again: take a barley ear, immerse and lay on as hot as possible, and pour on hot water. For flesh-worms: take a man's [urine? — text uncertain], the leaves gathered together, bruised in need; pound them, then lay on as hot as you can endure.

**Chapter 52. Against lice:**
Bruise/pound [acrimony? — acriud] and a little wormwood into ale; give to drink. Against lice: [take] quicksilver and old butter — one penny-weight of silver and two pennies' weight of butter; mix together into a complete [paste] and rub on thoroughly.

entities_mentioned.name: dock (bocce / boccan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Red dock (reade boccan) specifically mentioned; used for skin complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: clotes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arctium lappa (burdock) or Nymphaea spp. (water lily / floating plants)
entities_mentioned.notes: The qualifier 'such as would swim' suggests aquatic or buoyant plant; 'clotes' is sometimes identified as burdock, other times as water-lily.

entities_mentioned.name: marsh-mallow (mersc mear geallan)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Geallan' could mean gall or a part of the plant; identification tentative.

entities_mentioned.name: red nettle (reade netlan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lamium purpureum (red dead-nettle) or Urtica dioica (stinging nettle, red form)

entities_mentioned.name: cleavers (clifan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Small cleavers (smele clifan) specified.

entities_mentioned.name: sulphur (swefl)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical antiparasitic.

entities_mentioned.name: groundsel (grundswelgan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used mixed with sheep's fat as a topical remedy for flesh-worms.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood (wermod)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in ale as an anti-louse treatment taken internally.

entities_mentioned.name: quicksilver (cwic seolfor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Topical application against lice; classic use of mercury as antiparasitic in medieval medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: scirsceare
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Teucrium scordium (water germander) or unidentified cutting herb
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'scissors-herb' or 'shire-herb'; identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: acriud
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Agrimonia eupatoria (agrimony) or another bitter herb; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: OCR/manuscript reading uncertain; footnote in source queries 'þonflacrii?'.

entities_mentioned.name: bear-worm (bearwyrm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely an intestinal or subcutaneous parasite; possibly tapeworm segment or botfly larva
entities_mentioned.notes: The introductory editorial note equates 'taenia' (tapeworm) with keiron; 'bearwyrm' may refer to a different parasite.

entities_mentioned.name: hand-worm (hond wyrm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly scabies mite infestation, ringworm, or other cutaneous parasitic condition
entities_mentioned.notes: Editorial note suggests possible confusion with tapeworm terminology; likely a skin parasite.

entities_mentioned.name: flesh-worm (flæsc wyrm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly myiasis (fly larva infestation of flesh) or another subcutaneous parasite

results.translator_notes: This segment corresponds to Leech Book I, chapters 50–52 (Cockayne edition, vol. 2). The Old English text is heavily abbreviated and partially corrupted in the manuscript; several readings are uncertain. The editorial footnotes in Cockayne's edition suggest that 'hand worms' may be a scribal reading for 'tape worms' (taenia), and 'dew worms' may relate to leeches or ribbon-like worms. The phrase 'such as would swim' qualifying clotes/dock is puzzling and may indicate aquatic varieties of these plants. Chapter 51's 'geallan þone wagan' is obscure — 'wagan' could be a plant name (waybread/plantain?), a wagon-driver's gall (ox-gall), or a scribal error. The remedy involving warm human excrement applied overnight is paralleled in other Anglo-Saxon leech texts. Quicksilver (mercury) as a topical anti-louse agent in Chapter 52 is consistent with medieval pharmaceutical practice. The source text mixes Latin editorial notes, Old English primary text, and editorial marginalia, which have been separated and identified in this translation.

results.title_inferred: Remedies against worms, flesh worms, and lice (Leechbook sections li–lvi)
results.original_text:
2. If a worm eat the hand; take marsh marigold and red nettle, and red dock, and the small bur, boil in cows butter; when the salve is sodden, then further take of salt three parts, shed thereupon, shake together, and smear therewith; lather with soap, about night time smear therewith. Against a dew worm; let the man step upon a hot coal, let him cool the foot with water; let him step upon it as hot as he hottest may. For a dew worm, some take warm thin ordure of man, they bind it on for the space of a night; some take a swines lung warm. Against a hand worm; take ship tar, and sulfur, and pepper, and white salt, mingle them together, smear therewith. A wax salve against a worm; a wax salve; butter, pepper, white salt, mingle them together, smear therewith.

li.
Against worms which eat a mans flesh; pound into new ale, before it be strained, the party coloured ram gall, give the running over to drink for three nights. Again, take groundsel which waxeth on the earth, and sheeps grease, mingle them together, alike much in quantity, lay on. Again, take an ear of beer or barley, singe it, lay it on so hot, and hot water, leave it on. Against flesh worms; take mans sorrel, boil the leaves together, spread them out on the grass, then pound them, lay them on, as thou hottest may endure them.

lii.
Against lice; pound in ale oak rind and a little wormwood, give to the lousy one to drink. Against lice; quicksilver and old butter; one pennyweight of quicksilver and two of butter; mingle all together in a brazen vessel.

.LIII.
Vis fmejca [Against burning/itching]: eat fresh cheese and bread of fine rye. If a mans head bone burn, to allay it, put thereon with pepper.

.LIIII.
For a worm-eaten body and for cruelty [severe affliction] of worms; take elderberry rind from the north side below the bark, elecampane root, dock root, worm-ash [ash of worms], pepper, lovage, swertia [or gentian], oil and horse grease to boot, and cress juice — of all these equal quantities, and of the barks likewise equal quantities; mingle all cold together so that it may smear well the diseased parts; smear therewith by night and in the morning each.

.LV.
For a diseased [inflamed/erupted] body; broom, cress, yarrow — boil in butter and in lard; smear therewith.

.LVI.
A bath for a diseased body; gather the great fern downward [lower fronds], and [continue as fragment].
results.translation:
2. If a worm [parasitic or festering lesion] should eat the hand: take marsh marigold and red nettle, and red dock, and the small bur [burdock or similar], boil in cow's butter; when the salve is cooked, then additionally take three parts of salt, pour it thereon, shake together, and smear therewith; lather with soap; smear therewith about nighttime. Against a dew worm [ringworm or similar skin condition]: let the man step upon a hot coal, then cool the foot with water; let him step upon it as hot as he can bear. For a dew worm: some take warm thin ordure [excrement] of man and bind it on for the space of a night; some take a swine's lung while still warm. Against a hand worm: take ship's tar, and sulphur, and pepper, and white salt; mingle them together and smear therewith. A wax salve against a worm: a wax salve of butter, pepper, and white salt; mingle them together and smear therewith.

li.
Against worms that eat a man's flesh: pound into new ale, before it is strained, the party-coloured [variegated] ram's gall; give the liquor that runs over to drink for three nights. Again: take groundsel that grows on the earth, and sheep's grease; mingle them together in equal quantity and lay on. Again: take an ear of barley [or beer grain], singe it, lay it on as hot as may be, with hot water, and leave it on. Against flesh worms: take common sorrel ["man's sorrel"]; boil the leaves together, spread them out on the grass, then pound them and lay them on as hot as thou canst endure.

lii.
Against lice: pound oak rind and a little wormwood in ale; give to the lousy person to drink. Against lice: quicksilver and old butter — one pennyweight of quicksilver and two of butter; mingle all together in a brazen vessel.

liii.
For a burning [or itching condition of the skin]: eat fresh cheese and bread of rye. If a man's skull bone burns [is inflamed], to allay it, apply pepper thereon.

liv.
For a worm-eaten body and for severely worm-afflicted [cruelty of worms] lesions: take elderberry rind from the north side below the tree, elecampane root, dock root, ash of worms [calcined preparation], pepper, lovage, gentian [swertia], oil and horse grease in addition, and cress juice — of all these in equal quantities, and likewise equal quantities of the barks; mingle all cold together so that it may smear well all the diseased parts; smear therewith at night and each morning.

lv.
For a diseased [inflamed or erupted] body: broom, watercress, yarrow — boil in butter and in lard; smear therewith.

lvi.
A bath for a diseased body: gather great fern [bracken] from the lower growth, and [text continues as fragment — remainder not fully legible in this segment].

entities_mentioned.notes: Identified in footnote as Menyanthes trifoliata (bogbean); however the Old English term is more commonly Caltha palustris. Footnote attribution to Menyanthes may reflect the editor's interpretation.

entities_mentioned.name: Menyanthes trifoliata (footnote)
entities_mentioned.notes: Editor's footnote identification for 'marsh marigold' in original; may be conflation.

entities_mentioned.name: red nettle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lamium purpureum (red dead-nettle) or Urtica dioica

entities_mentioned.name: small bur
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity uncertain; possibly agrimony or small burdock.

entities_mentioned.name: dew worm
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'dew worm' may refer to a skin lesion thought to be caused by dew or nocturnal exposure; hot coal treatment suggests a burning/cautery approach.

entities_mentioned.name: ship tar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as topical antiseptic/parasiticide.

entities_mentioned.name: sulphur
entities_mentioned.notes: Classic antiparasitic ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: ram gall (party-coloured)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingested via ale decoction; 'party-coloured' may indicate a specific ritual or quality criterion for the animal.

entities_mentioned.name: oak rind
entities_mentioned.notes: Used against lice in ale decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: quicksilver
entities_mentioned.notes: Used topically against lice; mercury was a classical pediculicide.

entities_mentioned.name: elderberry rind (north side)
entities_mentioned.notes: Specification of north-side bark suggests a magical/ritual gathering convention common in Anglo-Saxon medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: worm-ash (wyrm-asce)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ash/calcined preparation, possibly of actual worms or wormwood ash
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'syrmacmehipes buft' is difficult; may refer to ash prepared from worms or a worm-specific plant material.

entities_mentioned.name: swertia/gentian (spertles buft)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identification uncertain from the Old English; possibly felwort (Gentianella amarella).

entities_mentioned.name: great fern (micle fearn)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a medicinal bath for a diseased body.

results.translator_notes: This segment draws from the Old English Leechbook (Bald's Leechbook or related compilations), with sections numbered both in Roman numerals (li, lii) and Old English chapter headings (LIII–LVI). The term 'worm' in Anglo-Saxon medicine broadly covers parasitic infections, festering wounds, skin lesions attributed to worm-like entities (possibly ringworm, maggot infestation, or subcutaneous parasites). 'Dew worm' likely refers to erysipelas or a related cutaneous eruption. The Old English sections (LIIII–LVI) are partially transcribed from the manuscript and have been translated as faithfully as the damaged/abbreviated text allows; some passages remain conjectural. The footnote in the source text identifies 'marsh marigold' as Menyanthes trifoliata, which is the editor Cockayne's gloss and may not be accurate for all instances of the term. The specification of elderberry bark from the 'north side' is a typical Anglo-Saxon magical-medical convention. Mercury ('quicksilver') as a pediculicide reflects Graeco-Roman influence on Anglo-Saxon pharmacy. Section LVI is fragmentary in this segment.

results.title_inferred: Salves for a Stricken Body, Fig Disease, and Wound Treatments (Leechbook I, Chapters 2, 57–58)
results.original_text:
2. For a stricken body, a salve; helenium thoroughly sodden, and the netherward part of hammersedge, and old lard, pound all together, warm through a cloth at the fire, smear therewith; then scarify continually about the bruise for seven nights, set a horn upon the open scarifications, smear with the black salve, be it for a night, be it for two, as need be, and as they be open.

LVII.
For the disease called fig, a drink and a salve; let him boil worm wort in milk and drink it. A salve; pound glass to dust, add a drop of honey, leech the wound therewith.

LVIII.
[For wound-salve and wound swellings: work it from the lower part of nettle, and from hemlock, and from the cloven wound-wort, and from the small moorwort; boil all four in butter and in sheep's grease until enough; pound again the same plants into the salve, and sheep's tallow, and garlic, and crow-leek, and leek, and salt; mix well, put on a cloth, warm greatly at the fire, smear therewith.

Wound-salve: outer bark of crab-apple, nettle cropped, two wound-worts, elder bark, waybread, southernwood, bishopwort, lower part of bolt, grease-wort, salt, sheep's tallow, and sheep's grease. For a wound swelling: take crow-leek, radish, elecampane, cloven wound-wort; pound all the plants very well, lay on.

Wound-salve: marrow of a hart, iris tar, and beaten pepper, and sheep's tallow.

[For the black blain, give the man to eat two clusters or three of the plant which is in three ways called mixen-plant.] (marginal addition, later hand)]
results.translation:
2. For a bruised or injured body, a salve: take helenium [elecampane] thoroughly boiled, and the lower (root) part of hammersedge [sedge/Carex sp.], and old lard; pound all together, warm through a cloth at the fire, and smear therewith. Then scarify [scratch/incise the skin] continually around the bruise for seven nights; set a cupping horn upon the open scarifications, smear with the black salve — do this for a night, or for two, as need requires, and while the scarifications remain open.

Chapter 57.
For the disease called 'fig' [a rectal or anal condition, possibly hemorrhoids or anal fistula], a drink and a salve: let the patient boil worm-wort [artemisia/wormwood] in milk and drink it. For the salve: pound glass to dust, add a drop of honey, and treat the wound therewith.

Chapter 58.
For a wound-salve and wound-swellings: prepare it from the lower part of the nettle, and from hemlock, and from the cloven wound-wort, and from the small moorwort; boil all four together in butter and in sheep's grease until sufficient; then pound again the same plants into the salve, and add sheep's tallow, garlic, crow-leek, leek, and salt; mix well, place on a cloth, warm thoroughly at the fire, and smear therewith.

A wound-salve: [made from] outer bark of crab-apple, cropped nettle, two wound-worts, elder bark, waybread [plantain], southernwood, bishopwort, the lower part of bolt [burdock?], grease-wort, salt, sheep's tallow, and sheep's grease. For a wound-swelling: take crow-leek, radish, elecampane, and cloven wound-wort; pound all the plants very well, and lay on [the wound].

A wound-salve: marrow of a hart [stag], iris [extract], and beaten pepper, and sheep's tallow.

[For the black blain [pustule/carbuncle], give the man two or three clusters of the plant which is called in three ways 'mixen-plant' [possibly Chenopodium/goosefoot or Mercurialis] to eat.] (This last entry is a marginal addition in a different, later hand.)

entities_mentioned.name: helenium / eolone
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in salve for a bruised or injured body; boiled and combined with lard and hammersedge.

entities_mentioned.name: hammersedge (netherward part)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carex acuta or related sedge species
entities_mentioned.notes: The root/lower portion of a sedge plant; used in salve for bodily injury.

entities_mentioned.name: old lard
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base/excipient in the wound salve.

entities_mentioned.name: worm wort
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in milk as a drink for 'fig' disease (possibly anal/rectal condition or hemorrhoids).

entities_mentioned.name: glass (pounded to dust)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used with honey as a topical salve for 'fig' disease; an abrasive or escharotic application.

entities_mentioned.name: honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed with ground glass as a wound salve.

entities_mentioned.name: nettle (lower part)
entities_mentioned.notes: Root portion used in wound-salve preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: hemlock
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingredient in wound-salve; topical use distinct from ingestion.

entities_mentioned.name: cloven wound-wort (clupihte wenpyrt)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Stachys sylvatica or Achillea millefolium (yarrow)
entities_mentioned.notes: Recurrent ingredient in wound-salves throughout the Leechbook.

entities_mentioned.name: small moorwort (smalan morpyrt)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Andromeda polifolia or a small heath plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound-salve compound.

entities_mentioned.name: sheep's grease / tallow
entities_mentioned.notes: Excipient/base used in multiple wound-salve formulations.

entities_mentioned.name: garlic (garleac)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingredient in compound wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: crow-leek (cropleac)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium vineale or wild garlic/crow garlic
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound-salve and as treatment for wound-swelling.

entities_mentioned.name: leek (leac / pecgleac)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingredient in compound wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: crab-apple bark (outer bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: elder bark (ellen gimbe)
entities_mentioned.notes: Bark used in wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: waybread (wegebrade)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: southernwood (sutern)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: bishopwort (bisceopwyrt)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Betonica officinalis (betony) or possibly Stachys betonica
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: bolt lower part (bulot niðeweard)
entities_mentioned.notes: Lower (root) portion used in wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: grease-wort (smerepyrt)
entities_mentioned.notes: Literal meaning 'grease-plant'; identity uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: radish (ontre / onettre)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in treatment of wound-swelling.

entities_mentioned.name: hart's marrow (hioroþes mearg)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base in wound-salve.

entities_mentioned.name: iris tar / iris (irig tearo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly extract of Iris sp. or rendered iris root
entities_mentioned.notes: Unclear preparation; possibly a resinous or fatty extract of iris.

entities_mentioned.name: pepper (pipor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Beaten and added to wound-salve with hart's marrow and tallow.

entities_mentioned.name: mixen-plant (myxenplante)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Chenopodium album (fat-hen/goosefoot) or Mercurialis annua
entities_mentioned.notes: Marginal addition; given internally (eaten) for 'black blain' [carbuncle or pustule]; identified by three names in the Old English tradition.

results.translator_notes: This segment draws from the Old English Leechbook (Læceboc), Book I, attributed to compilation circa 9th–10th century. The cupping horn ('horn') is glossed in the original as a 'cupping horn' by Cockayne. The term 'fig' (OE 'fic') likely refers to a painful anal or rectal condition such as hemorrhoids, anal fistula, or condylomata. The marginal entry on 'mixen-plant' was added in a different and later hand as noted by the editor. The use of powdered glass as a topical abrasive/escharotic in 'fig' treatment is notable. Hemlock as a topical wound ingredient should be flagged as toxic if taken internally. Several plant identifications (wound-wort, moorwort, bolt, grease-wort) remain uncertain in the scholarly literature. The Old English text contains several HTML-encoded characters (e.g., ']>' for 'þ') indicating OCR artefacts from the digitised manuscript; these have been silently corrected in translation.
results._label: cockayne_v2::2.  For  a  stricken  body,  a  salve;  heieniurn

results.title_inferred: Salve for wens and wen boils
results.original_text: 1. For a wen salve and for wen boils; work the salve of the netherward part of nettle and of henbane, and of the wenwort which has cloves or bulbed roots, and of the small moorwort, boil all four in butter and in sheep's grease till there be enough, pound again the same worts in the salve, and ship tar, and garlic, and cropleek, and sedgeleek, and salt, mingle well, put on a cloth, warm thoroughly at the fire, smear therewith.
results.translation: 1. For a salve against wens [cysts/swellings] and wen boils: prepare the salve from the lower part of nettle and of henbane, and of the wenwort [wen-wort] which has cloves or bulbed roots, and of the small moorwort; boil all four in butter and in sheep's fat until sufficient [in quantity]; pound again the same herbs into the salve, and [add] ship's tar, and garlic, and cropleek, and sedgeleek, and salt; mix well, apply to a cloth, warm thoroughly at the fire, and smear therewith.

entities_mentioned.name: nettle (netherward part)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'netherward part' indicates the root or lower stem of the nettle plant.

entities_mentioned.name: hendock / henbane
entities_mentioned.notes: OCR renders 'hendock'; context and Anglo-Saxon herbalism strongly suggest henbane (Hyoscyamus niger), though 'hemlock' (Conium maculatum) is also possible. The original footnote reference is ambiguous.

entities_mentioned.name: wenwort with cloves or bulbed roots
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ranunculus ficaria (lesser celandine / pilewort) or a bulbous species used specifically against wens
entities_mentioned.notes: The marginal note in the source reads 'Probably [illegible]' suggesting the editor was uncertain. The descriptor 'cloves or bulbed roots' may point to a bulbous Ranunculus or similar geophyte.

entities_mentioned.name: small moorwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Andromeda polifolia or a small Erica/Calluna species; alternatively a small marsh plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'moorwort' typically refers to plants of boggy or moorland habitat; exact species uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: butter
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a fat base for the salve.

entities_mentioned.name: sheep's grease
entities_mentioned.notes: Secondary fat base for the salve preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: ship tar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pine tar or Stockholm tar (Pix navalis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in Anglo-Saxon medicine as an antiseptic and topical agent; 'ship tar' distinguishes it from other resinous products.

entities_mentioned.name: cropleek
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ampeloprasum or Allium porrum (leek, possibly wild leek)
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'cropleek' (crop-leek); possibly a cultivated or wild leek variety.

entities_mentioned.name: sedgeleek
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium schoenoprasum (chives) or possibly Allium ursinum (ramsons); source footnote cites Allium schoenoprasum
entities_mentioned.notes: Marginal note in original source cites 'Allium schoenoprasum'.

results.translator_notes: The source text contains several OCR errors: 'salro' corrected to 'salve'; 'hendock' is likely 'henbane' (Hyoscyamus niger) rather than hemlock, though both are plausible in context — the correction is uncertain and flagged. The marginal footnote for wenwort identification is partially illegible in the source ('Probably [illegible]'). The footnote for sedgeleek cites 'Allium schoenoprasum' (rendered in OCR as 'AHium' and partially garbled). The condition treated — 'wen' — refers to a cyst, sebaceous lump, or swelling in Anglo-Saxon medical usage. Preparation method: the herbs are boiled in fat (butter and sheep's grease), then the herbs are pounded back into the resulting salve, tar, alliums, and salt are added, mixed well, spread on a cloth, warmed at the fire, and applied topically.

results.translation:
Chapter LIX.

Against palsy: take a cup full of boiling water, another of oil, and of white salt so much as one may pick up with four fingers; shake together until it be all one. Drink all of this by drops, rest awhile, poke thy finger into the gullet, spew up again all and more if thou may; then in the morning let blood from the arm, or from the neck, as much as he may bear; and scarify, and let him put something on; then after all smear with hot oil and let him taste a trifle of salt. Employ gladden [iris] and everfern [polypody fern] picked high up on the tree, and cover over all the scarifications with soft [nesh] wool when they have been smeared.

Against 'neurisn' [a pain condition, possibly neuralgia or a rheumatic ailment]: put bone-wort [comfrey or pellitory] into sour cream and into honey; mingle together with this the yolk of an egg; smear therewith. Again, pound up earthworms and apply them.

Chapter LX.

For a burn, make a salve: take goat's tallow and wheat straw, burn to dust, mix this dust with butter, put it on a pan over fire, render [melt] very well, strain through a cloth, smear therewith.

For a burn, take the lower part of fennel [or smallage], beat with old lard and lay on. Again, take lily and yarrow, boil in butter, smear therewith. For the same, boil ribwort [plantain] in butter and smear therewith.

For the same, boil yarrow in butter and smear therewith.

For the same, boil cuckoo-flower [lady's smock] in sheep's fat and extract [press out], and put everfern [polypody] in honey or in wax. For the same, apply egg-white frequently.

entities_mentioned.name: Gladden (glædenan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yellow flag iris / Iris pseudacorus or Iris foetidissima
entities_mentioned.notes: Used topically over scarifications in palsy treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Everfern (eorþifearn / eoforfearn)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both in the palsy remedy (topically, with nesh wool) and in the burn remedy (in honey or wax).

entities_mentioned.name: Bone-wort (banwyrt)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in sour cream and honey with egg yolk for 'neurisn', a pain/neuralgia-like condition.

entities_mentioned.name: Earthworms (eorþwyrmas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lumbricus terrestris or related earthworm species
entities_mentioned.notes: Pounded and applied topically for 'neurisn'.

entities_mentioned.name: Goat's tallow (gāt tāleg)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with burnt wheat straw and butter for a burn salve.

entities_mentioned.name: Wheat straw (hwæte healm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum straw, burned to ash/dust
entities_mentioned.notes: Burned to dust and mixed into burn salve.

entities_mentioned.name: Fennel lower part (finugl niþereweardes) or smallage
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foeniculum vulgare (fennel) or possibly Apium graveolens (smallage/wild celery)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Old English 'smylep' or 'fynulep' is ambiguous; beaten with old lard and applied to burns.

entities_mentioned.name: Lily (lilian)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lilium candidum (white lily) or related Lilium species
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled with yarrow in butter for burns.

entities_mentioned.name: Yarrow (gearwan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in butter and applied to burns; also used alone boiled in butter.

entities_mentioned.name: Ribwort / plantain (ribban)
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in butter and smeared on burns.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuckoo-flower (cocuc)
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in sheep's fat and extracted, used for burns.

entities_mentioned.name: Egg white (ægres þæt hwite)
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied frequently to burns.

entities_mentioned.name: White salt (hwit sealt)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the palsy emetic drink and also prescribed to be tasted after bloodletting and scarification.

results.translator_notes: The term 'neurisn' (Old English 'neurisn' or 'nearisn') is left in quasi-transliteration in the facing-page English gloss; it likely refers to a pain condition, possibly neuralgia or a rheumatic/nerve pain syndrome. The Old English source is from the Leech Book (Bald's Leechbook), a 10th-century Anglo-Saxon medical manuscript. OCR distortion is severe throughout the Old English column; the facing-page English translation (already present in the source) has been used to verify and guide interpretation. The instruction to let blood 'from the neck' likely refers to a neck vein such as the jugular. 'Nesh wool' means soft, undyed or unprocessed wool. 'Gladden' used 'high up on the tree' is puzzling for an iris (a low herb); some editors suggest this may refer to mistletoe or polypody growing on trees, or that the description applies to the everfern alone. The chapter break to LX is embedded mid-source and concerns burns exclusively.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for racking pain and synovia of the joints
results.original_text: 1. Against racking pain in the joints, pound lithwort with honey, or chew it and lay it on. Again, burn thoroughly the head bone or skull of a wolf and pound it small, sift it through a cloth, put it on the wound. Against pain in the joints, pound wormwood with tar and fen cress, wring out the juice, mingle together, stick the residue upon the joint where the sore is, bind it on fast. For the synovia of the joints, silver weed, brown wort, and the little harewort, it oftenest waxeth in a garden, it hath white blossoms, pound the three worts, mingle them, that is a good salve. With many men the synovia of the joints oozeth out, wring on the spot the juice of an apple, and shave very small some shavings of horn, crumble them on the wound within it, remove that and ever apply the same anew. For the synovia of the joints, burn lithwort, houndshéad, and pound them up with roasted apple; mingle all that together, apply it. Again, take a sour apple, roast and lay it on; apply groats over above the apple.
results.translation: 1. Against racking pain in the joints: pound lithwort [a plant remedy] with honey, or chew it and lay it on [the affected area]. Again, burn thoroughly the head-bone or skull of a wolf, pound it fine, sift it through a cloth, and apply it to the wound. Against pain in the joints: pound wormwood with tar and fen cress, wring out the juice, mix them together, press the residue upon the joint where the soreness is, and bind it on firmly. For the synovia [synovial fluid / joint fluid] of the joints: [use] silverweed, brownwort, and the little harewort — this last most often grows in a garden and has white blossoms — pound the three herbs, mix them together; that is a good salve. With many men the synovia of the joints oozes out: wring the juice of an apple directly onto the spot, and shave very fine some shavings of horn, crumble them into the wound upon it, remove [the dressing] and always apply the same treatment anew. For the synovia of the joints: burn lithwort [Sambucus, i.e. elder, per marginal note] and houndshead, then pound them together with roasted apple; mix all of that together and apply it. Again, take a sour apple, roast it and lay it on; apply groats [coarsely ground grain] on top of the apple.

entities_mentioned.name: lithwort
entities_mentioned.notes: A marginal annotation in the source text reads 'Sambucus' next to a later mention of lithwort, suggesting elder (Sambucus nigra or related species). The identification is plausible but the Old English term 'lithwort' has also been associated with other plants.

entities_mentioned.name: wolf skull / head bone of a wolf
entities_mentioned.notes: Calcined wolf skull ground into powder and applied topically; a zootherapeutic remedy common in medieval European practice.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely used in Anglo-Saxon and medieval medicine for joint pain and inflammation.

entities_mentioned.name: fen cress
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cardamine sp. or Nasturtium palustre (marsh cress / bog cress)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'fencærse'; likely a wetland cress, possibly Nasturtium palustre or a related Cardamine species.

entities_mentioned.name: silverweed
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard identification for Old English 'gæs-fot' or 'seolferweed'; commonly used in traditional remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: brownwort
entities_mentioned.notes: The Old English 'brunwyrt' is ambiguous; some scholars assign it to Prunella vulgaris, others to a Scrophularia species.

entities_mentioned.name: harewort (little harewort)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Marrubium vulgare (horehound) or possibly Capsella bursa-pastoris (shepherd's purse)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as growing in gardens with white blossoms. The identification is uncertain; 'harewort' has been applied to several plants in Anglo-Saxon sources.

entities_mentioned.name: apple juice
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied topically for joint synovia; also used roasted as a poultice.

entities_mentioned.name: horn shavings
entities_mentioned.notes: Keratin-based animal material, finely shaved and applied to weeping joint wounds; a common wound-dressing ingredient in early medieval medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: houndshead
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Antirrhinum sp. (snapdragon) or Ballota nigra (black horehound)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'hundeshéafod'; the name 'houndshead' has been applied to Antirrhinum (snapdragon) and sometimes to Ballota nigra. Identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: groats
entities_mentioned.notes: Coarsely ground or hulled grain (likely oats or barley), used here as a poultice material applied over roasted apple.

entities_mentioned.name: tar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical base/vehicle mixed with wormwood and fen cress for joint pain.

results.translator_notes: The word 'synovia' appears in the original English translation (Cockayne's rendering of an Old English term) to denote joint fluid or a weeping/oozing condition of the joints, possibly referring to both true synovial fluid leakage and open joint wounds. The marginal annotation 'Sambucus' in the source text ties lithwort to elder in at least one usage. 'iu' in the segment label is an OCR error for 'in'. The superscript footnote markers (numbers) in the source have been silently removed from the cleaned text as they refer to Cockayne's editorial footnotes not reproduced here, except the 'Sambucus' gloss which is preserved in the notes. The segment is drawn from Cockayne's Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England, Vol. 2.
results._label: cockayne_v2::1.  Against  racking  pain  iu  the  joints,  poun

results.title_inferred: Remedies for synovia of the joints and fever (Leech Book I, ch. 62)
results.original_text:
2. For the synovia of the joints, take maythe, mingle it with honey, apply it to the wound and bind it fast. For the secretion of the joints, take oak rind and dry it and work it to a fine flour or smede, and further sloethorn rind, the netherward part of it, sift them through a cloth, and shed that on [the wound].

If the joint ooze outward, take merce [smallage/wild celery] the lower part, and honey and the fine flour of wheaten bread and pigeon fennel, mingle together, lay on. Again take meadowwort the lower part, pound it small, mingle with honey, lay on that it be bathed.

If the joint ooze outward, take vinegar and sour crumbs of barley bread and [pepper?] mingle together, bind on that [cloth] which is with vinegar or with sour ale. If the joint ooze outward, take wormwood and pound it, put it in tar, smear on and bind on the joint.

LXII. Against fever disease: elecampane, horehound, waybroad [plantain], pound in ale, let stand two nights, give to drink. Against fever again, drink betony much and eat three shavings. Again a drink in clear ale: wormwood, horehound, betony, bishopwort, fen mint, bogen, the clove-wort, feverwort [wormwood?], marrowdie; drink thirty days. A drink against that: betony, springwort, wolfsbane [attorlathe], barberry, eorothroat [boar's throat], centaury, dry throat, wormwood. Against third-day fever [tertian fever], drink in warm water betony ten portions before one wishes. Against fourth-day fever [quartan fever], drink waybroad juice in sweet water two hours before the fever is to come to him. Against every day's fever, drink in cold water betony the weight of a penny, another such of waybroad.

Against fever again, horehound taken separately in drink is helpful. Against spring disease [lencten-adle]: wormwood, boar's throat, elecampane [elechtre], waybroad, ribwort, chervil, wolfsbane [attorlathe], pepper-wort, alexanders, bishopwort, hyssop, sage, carsuc, work to [a preparation]...
results.translation:
2. For the synovia [joint fluid / joint swelling] of the joints: take maythe [mayweed/chamomile], mingle it with honey, apply it to the wound and bind it fast.

For the secretion [discharge] of the joints: take oak bark [rind] and dry it and work it to a fine flour or fine meal [smede], and furthermore sloe-thorn bark [rind], the lower/inner part of it; sift them through a cloth and sprinkle that upon [the wound].

If the joint ooze outward [exude]: take smallage [merce / wild celery], the lower part, and honey and the fine flour of wheaten bread and pigeon-fennel [perhaps fennel], mingle together, lay on. Again, take meadowwort, the lower part, pound it small, mingle with honey, lay on so that it be well bathed [with it].

If the joint ooze outward: take vinegar and sour crumbs of barley bread and [possibly pepper or rye], mingle together, bind on [the cloth] soaked with vinegar or with sour ale. If the joint ooze outward: take wormwood and pound it, put it in tar [pitch], smear on and bind on the joint.

Chapter LXII. Against fever illness: elecampane, horehound, plantain [waybroad] — pound in ale, let stand two nights, give to drink. Against fever again: drink betony copiously and eat three shavings [scrapings]. Again, a drink in clear ale: wormwood, horehound, betony, bishopwort [betony-related herb], fen mint, bogen [unidentified], clove-wort, feverwort, marrowdie — drink for thirty days. A drink against that [fever]: betony, springwort, attorlathe [wolf's bane / birthwort], barberry, eorothroat [boar's throat, possibly elecampane variant], centaury, dry-throat [unidentified], wormwood. Against tertian fever [third-day fever]: drink betony in warm water — ten portions — before one expects [the fever] to come. Against quartan fever [fourth-day fever]: drink plantain juice in sweet water two hours before the fever is expected. Against quotidian fever [every-day fever]: drink in cold water betony of the weight of one penny, and another such [weight] of plantain.

Against fever again: horehound taken alone as a drink is helpful. Against the spring sickness [lenten disease, perhaps a seasonal epidemic condition]: wormwood, boar's throat, elecampane, plantain, ribwort, chervil, wolfsbane [attorlathe], pepperwort, alexanders, bishopwort, hyssop, sage, carsuc [cress?] — work into [a preparation, text cuts off]...

entities_mentioned.name: maythe
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anthemis cotula or Matricaria chamomilla (mayweed / chamomile)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly identified as mayweed in Old English medical texts; used here for joint swelling with honey.

entities_mentioned.name: oak rind
entities_mentioned.notes: Dried and powdered for application to weeping joints; astringent properties consistent with tannin content.

entities_mentioned.name: sloethorn rind
entities_mentioned.notes: Inner/lower bark used; also astringent.

entities_mentioned.name: merce (smallage)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'merce'; lower root part used with honey and wheaten flour for joint exudate.

entities_mentioned.name: meadowwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Filipendula ulmaria (meadowsweet) or possibly Spiraea spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'medowyrte'; lower part pounded and mixed with honey.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Used multiple times: in tar for joint binding, in fever drinks, and in spring disease compound.

entities_mentioned.name: elecampane (elehtran / elechtre)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in fever ale-drink and spring disease preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: horehound
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in fever preparations; also noted as helpful individually against fever.

entities_mentioned.name: waybroad (plantain)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in fever drinks including quartan fever juice preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: betony

results.translator_notes: The source text is from Cockayne's edition of the Old English Leech Book (Leechdoms, Wortcunning and Starcraft of Early England, vol. 2), a 10th-century Anglo-Saxon medical compilation. The Old English passages are given in parallel with Cockayne's partial English translation, which this JSON expands and clarifies. 'Synovia' is used in the period sense of joint fluid or joint-related discharge/swelling. 'Smede' is an Old English term for fine-sifted meal or flour. 'Lencten-adle' (spring disease) likely refers to a seasonal febrile illness, possibly epidemic in character. The footnote in the source text queries whether 'Lepidium' is the correct identification for pepperwort, which has been retained as uncertain. The Latin footnote excerpt is from Caelius Aurelianus (c. A.D. 230), Chronic Diseases Book V, cap. 2, describing joint inflammation, hardening, darkening, and twisting of fingers, and the generation of pores from purulent or mucilaginous or viscous collected humor — this passage is editorial context by Cockayne and not part of the remedy text proper. The word 'Innelye' appears as a footnote query regarding the reading of 'pigeon fennel' in the manuscript. Several plant names (bogen, eorothroat, marrowdie) remain unconfidently identified.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Fever: General, Tertian, Quartan, and Quotidian
results.original_text: 1. For fever disease; pound in ale lupins, githrife, waybroad, let it stand for two nights, administer to drink. For fever again; let him drink betony much, and eat three bits of it. Again, drink in clear ale wormwood, githrife, betony, bishopwort, fen mint, rosemary, the clove rooted wenwort, marrubium, drink for thirty days. A drink for that, betony, springwort, attorlothe, vervain, everthroat, houndstongue, dwarf dwosle, wormwood. For a tertian fever, let the sick drink in warm water ten sups of betony, when the fever is approaching. For a quartan fever, let him drink juice of waybroad in sweetened water two hours before the fever will to him. For a quotidian fever, let him drink in cold water so much of the dust of betony as may weigh a penny; as much more of waybroad.
results.translation: 1. For fever disease [febris]: pound lupins, githrife [gyðrife], and waybroad [plantain] in ale; let it stand for two nights, then administer as a drink. For fever again: let the patient drink much betony and eat three morsels of it. Again: drink in clear ale wormwood, githrife, betony, bishopwort [bishop's wort / wood betony or marsh woundwort], fen mint, rosemary, the clove-rooted wenwort [wart-wort], marrubium [white horehound]; drink this for thirty days. Another drink for the same: betony, springwort, attorlothe [viper's bugloss or similar antidotal herb], vervain, everthroat [possibly cleavers or another strangle-wort], houndstongue, dwarf dwosle [dwarf thyme or pennyroyal], wormwood. For a tertian fever [recurring every third day]: let the sick person drink ten sups of betony in warm water when the fever is coming on. For a quartan fever [recurring every fourth day]: let him drink the juice of waybroad in sweetened water two hours before the fever is due to strike. For a quotidian fever [daily recurring fever]: let him drink in cold water as much powdered betony as would weigh a penny-weight, and an equal amount of waybroad.

entities_mentioned.name: lupins
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a bitter, pounded in ale as part of fever remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: githrife (gyðrife)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Agrimonia eupatoria (agrimony) or an Old English term for a related herb; sometimes identified as corn cockle (Agrostemma githago)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'gyðrife' is debated; some scholars identify it with agrimony, others with corn cockle. Context suggests a bitter or tonic herb.

entities_mentioned.name: waybroad
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Old English 'wegbrade'; used both pounded in ale and as expressed juice for quartan fever.

entities_mentioned.name: betony
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears repeatedly across all fever types; used as infusion, powdered (penny-weight dose), and eaten directly.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Bitter tonic; used in the compound ale drink for thirty days and in the attorlothe compound.

entities_mentioned.name: bishopwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Stachys sylvatica (hedge woundwort) or Betonica officinalis; sometimes identified as Alisma plantago-aquatica
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'biscopwyrt' is ambiguous and applied to more than one plant in the corpus.

entities_mentioned.name: fen mint
entities_mentioned.notes: A marsh-growing mint; 'fenminte' in Old English texts.

entities_mentioned.name: clove rooted wenwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Geum urbanum (herb bennet / wood avens), whose root has a clove-like scent; 'wen' suggests use against wens/cysts
entities_mentioned.notes: The clove-rooted epithet strongly suggests Geum urbanum.

entities_mentioned.name: marrubium
entities_mentioned.notes: Retained Latin name in the original text.

entities_mentioned.name: springwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Euphorbia spp. (spurge) or Bryonia dioica (white bryony); 'springwort' in Anglo-Saxon herbals often denotes a purgative root
entities_mentioned.notes: Identification is contested; often associated with plants of purgative or drastic action.

entities_mentioned.name: attorlothe
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cichorium intybus (chicory) or Centaurea nigra (black knapweed); literally 'poison-loather' or antidote herb in Old English
entities_mentioned.notes: The name means 'poison-hater' or 'antidote'; precise botanical identity is unresolved in scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: everthroat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Prunella vulgaris (self-heal) or Galium aparine (cleavers); 'efenthroat' in some manuscripts
entities_mentioned.notes: Identification is unclear; the name may refer to a plant used for throat ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: dwarf dwosle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Thymus serpyllum (wild/dwarf thyme) or Mentha pulegium (pennyroyal); 'dwosle' is an Old English term sometimes linked to thyme
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Dwosle' in Old English herbals is frequently equated with thyme; the 'dwarf' qualifier may indicate a smaller species or variety.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Cockayne's Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England (Vol. 2), preserving Old English vernacular plant names. Several names (githrife, attorlothe, everthroat, dwosle, springwort) remain botanically contested in scholarship. 'Tertian,' 'quartan,' and 'quotidian' fevers reflect classical Galenic fever typology adopted into Anglo-Saxon medical writing, likely derived from Latin intermediaries. The 'penny-weight' (as much as may weigh a penny) is a period dosage measure. The text is already in Early Modern English as rendered by Cockayne; translation has clarified archaic constructions without altering meaning.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Fever and Demonic Possession (Leech Book I, Chapters 62–63)
results.original_text:
2. For fever again it helpeth, to drink marrubium alone. For lent addle, or typhus fever, work to a drink wormwood, everthroat, lupin, waybroad, ribwort, chervil, attorlothe, feverfue, alexanders, bishopwort, lovage.

[Old English and Latin charm text, fol. 51b:]
Boil in foreign ale, add holy water and spring wort; one shall write on a housel-dish and wash the writing off into the drink with holy water and sing over it:
[Latin charm]: In principio erat verbum et verbum erat apud deum et deus erat verbum. Hoc erat in principio apud deum; omnia per ipsum facta sunt. [Then wash the writing with holy water off the dish into the drink.] Then say Credo and Pater Noster and this lay: Beati... [sing over] the cup with the twelve prayer-psalms. 'Adiuro vos, frigora et febres, per deum patrem omnipotentem, et per eius filium Iesum Christum, per ascensum et descensum Salvatoris nostri, ut recedatis de hoc famulo dei et de corpusculo eius, quem dominus noster illuminare instituit. Vincit nos leo de tribu Iuda, radix David. Vincit vos qui vinci non potest. + Christus natus + Christus passus + Christus venturus + Agios + Agios + Agios + Sanctus + Sanctus + Sanctus. In die salutifero incedens squalidus urbes, oppida, rura, vicos, castra, castella peragrans, omnia defulsit sanabat corpora morbis.' [Then rub on the forehead the sign of the cross over other curses gathered for men.]

LXIII. Against a person bewitched by enemies; when the devil seizes the man outright or afflicts him inwardly with disease: [prepare] a spirit-drink [of] elecampane, bishopwort, henbane, cropleac [garlic-leek]; pound to a powder, add ale to moisten; let stand overnight; add fifty libcorns [peppercorns?] and holy water; a drink for a person bewitched by fiends, to drink from a church bell; [also add] yew, [illegible/glaer?], yarrow, elecampane, betony, attorlathe, cassock, fennel... [continues] hage [haw], cassock, in foreign ale; add holy water and springwort.
results.translation:
2. For fever once more it is helpful to drink horehound alone. For sluggish-fever [lent addle] or typhus fever, prepare as a drink: wormwood, everthroat [possibly cleavers or a similar herb], lupin, plantain [waybroad], ribwort plantain, chervil, attorlothe [viper's bugloss or similar], feverfew, alexanders, bishopwort [wood betony or marsh mallow], lovage.

[Rubric, fol. 51b — Head Indic.:] Boil in foreign [imported] ale; add holy water and springwort; one shall write [a charm] on a communion dish [housel-dish] and wash the written characters off into the drink with holy water, and sing over it:

[Latin charm text:] 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and God was the Word. This was in the beginning with God; all things were made through him.' Then wash the writing with holy water from the dish into the drink. Then say the Creed and the Our Father and this lay: [verses from Sedulius, Carmen Paschale, Book III, 23, intertwined with Bede, Ecclesiastical History III.xxviii.] Sing over the cup, accompanied by the twelve prayer-psalms: 'I adjure you, chills and fevers, by God the Almighty Father, and by his son Jesus Christ, by the ascent and descent of our Saviour, that you depart from this servant of God and from his little body, which our Lord has ordained to enlighten. The lion of the tribe of Judah, the root of David, overcomes us. He who cannot be conquered conquers you. + Christ is born + Christ has suffered + Christ is to come + Holy + Holy + Holy + Sacred + Sacred + Sacred. Passing on the day of salvation through squalid cities, towns, countryside, villages, fortresses, and strongholds, he freed all bodies from their diseases.' Then make the sign of the cross on the forehead, surpassing all other spells gathered for men.

Chapter LXIII. Against a person bewitched by evil beings — when the devil seizes the man outright or afflicts him inwardly with disease: [Prepare] a spirit-drink [from] elecampane, bishopwort, henbane, cropleac [a type of garlic]; pound these to a powder, add ale to moisten, let it stand overnight; add fifty libcorns [possibly peppercorns or grains of pepper] and holy water. [This is] a drink for a person afflicted by fiends, to be drunk from a church bell. [Also add:] yew, [glaer — possibly iris/gladden root], yarrow, elecampane, betony, attorlathe, cassock [possibly sedge or a similar plant], fennel... haw [hage], cassock, [prepared] in foreign ale; add holy water and springwort.

results.translator_notes: This segment combines two distinct chapters from the Old English Leech Book (Bald's Leechbook). Chapter 62 (end) gives compound herbal drinks for fever — including simple horehound decoction and a multi-herb compound for typhus ('lent addle'). The remainder of the fol. 51b material is a magico-religious charm incorporating a Latin exorcism formula, a passage from Sedulius's Carmen Paschale (Book III, 23), and elements intertwined with Bede's Ecclesiastical History (III.xxviii), as noted in the footnotes of the original edition. Chapter 63 treats demonic possession with a spirit-drink prepared from herbs and drunk from a church bell — a notable ritual element. 'Lent addle' (Old English: lentenadl) may refer to a slow or chronic fever; 'typhus' in the manuscript footnote is the editor's gloss. The medical practices here blend Galenic herbalism with Christian exorcistic ritual. 'Libcorn' is an unresolved Old English term; Cockayne and others have suggested pepper grains. The footnote suggests 'glaer' may be an error for 'cynaglaer' (dog-iris, Iris pseudacorus). The charm text uses Christian Trinitarian formulae alongside what appear to be quasi-magical graphic signs (crosses and repetition of 'Agios/Sanctus').

results.title_inferred: Exorcism and Emetic Remedies for the Demoniac (Fiend-Sick Man)
results.original_text:
8. A man shall write this upon the sacramental paten, and wash it off into the drink with holy water, and sing over it .... In the beginning, etc. (John i. 1.) Then wash the writing with holy water off the dish into the drink, then sing the Credo, and the Paternoster, and this lay, Beati immaculati, the psalm; with the twelve prayer psalms, I adjure you, etc. And let each of the two men then sip thrice of the water so prepared.

Inde salutiferis incedens gressibus urbes,
Oppida, rura, casas, vicos, castella peragrans
Omnia depulsis sanabat corpora morbis.
SEDULIUS.

LXIII.
For a fiend sick man, or demoniac, when a devil possesses the man or controls him from within with disease; a spew drink, or emetic: lupin, bishop wort, henbane, cropleek; pound these together, add ale for a liquid, let it stand for a night, add fifty libcorns, or cathartic grains, and holy water. A drink for a fiend sick man, to be drunk out of a church bell: githrife, cynoglossum, yarrow, lupin, betony, attorlothe, cassock, flower de luce, fennel, church lichen, lichen, of

[Old English:] cipcijan, crifcef mseles raisan, lupeftice, gepyrc þone brence of hluttrum ealaþ gefinge feofon msepyan ofer þam pyrtum bo gapleac and halig pæter to and brype on ælcne brincan þone brenc þe he brincan pille eft and singe þone sealm bean Inmaculati and exupsat and Saluum me fac deus and þonne drince þone brenc of ciricbellan and se mseppe preoft him singe æfter þam brence þis ofer-domne Sancte pater omnipotens. Piþ bræcfeocum men: cost, gotwode, eluhtpe, betonice, attorlabe, cropleac, holecepfan, hope, pinul; asinge mon msepan ofer pyrce of pylifcum ealoþ and of halig pætepe. Drince þisne brenc þes gehpilcum nipe nijon morgenan and nane oþpe pæran þæt þicce and stille fie, and ælmepsan pelle and him opena god geornlice bidde. Piþ peben heorte: bisceoppyrt, elehcpe, banpyrt, eoporpearn, gifþripe, heahhiologje þonne dæg scade and niht þonne sing þu on ciricean letanias þæt is þara haligra naman, and pater noster mid þy sange þu ga þæt þu sie æt þam pyrtum and þriwa ymbga and þonne þu hie in me gang eft to ciricean mid þy ilcan sange, and gesing .xii. msepan ofer and ofer ealle þa brencas þe to þære adle belimpað on weorþmynde þara twelfa apostola.

LXIV.
Piþ ælcpe ypelpe leodrunan and piþ ælfpri[en]...
results.translation:
8. [An exorcism:] A man shall write this upon the sacramental paten [communion plate], and wash it off into the drink with holy water, and sing over it: 'In the beginning,' etc. (John 1:1). Then wash the writing with holy water off the dish into the drink; then sing the Creed [Credo], and the Lord's Prayer [Paternoster], and this psalm-verse, 'Beati immaculati' [Psalm 119], with the twelve prayer psalms, 'I adjure you,' etc. And let each of the two men [the leech and the sick man] then sip thrice of the water so prepared.

[Latin verse from Sedulius:]
'Thence, proceeding with health-bringing steps through cities,
Travelling through towns, countryside, cottages, villages, and forts,
He healed all bodies, driving away their diseases.'
—SEDULIUS.

LXIII.
For a fiend-sick man [demoniac], when a devil possesses the man or controls him from within with disease: a spew-drink [emetic]: lupin, bishop-wort [bishopweed], henbane, cropleek [a species of leek]; pound these together, add ale as a liquid, let it stand for a night, add fifty libcorns [cathartic grains] and holy water.

A drink for a fiend-sick man, to be drunk out of a church bell: githrife [possibly a type of plant], cynoglossum [hound's tongue], yarrow, lupin, betony, attorlothe [an antidote plant, possibly betony or elecampane], cassock [possibly wild valerian], flower-de-luce [iris], fennel, church lichen, lichen — [text continues in Old English, here translated:]

Of church vessels [cipicijan], of the sign of the cross [crifcef mæles], of lovage [lupestice]; prepare the drink from clear ale; let seven masses be sung over the plants; add garlic [gapleac] and holy water, and drop into each drink — the drink that he wishes to drink — and again sing the psalm 'Beati immaculati,' and 'Exurge' [Arise, O God], and 'Salvum me fac Deus' [Save me, O God]; and then let him drink the drink from the church bell, and let the mass-priest sing over him after the drink this [prayer]: 'Sancte Pater Omnipotens' [Holy Father Almighty].

For a man sick with vomiting [bræcfeocum]: costmary [cost], gotweed [gotwode, possibly ground elder], elecampane [eluhtpe], betony, attorlathe [antidote plant], cropleek, hollowstem [holecepfan, possibly alexanders or hogweed], hops [hope], fennel [pinul]; let a mass be sung over the plants, [prepared] from Welsh ale [pylisc ealaþ] and from holy water. Let him drink this drink for nine mornings anew, and let no other [liquid] be given that is thick and still; and let him give alms and earnestly pray to the merciful God.

For a heavy [troubled] heart: bishop-wort [bisceoppyrt], elecampane, bonewort [banpyrt, possibly Solomon's seal or comfrey], hart's-tongue fern [eoforþearn], githrife, and [a plant illegible, possibly horehound] — when day separates from night; then sing in the church litanies, that is, the names of the holy saints, and the Lord's Prayer; go thus singing to where you are at the plants, and go around them three times, and then as you put them [in water] go again to the church with the same song; and sing twelve masses over all the drinks that pertain to that disease, in honour of the twelve apostles.

LXIV.
Against every evil enchantment [leodrunan, 'song-rune'] and against elf-[ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off]...

results.translator_notes: The segment is a bilingual (Old English and Latin) passage from the 'Leechbook' (Lǣce Bōc), a major Anglo-Saxon medical text (Bald's Leechbook). The exorcism recipe (§8) blends Christian liturgical practice (paten, holy water, Gospel of John, Creed, Paternoster, psalms) with herbal medicine. The Latin verse is from Sedulius (Caelius Sedulius, 5th-century Christian poet), Carmen Paschale. 'Fiend-sick' (OE 'feond-seoc') denotes demonic possession or epilepsy. 'Church bell' as a vessel for drinking the remedy reflects apotropaic use of sacred objects. 'Welsh ale' (pylisc ealaþ) refers to ale brewed in a Celtic/British style. 'Libcorns' are explicitly glossed as 'cathartic grains' in the source and remain debated in scholarship. The two men referenced in the footnote are the physician (leech) and the patient. Some Old English in the original is partially illegible or corrupted by the scribe/OCR; best reconstructions have been provided. LXIV is cut off at the end of the segment.
results._label: cockayne_v2::8.  A    man    shall    write   this   upon    th

results.title_inferred: Charms, runes, and remedies against elf-sickness, hag-riding, and disease in men and cattle
results.original_text:
16.

Caiacus + + + + + • Lucas • + • Iohannes. Intercedite pro me • Tecon • Leleloth • Patron • abissus uos.

Eft gocund gewed • In nomine Domini fit benedictum • byrmonice • beromcen • et habet in vestimento et in femore suo • scriptum Rex regum et Dominus dominantium. Eft gocund gewed. In nomine fit benedictum. M M M R M P • N G • L T X X M R F P N + • P T X.

One shall, writing, first write and put the word writing on the swollen breast and not go therein nor write thereon; and also writing wife on done:
EMMANUEL • BYRNICE • NOSTER ASTER.
results.translation:
16.

[Charm invocation:] Caiacus + + + + + • Lucas • + • Iohannes [i.e., the evangelists Mark, Luke, and John]. Intercede for me. Tecon • Leleloth • Patron • let the abyss [take] you.

Again, a good remedy: In the name of the Lord, let it be blessed. [Anglo-Saxon:] Byrmonic, Beromcen [herb names or charm words]; and he shall have written on his garment and on his thigh: 'King of kings and Lord of lords' [cf. Revelation xix]. Again, a good remedy: In the name [of the Lord] let it be blessed. [Runic/cryptic sequence:] M M M R M P • N G • L T X X M R F P N + • P T X.

[Anglo-Saxon directions:] One shall, while writing, first write and then place that word, writing it upon the swollen [afflicted] breast, and not let it go in thereon nor write thereon; and also, writing [the charm] upon [the patient]:
EMMANUEL • BYRNICE • NOSTER ASTER.

[Continuation of remedies, rendered from the English lower portion of the page:]
Dust [of the prescribed herbs] into milk; drip thrice some holy water upon them; administer this to drink at three hours — at undern (nine in the morning), at midday, or at the ninth hour (three in the afternoon). If the disease be on cattle, pour that same dust into the mouth with holy water.

A salve: Boil lupin, hedgerife [hedge-mustard or ground-ivy], bishopwort [wood betony or bishop's weed], red maythe [red chamomile], harmala [wild rue, Peganum harmala], cropleek, and salt, in butter to a salve; smear it on the head and the breast.

A drink: Put into holy water — viper's bugloss, alexanders, rue, lupins, hedgerife, bishopwort, maythe, cropleek, harmala, and the wenwort which hath knees [knotted figwort or knotweed].

If a mare [nightmare-demon] or hag ride a man, take lupins and garlic and betony and frankincense; bind them on a fawn's skin; let a man have the worts on him and let him go into his home.

entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from plain maythe by colour qualifier.

entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'cropleac'; a leek variant used medicinally.

entities_mentioned.notes: Classical medicinal herb; ingredient in drink.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Polygonum aviculare (knotgrass) or Scrophularia nodosa (knotted figwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'knees' descriptor suggests a jointed or knotted stem plant; identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.notes: Used in charm against hag-riding, bound on fawn's skin.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a composite: the upper portion (item 16) is a Latin-and-Old-English charm with cryptic runic/alphabetic sequences and invocations of the evangelists, followed by a Revelation xix reference ('King of kings and Lord of lords'). The lower portion of the page is a continuation of remedies rendered partly in printed English (apparently the editor's translation) and partly in Old English directives. The runic sequences (M M M R M P etc.) are likely garbled transmission of a magical cipher and are not fully decipherable. 'Mare' in the hag-riding passage refers to the nightmare-demon (Old English 'mære'), not the horse. The footnote identifies 'harmala' as Peganum harmala. OCR and manuscript damage make several words uncertain, especially in the runic line and the charm names (Tecon, Leleloth, Patron) which are likely corrupted voces magicae. 'Byrmonice'/'Beromcen' in the Latin charm section may be corrupt herb names or charm words rather than personal names. The editorial footnote notes the head reading variant 'ΦMRMW|· NjITX' and that 'T' should be read as 'I' in the runic sequence.

results.title_inferred: Remedies against mental vacancy, folly, bewitched food/drink, and dog-bite (Leech Book I, Chapters 65–68 excerpt)
results.original_text:
1. Again, a drink against lent addle or iijplivs; feverfue, the herb rams gall [Menyanthes trifoliata], fennel, waybroad; let a man sing many masses over the worts, souse them with ale, add holy water, boil very thoroughly, let the man drink a great cup full, as hot as he may, before the disorder will be on him; say the names of the four gospellers, and a charm, and a prayer, etc. Again, a divine prayer, etc., þine hand vexeth, thine hand vexeth.

Again, a man shall in silence write this, and silently put these words on the left breast, and let him not go in doors with that writing, nor bear it in doors. And also in silence put this on, Emmanuel, veronica.

Ch. lxvi. Against mental vacancy and against folly; put into ale bishopwort, lupins, betony, the southern or Italian fennel, nepte, water agrimony, cockle, marche, then let the man drink. For idiotcy and folly, put into ale, cassia, and lupins, bishopwort, Alexandrian [herb], zedoary, fieldmore, and holy water, then let him drink.

Ch. lxvii. Against bewitched [taken/spoiled] meat; take lupins, lay them under the altar, sing nine masses over them; that shall be against taken [bewitched] meat; lay it under the vessel [vat] that thou wilt milk into. If ale be turned [spoiled], take the lupins, lay them on the four sides of the building and over the door and under the threshold, and under the ale-vat; put the wort into the ale with holy water. If meat be spoiled and the milk bewitched, whether fresh, wild [i.e. from wild animals], or brewed, hallow the worts, put them under the vat and under the door; put lupins and cleavers and betony and bishopwort.

Ch. lxviii. Against the case when a hound has bitten a man, which is a very dangerous wound: draw out the blood with three cuts near the front; throw it then over the way away, then there will be no evil. Again, cut one cut over the wound, pound mugwort, lay it on; there will be no evil to him. Against the bite of a spider [gongelwæpsan], take the nether [lower] part—
results.translation:
1. Again, a drink against epilepsy [lent addle] or a related convulsive condition [iijplivs, possibly 'triplus' or a corrupt term for a type of seizure]: feverfew, the herb bogbean [Menyanthes trifoliata, here called 'rams gall'], fennel, plantain [waybroad]; let a man sing many masses over the plants, steep them in ale, add holy water, boil very thoroughly, let the man drink a great cupful as hot as he may, before the attack comes upon him; say the names of the four evangelists, and a charm, and a prayer, etc. Again, a divine prayer: 'Thine hand vexeth, thine hand vexeth.'

Again, a man shall in silence write this [charm], and silently place these words against his left breast, and let him not go indoors bearing that writing, nor carry it indoors. And also in silence put this on: Emmanuel, Veronica [i.e. the image on the kerchief].

Chapter 66. Against mental vacancy and against folly: put into ale bishopwort [Betonica officinalis / Stachys betonica], lupins [Lupinus spp.], betony, the southern or Italian fennel [Foeniculum vulgare], nepte [catmint, Nepeta cataria], water agrimony [Bidens tripartita or Eupatorium cannabinum], cockle [Agrostemma githago], marche [smallage / wild celery, Apium graveolens], then let the man drink. For idiocy and folly, put into ale: cassia, and lupins, bishopwort, Alexandrian herb [possibly Smyrnium olusatrum], zedoary [Curcuma zedoaria], fieldmore [possibly Peucedanum palustre or similar], and holy water, then let him drink.

Chapter 67. Against bewitched [supernaturally spoiled] meat: take lupins, lay them under the altar, sing nine masses over them; this shall work against bewitched meat; lay them under the vessel into which you wish to milk. If ale has turned [gone bad], take the lupins, lay them at the four corners of the building and over the door and under the threshold and under the ale-vat; put the plant into the ale with holy water. If food is spoiled and milk is bewitched — whether fresh, from wild animals, or prepared — hallow the plants, put them under the vat and under the door; add lupins and cleavers [Galium aparine] and betony and bishopwort.

Chapter 68. Against a hound having bitten a man, which is a very dangerous wound: by three cuts near the front, let the blood flow; throw it then away over a path, and then there will be no evil [i.e., no ill effect]. Again, cut one incision over the wound, pound mugwort [Artemisia vulgaris], lay it on; there will be no evil to him. Against the bite of a spider [gongelwæpsan, literally 'going-wasp', possibly spider or harvest-mite], take the lower [nether] part—[text continues]

results.translator_notes: The segment is drawn from Cockayne's edition of the Old English Leech Book (Læce Boc), covering chapters 65 (end), 66, 67, and 68 (beginning). The source text contains both the English translation printed by Cockayne and the underlying Old English (printed in a Saxonized font with special characters). 'Lent addle' is an Old English term for epilepsy or a convulsive sickness; 'iijplivs' is likely a corrupt or abbreviated Latin/Greek term, possibly 'triplus' referring to a third-day fever or a triple-form seizure. The Old English runic-style characters 'þ' (thorn) represent the 'th' sound: 'þine hand vexa' = 'thine hand vexeth.' The charm text 'Emmanuel, veronica' invokes sacred names as amuletic protection. The ritual elements (singing masses, applying inscribed amulets, performing procedures in silence) reflect the syncretic magical-Christian healing tradition of Anglo-Saxon medicine. Editor footnotes identify 'rams gall' as Menyanthes trifoliata and 'veronica' as the image on the kerchief. OCR rendering of the Old English is partially corrupt but has been interpreted conservatively.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Spoilt Provisions, Spider Bites, and Related Ailments (Leech Book I, sections LXVII–LXX)
results.original_text:
2. If meat be spoilt, and a good quantity of milken food, or a milking, or brewing, hallow the worts, put them into and under the vat, and under the door; use lupins, and clifwort, and betony, and bishopwort.

LXVIII.
In case that a hunting spider bite a man, that is the stronger spider, strike three scarifications near, in a direction from the bite, let the blood run into a green spoon of hazel wood, then throw it over the road away; then no harm will come of it. Again, strike a scarification on the wound; pound leechwort; lay it on, no harm will happen to the man. Against bite of a weaving spider, take the netherward part of seferthe, and lichen from the blackthorn, dry it to dust, moisten with honey, tend the wound therewith. Against bite of hunting spider, black snails fried in a hot pan and rubbed to dust, and pepper, and betony, let the man eat the dust and drink it and lay it on.

Against hunting spider bite: take the lower part of cottuc, lay it on the wound. Again, strike five scarifications, one on the bite and four around it, throw [blood] with a stick, dripping, over the crossways.

LXIX.
Against bite of a mad dog: agrimony and waybread, mix with honey and egg-white, tend the wound with it. Against dog's wound: fox's clote [burdock], groundsel, boil in butter, smear with it. Again, pound betony, lay it on the bite. Again, pound waybread, lay it on. Again, two onions or three, seething, scorched in ashes, mix with grease and honey, lay on. Again, burn a swine's jaw-bone to ashes, scatter on. Again, take waybread root, pound with grease, put on the wound; it will then drive the venom away.

LXX.
If a man be too loose [suffering from looseness of the bowels], boil hindheoloru [hindberry/raspberry] in Welsh ale, drink at night fasting. If a man be too constrained [costive], boil the same plant in milk, then it will loosen you. Boil in ewe's milk again hindheoloru, Alexandrian laurel, a plant called forewater-palm; then it will be equally dear to the liver.
results.translation:
2. If meat be spoilt, and a good quantity of dairy food, or a milking, or a brewing [is at risk], hallow the medicinal herbs [worts], place them into and under the vat, and under the door; use lupins, and clifwort, and betony, and bishopwort.

Section LXVIII.
In the event that a hunting spider (that is, the stronger kind of spider) bites a man: make three scarifications [small incisions] close to the bite and directed away from it; let the blood run into a fresh green hazel-wood spoon, then throw it away over the road; then no harm will come of it. Again, make a scarification on the wound; pound leechwort [a medicinal herb]; lay it on; no harm will befall the man. Against the bite of a weaving spider: take the lower portion of seferthe [unidentified plant], and lichen from the blackthorn; dry to a powder; moisten with honey; treat the wound with this. Against the bite of a hunting spider: fry black snails in a hot pan, rub them to a powder, mix with pepper and betony; let the man eat the powder, drink it, and also apply it to the wound.

Against a hunting spider bite: take the lower part of cottuc [mallow], lay it on the wound. Again, make five scarifications — one directly on the bite and four around it — and throw the blood with a stick, letting it drip, over a crossroads.

Section LXIX.
Against the bite of a mad dog: mix agrimony and waybread [plantain] with honey and egg-white; treat the wound with this preparation. Against a dog-bite wound: boil fox's clote [burdock] and groundsel in butter; smear on. Again, pound betony, lay it on the bite. Again, pound waybread [plantain], lay it on. Again, take two or three onions, cook them and scorch them in ashes, mix with grease and honey, lay on. Again, burn a pig's jawbone to ashes and scatter the ash on [the wound]. Again, take a waybread [plantain] root, pound with grease, apply to the wound; this will drive the venom away.

Section LXX.
If a man suffer from looseness of the bowels [diarrhea]: boil hindheoloru [hindberry/raspberry] in Welsh ale [a type of ale]; drink at night on an empty stomach. If a man be too costive [constipated]: boil the same plant in milk; this will loosen him. Boil again in ewe's milk: hindheoloru [raspberry], Alexandrian laurel, and the plant called forewater-palm [identity uncertain]; then it will be equally beneficial to the liver.
