results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from the Old English Leech Book (Bald's Leechbook), Book I, sections LXVII–LXX, as edited and translated by Cockayne. The source interleaves Cockayne's English translation with the original Old English manuscript text (with some MS variants noted). Several plant names (seferthe, cottuc, clifwort, forwæterpealm) remain uncertain in modern botanical identification. The editor's footnotes identify the 'hunting spider' with Salticus scenicus but express reservations, suggesting the description better fits a tarantula; the 'weaving spider' is identified as Aranea viatica. The scarification-and-crossroads ritual for spider bite reflects both medical and folk-magical practice characteristic of Anglo-Saxon medicine. Section LXX presents a notable example of the same plant (hindheoloru/raspberry) being prescribed both for diarrhea (in ale) and constipation (in milk), illustrating the theory that the vehicle (ale vs. milk) determines the therapeutic action. OCR artifacts in the Old English text have been preserved as faithfully as possible; some characters are rendered approximately due to Unicode limitations of the source scan.

results.title_inferred: Leech Book I, Chapters 71–72: Treatments for the Dorsal Muscle, Broken Heel Sinew, and Rules for Bloodletting by Season and Moon
results.original_text:
LXXII. Ch. lxxi.

For the dorsal muscle, seethe in oil and in wax, rue so green, smear the dorsal muscle therewith. Again, take goats hair, make it smoke under the breech up against the dorsal muscle. If a heel sinew be broken, take Fornets palm, seethe it in water, foment the limb therewith, and wash the limb therewith; and work a salve of butter, smear after the fomentation.

Lxxii.

On what season bloodletting is to be foregone, on what to be practised. Bloodletting is to be foregone fifteen nights ere Lammas, and after it for five and thirty nights, since then all venomous things fly and much injure men. Leeches who were wisest, have taught, that in that month no man should either drink a potion drink, nor anywhere weaken his body, except there were a necessity for it; and that in that case, he during the middle of the day should remain within, since the lyft or air is then most mingled and impure. The Romans for this reason, and all south folk, wrought to themselves earth houses, for the boiling heat and venomousness of the lyft. Also leeches say that blossomed worts are then best to work, either for drinks, or for salves, or for dust. Here is set forth how a man shall forego bloodletting on each of the six fives in the month, and when it is best. Leeches teach that no man on the five nights old moon, and again on the ten nights old, and fifteen nights old, and twenty, and five and twenty, and on the thirty nights old moon should let blood; but between each of those six fives. And there is no bloodletting time so good as in early spring, when the evil humours that were drunk in during winter are gathered together; and on the Kalends of April most of all, when trees and plants first shoot forth, then grows the evil fleshy growth and evil blood in the hollows of the body. If a man's blood be evilly swollen, take then a decoction of cress, boil in water and foment therewith, and scrape downward, lay on. If thou wilt stop blood flowing in a cut, take some charcoal ground to dust, scatter on the wound. Take rye straw again and barley, burn to dust [powder].
results.translation:
Chapter 71.

For the dorsal muscle [back muscle]: seethe rue, while still green, in oil and in wax, and smear the dorsal muscle therewith. Again, take goat's hair, make it smoke under the breech [buttocks/perineum] up against the dorsal muscle. If a heel sinew be broken, take Fornet's palm [an unidentified plant], seethe it in water, foment the limb therewith, and wash the limb therewith; and work a salve of butter, smear [it on] after the fomentation.

Chapter 72.

On what season bloodletting is to be foregone and on what [season] it is to be practised. Bloodletting is to be foregone for fifteen nights before Lammas [August 1st] and after it for thirty-five nights, since at that time all venomous creatures fly about and greatly injure men. The wisest leeches [physicians] have taught that in that month no man should drink a medicinal potion, nor in any way weaken his body, unless there were necessity for it; and in that case he should remain indoors during the middle of the day, since the lyft [air/atmosphere] is then most mingled and impure. The Romans for this reason, and all southern peoples, constructed for themselves underground houses, because of the scorching heat and venomousness of the lyft. The leeches also say that blossomed herbs are then best to prepare, whether for drinks, or for salves, or for powders. Here it is set forth how a man shall avoid bloodletting on each of the six groups of five [nights] in the month, and when it is best [to let blood]. Leeches teach that no man should let blood on the five-nights-old moon, nor again on the ten-nights-old, nor the fifteen-nights-old, nor the twenty, nor the twenty-five, nor the thirty-nights-old moon; but [only] between each of those six groups of five. And there is no bloodletting time so good as early spring, when the evil humours that were ingested during winter are gathered together; and on the Kalends of April [April 1st] most of all, when trees and plants first shoot upward — then the evil fleshy growth and evil blood increase in the cavities of the body. If a man's blood be evilly swollen [congested/inflamed], then take a decoction of cress, boil it in water and foment therewith, and scrape downward [toward the extremities], and lay it on. If thou wilt stop blood from flowing in a wound, take some charcoal ground to dust and scatter it on the wound. Take rye straw again and barley, burn to dust [powder].

entities_mentioned.name: rue (so green)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used fresh/green, seethed in oil and wax, applied as a smear to the dorsal muscle for back pain or muscle complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: goat's hair
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a fumigation remedy — burned and applied as smoke to the perineal/dorsal region. No modern pharmacological equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: Fornet's palm (Fornetes palm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Unidentified plant name; possibly a proper-name plant or a corruption of an Old English plant name. Used for a broken heel sinew: seethed in water, used as a fomentation and wash.

entities_mentioned.name: butter (salve)
entities_mentioned.notes: Worked into a salve and applied after fomentation for a broken heel sinew.

entities_mentioned.name: cress (geocsæu leaf / decoction of cress)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Nasturtium officinale (watercress) or a related Lepidium/Cardamine sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in water, used as a fomentation for blood swelling/congestion; the Old English 'geocsæu' is tentatively identified with cress.

entities_mentioned.name: charcoal (ground to dust)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a hemostatic powder scattered on wounds to stop bleeding.

entities_mentioned.name: rye straw and barley (burned to dust/powder)
entities_mentioned.notes: Burned to ash/powder; likely also used as a hemostatic or wound-dressing agent, though the text is fragmentary at this point.

entities_mentioned.name: blossomed worts
entities_mentioned.notes: General term for herbs in flower; recommended for preparation of drinks, salves, and powders during the dangerous summer season around Lammas.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Lyft' is an Old English term for air or atmosphere, retained in the original with a bracketed gloss. 2. 'Leeches' throughout refers to physicians/healers, not the animal. 3. 'Lammas' is August 1st (Old English harvest festival). 4. 'Kalends of April' = April 1st. 5. 'Fornets palm' remains unidentified; Cockayne's edition does not resolve it satisfactorily. 6. The segment header 'X. nihca -j piptyne…' appears to be a fragment of Old English text relating to 'ten and twenty…thirty' moon-phase enumeration, consistent with the bloodletting moon-phase rules in the body of the chapter. 7. The footnote reference to 'bloblaese is feminine' relates to an Old English grammatical point about the word for bloodletting (blódlǣs), not translated into the main text. 8. The Latin quotations in footnotes (plumbeus auster, etc.) are Horatian/Virgilian allusions added by the Victorian editor Cockayne, not part of the original source. 9. The final lines of the Old English are fragmentary/damaged; translation of the rye straw passage is tentative.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Hair Loss, Baldness, Mange in Horses, and Related Ailments
results.original_text:
1. If a mans hair fall off, work him a salve, take the mickle wolfs bane, and vipers bugloss, and the netherward part of burdock, and ferdwort, work the salve out of that wort, and out of all these, and out of that butter on which no water hath come. If hair fall off, boil the polypody fern, and foment the head with that, so warm. In case that a man be bald, Plinius, the mickle leech, saith this leechdom: take dead bees, burn them to ashes, and linseed also, add oil upon that, seethe very long over gledes, then strain, wring out, and take leaves of willow, pound them, pour the juice into the oil, boil again for a while on gledes, strain them, smear therewith after the bath.

[Old English continuation:]
Deaþ bona þis þon · felijeð leaf pylle on wætere þweah mid þy tep þu hit smepupe · þa leap cnua swa jefoden wruþ on niht on oþ þ hio fie · bruje þ þu mæje smeppan æfter mid þære sealpe bo swa xxx. nihta lenj jif his þearf lie. Wiþ þon þe hwer ne weaxe fol. 58a. lemetwan rejpu jenim mid smit on þa stofe ne cymþ þær næppe ænij weax up; Gif hær to þicce lie jenim swealpan jebærn under cyrelan to ahsan þ þet sceadan þa ahsan on.

LXXXVIII.
Wiþ horses hreopole · nim þa hæranwyrt cnua wel jemenj þonne wiþ frescre buteran jyl swiþe on butian bo on þ hors swa hit hatost mæje smipe ælce dæje bo simle þa sealpe on · jif sio hreopolie micel jenim hland jehæst mid stanum þweah mid þy hlonbe swa hatum þ hors þonne hit drije fie smipe mid þiere sealpe lacua nine. Eft jenim rinjan sealtes þweah mid þy · þ þonne drije fie smipe mid pipres smejipe. Jif hors jeallede fie · nim seþelwerjunj ryrt · þ jorþorman · þ mæjeþan jecnua wel bo buteran to pring wrungendre þurh claþ bo hwit sealt on hwer siþþe lacna þone jeallan mid. Þis horses jeallan nim æccþrotan þ jorwoþan neowearde þ bojen eac swa cnua tosomne syl on wyle þ on buteran afeoh þurh claþ smipe mid.

Gif hors fie ofscoten oþþe oþer neat nim omppan fol. 58b. jieb þ scottisc weax jesinge mon xii. siþþan ofer þ bo halijwæter on þ hors oþþe on swa hwilc neat swa hit fie hafa þe þa wyrte simle mid.

Wiþ þon ilcan nim tobreceendre næble eaje scinje hindan on þone byrelan ne biþ nan teona.
results.translation:
1. If a man's hair falls off, work him a salve: take the great wolf's bane [aconite], and viper's bugloss, and the lower/root part of burdock, and ferdwort [herb uncertain]; work the salve from that plant and from all these, and from butter upon which no water has come [i.e., clarified/unsalted butter]. If hair falls off, boil the polypody fern and foment [bathe] the head with that, warm. In case a man be bald, Pliny, the great physician [leech], gives this remedy: take dead bees, burn them to ashes, and linseed likewise; add oil to that, and boil very long over coals [gledes]; then strain and wring out; then take willow leaves, pound them, and pour the juice into the oil; boil again for a while on the coals; strain; smear therewith after the bath.

[Old English text, translated:]
Beyond this, concerning that [condition]: take leaves of willowherb [?], boil in water, wash with it [the affected area], while you smear it; pound the leaves thus prepared; apply in the night until it [the affected skin] be dry, so that you may smear afterward with the salve; do so thirty nights long if need be. Against [the condition] that hair may not grow [i.e., to prevent regrowth in unwanted places]: take soot [or ashes of a certain preparation] and apply with it to the place; no hair will ever grow there again. If hair be too thick: take a swallow, burn it under a tile [or earthen cover] to ashes, and scatter those ashes on [the head/scalp].

LXXXVIII.
For mange [hreopol, i.e., scab/mange] in a horse: take the herb hare-wort [hæranwyrt, uncertain], pound it well, mix it then with fresh butter, boil vigorously in butter; apply it to the horse as hot as can be borne; smear every day, always applying the salve. If the mange be great [severe], take urine [hland], heat it with stones, wash the horse with that urine as hot as possible; when it is dry, smear with the salve as the remedy requires. Again, take brine [salted water], wash with it; and when dry, smear with pepper ointment [pipres smeþe]. If a horse has galls [sores/swellings from harness]: take agrimony [?seþelwerjung-wyrt], and groundsel [gorþorman?], and mayweed [mægeþan]; pound them well; add butter; wring out through a cloth; add white salt at each treatment; treat the gall therewith. For horse galls [another remedy]: take sorrel [æcþrote?] and the upper part of groundsel [gorwoþan neowearde], and also ivy [bojen]; pound together; boil in wine and in butter; strain through a cloth; smear therewith.

If a horse or other animal is elf-shot [ofscoten, i.e., afflicted by sudden mysterious illness]: take [a certain herb, name ILLEGIBLE: text damaged], and Scottish wax; let a man sing [i.e., chant or recite] twelve times over it, and put holy water on the horse or on whatever animal it may be; let him who [administers the remedy] always keep the plant with him [as a charm].

For the same [elf-shot]: take a broken needle; sing [chant] over it from behind; thrust it into the [animal's] hide; there will be no harm.

entities_mentioned.name: mickle wolf's bane (great wolf's bane)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum napellus (monkshood/aconite) or Verbascum thapsus (great mullein); more likely Verbascum or a large-leaved Aconitum in Anglo-Saxon usage
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'micel wulfes bana'; the 'mickle' (great) qualifier may distinguish a species from lesser wolfsbane. Used topically in a hair-loss salve.

entities_mentioned.name: viper's bugloss
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Anglo-Saxon plant name mapping. Ingredient in hair-loss salve.

entities_mentioned.name: burdock (netherward part, i.e., root)
entities_mentioned.notes: The root portion ('netherward part') was used. Ingredient in hair-loss salve.

entities_mentioned.name: ferdwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon plant name not securely identified in modern botanical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: polypody fern
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled and used as a warm fomentation on the head for hair loss.

entities_mentioned.name: dead bees (burned to ash)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny's remedy for baldness: burned to ash, mixed with linseed and oil.

entities_mentioned.name: linseed
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with bee ash and oil in baldness remedy attributed to Pliny.

entities_mentioned.name: willow leaves
entities_mentioned.notes: Pounded; juice added to oil in Pliny's baldness remedy. Applied after bathing.

entities_mentioned.name: hæranwyrt (hare-wort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in horse mange remedy; not securely identified in modern botany.

entities_mentioned.name: mægeþan (mayweed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingredient in horse gall remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: seþelwerjung-wyrt (agrimony or noble herb)
entities_mentioned.notes: Name is tentative; used for horse galls.

entities_mentioned.name: gorþorman / gorwoþan (groundsel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in horse gall remedies; upper part (neowearde) specified in one instance.

entities_mentioned.name: bojen (ivy)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with groundsel and pounded for horse gall remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: Scottish wax
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in elf-shot remedy; nature unclear—possibly a type of wax or resinous substance of Scottish provenance.

entities_mentioned.name: swallow (burned to ash)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hirundo rustica or Riparia riparia (sand martin, as noted in footnote)
entities_mentioned.notes: Footnote identifies 'sand martins' (hirundines ripariae). Ash scattered on scalp to thin excessive hair.

entities_mentioned.name: holy water
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied to elf-shot animals as part of a charm remedy combining herbal and liturgical elements.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Cockayne's edition of the Old English Leechbook (Leece Boc / Lœce Boc), Volume II. The first portion (hair-loss remedies) is in Middle English translation by Cockayne; the remainder is the underlying Old English text, here translated from the normalized Old English. The remedy attributed to 'Plinius the mickle leech' (Pliny the Elder) reflects classical influence on Anglo-Saxon medicine via intermediary texts. 'Gledes' means live coals or embers. 'Ofscoten' (elf-shot) denotes a folk-medical concept of sudden illness in animals attributed to supernatural causes. Footnote 1 in the original identifies 'sand martins' (hirundines ripariae) in relation to the swallow remedy. Several plant identifications remain uncertain due to ambiguous Old English names. OCR artifacts in the Old English text (e.g., 'j' for 'g', thorn/eth confusion) have been silently normalized in the translation. The editorial footnotes suggesting emendations (e.g., 'Read rinjan sealtes' = brine/salt-water) have been incorporated into the translation.

results.title_inferred: Veterinary remedies for horse leprosy and galls
results.original_text: 1. For a horses leprosy, take the harewort, pound it well, then mingle with fresh butter, boil thoroughly in butter, put it on the horse as hot as possible, smear every day, always apply the salve. If the leprosy be mickle, take piss, heat it with stones, wash the horse with the piss so hot; when it is dry, smear with the salve, apply also leechdoms inwardly. Again, take runnings of salt, heat them, wash with that, and when it is dry, smear with fishes grease. If a horse be galled, take stichwort, and goutweed, and maythe, pound well, add butter, wring it wetting it through a cloth, add white salt, shake thoroughly, leech the gall therewith. For a horses gall, take ash-throat, and the upward part of goutweed, and rosemary also, pound together, boil in fat and in butter, strain through a cloth, smear therewith.
results.translation: 1. For a horse's leprosy [scabrous or mange-like skin condition], take harewort, pound it well, then mix with fresh butter, boil thoroughly in butter, apply it to the horse as hot as possible, smear every day, and always keep the salve on. If the leprosy be severe [mickle = great], take urine, heat it with hot stones, wash the horse with the urine while it is hot; when it is dry, smear with the salve, and also apply remedies [leechdoms] internally. Again, take brine [runnings of salt], heat it, wash the horse with that, and when it is dry, smear with fish grease. If a horse be galled [has a sore or abrasion], take stichwort, and goutweed, and maythe [mayweed/chamomile], pound well, add butter, wring the mixture through a cloth, add white salt, shake thoroughly, and treat the gall with it. For a horse's gall, take ash-throat, and the upper part of goutweed, and rosemary as well, pound together, boil in fat and in butter, strain through a cloth, and smear therewith.

entities_mentioned.name: harewort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Althaea officinalis (marsh mallow) or a related mucilaginous herb; some authorities identify Old English 'harewort' with Arctium lappa (burdock)
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of Old English 'harewort' is disputed. Used here as an external emollient for horse skin disease.

entities_mentioned.name: fresh butter
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle/base for the herbal salve.

entities_mentioned.name: piss (urine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Heated and used as a wash for severe skin disease; a common ancient veterinary and medical practice owing to mild ammonia content.

entities_mentioned.name: runnings of salt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brine; liquid that drains from salted meat or a salt solution
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an antiseptic/astringent wash.

entities_mentioned.name: fishes grease
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied as an emollient after washing.

entities_mentioned.name: stichwort
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stellaria holostea (greater stitchwort) or related Stellaria spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used externally for galls (sores/abrasions) in horses.

entities_mentioned.name: goutweed
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in two separate remedies; used both for galls and in combination with ash-throat and rosemary.

entities_mentioned.name: maythe
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anthemis cotula (stinking mayweed) or Matricaria chamomilla (chamomile)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'mægþe' is generally identified as a mayweed or chamomile species.

entities_mentioned.name: white salt
entities_mentioned.notes: Added to the poultice as a preservative and mild antiseptic.

entities_mentioned.name: ash-throat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly Verbascum thapsus (mullein) or an ash-related plant preparation; some suggest a compound name referring to the ash tree (Fraxinus excelsior) bark or inner part
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English veterinary term not definitively identified in modern botanical literature. Possibly 'æsc-þrote' = mullein, which is sometimes glossed as such.

entities_mentioned.name: rosemary
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an aromatic and anti-inflammatory herb in the salve for horse galls.

results.translator_notes: The source text is already in Early Modern English (a translation from Old English, likely from Cockayne's 'Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England,' Vol. 2). The segment number '1.' suggests it is the first recipe in a veterinary chapter. 'Leprosy' in this context almost certainly refers to a mange-like or scabby skin disease in horses rather than true leprosy (Mycobacterium leprae). 'Mickle' is an archaic/dialectal word meaning 'great' or 'much.' 'Leechdoms inwardly' means internal medicinal remedies administered orally. 'Galled' refers to skin sores or chafing wounds, common in working horses. The OCR artifact 'inwardl3^' has been silently corrected to 'inwardly' and 'dr}'' to 'dry' per translation rules.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Elf-Shot in Livestock and Table of Contents for Leech Book II (Disorders of the Stomach/Inwards)
results.original_text:
2. If a horse or other neat be elf shot, take sorrel seed and Scottish wax, let a man sing twelve masses over it, and put holy water on the horse, or on whatsoever neat it be, have the worts always with thee.

3. For the same; take an eye of a broken needle, give the horse a prick with it behind in the barrel, no harm shall come.

[Footnotes: 1. Grease in the legs? 2. The Scottish phrase for this disease; see the Glossary.]

Book II. Contents:
i. These leechdoms belong to all disorders of the inwards.
ii. Leechdoms for sore of the maw, in all ten, and if the maw be distended, and what the patient shall eat in that disorder.
iii. Leechdoms for swelling and sore of the maw, how one must let him, the patient, blood.
iv. Leechdoms for hard swelling of the maw, and smearings, or unguents, and what the patient's diet shall be.
v. Leechdoms for distension of the maw, and what the patient shall eat in that disorder.
vi. Leechdoms for nausea and phlegm that come from the maw, and what the patient shall eat; four remedies.
vii. Leechdoms for a deadened maw, and if it be disordered, and signs of a deadened maw, how it does not digest what he eats; six leechdoms.
viii. Leechdoms for sore and nausea of the maw, such as cannot be healed either with food or with drink, and bitter hiccupping of the throat; four remedies.
ix. Leechdoms for a diseased maw.
x. A leechdom for phlegm and for heating an infirm maw.
xi. A leechdom for a windy and sour distension of the maw.
xii. A leechdom for hiccup and for when food will not remain below.
xiii. A leechdom for springiness [spasm] of the maw.
xiv. A leechdom for all infirmities of the maw.

results.translation:
2. If a horse or other cattle [neat] be elf-shot [afflicted with a sudden lameness or mysterious ailment attributed to elves], take sorrel seed and Scottish wax [a medicinal wax of Scottish origin], let a man sing twelve masses over it, and put holy water on the horse, or on whatever cattle beast it may be; keep the herbs [worts] always with thee.

3. For the same condition: take the eye [hole] of a broken needle, give the horse a prick with it behind in the barrel [the body cavity/flank], and no harm shall come.

[Editor's footnotes: 1. 'Elf-shot' may refer to 'grease in the legs' [a condition causing swelling in horse legs]. 2. 'Elf-shot' is the Scottish phrase for this disease; see the Glossary.]

Leech Book II — Table of Contents:
i. These remedies [leechdoms] pertain to all disorders of the internal organs [inwards].
ii. Remedies for soreness of the stomach [maw], ten in all, and for when the stomach is distended, and what the patient should eat during that illness.
iii. Remedies for swelling and soreness of the stomach, including how one must let the patient's blood.
iv. Remedies for hard swelling of the stomach, and ointments [smearings/unguents], and what the patient's diet should be.
v. Remedies for distension of the stomach, and what the patient should eat during that illness.
vi. Remedies for nausea and phlegm coming from the stomach, and what the patient should eat; four remedies.
vii. Remedies for a deadened [atonic] stomach, and if it be disordered, and signs of a deadened stomach — how it fails to digest what is eaten; six remedies.
viii. Remedies for soreness and nausea of the stomach that cannot be healed by either food or drink, and for bitter hiccupping of the throat; four remedies.
ix. Remedies for a diseased stomach.
x. A remedy for phlegm and for warming an infirm stomach.
xi. A remedy for windy and sour distension of the stomach.
xii. A remedy for hiccup and for when food will not stay down.
xiii. A remedy for spasm [springiness] of the stomach.
xiv. A remedy for all infirmities of the stomach.
xv. A remedy for spasm of the stomach, when bitterness comes up through the mouth in belching or hiccupping, or when [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off at page end].

entities_mentioned.name: sorrel seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rumex acetosa (common sorrel) or Rumex acetosella (sheep's sorrel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as part of a remedy for elf-shot in livestock. The seed specifically is called for.

entities_mentioned.name: Scottish wax
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly a medicinal wax preparation of Scottish provenance. The editor notes this is a Scottish term; exact composition unclear.

entities_mentioned.name: holy water
entities_mentioned.notes: Blessed water used as part of a ritual/religious healing practice for livestock; reflects Christian overlay on folk remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: worts (herbs)
entities_mentioned.notes: General Old English term for medicinal herbs/plants; no specific plant identified here.

entities_mentioned.name: broken needle (eye of)
entities_mentioned.notes: A ritual/practical implement; the eye (hole) of a broken needle used to prick the horse behind the barrel (flank) as a counter-charm for elf-shot.

entities_mentioned.name: elf-shot (elf shot)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly 'grease' (lymphangitis/scratches) in horses, or other sudden-onset lameness
entities_mentioned.notes: A folk disease category attributed to malicious elves; the editor suggests it may correspond to 'grease in the legs,' a swelling condition in horses. In humans, elf-shot was associated with sudden sharp pains.

results.translator_notes: The segment combines two distinct textual layers: (1) a vernacular remedy section for livestock elf-shot (entries 2–3), and (2) the Old English table of contents for Leech Book II dealing with gastric/stomach disorders. The Old English chapter headings have been rendered into English following the facing-page translation already partially provided in the source, with additional entries translated from the Old English. The term 'maw' (Old English 'maga') refers to the stomach. 'Springiness' (OE 'springe') denotes spasm or convulsive movement. 'Neat' is an archaic English term for bovine cattle. Entry xv is incomplete due to a page break in the manuscript. The footnote numbers in the source appear to be editorial annotations by Cockayne (the editor), not original to the manuscript.
results._label: cockayne_v2::2.  If  a  horse  or  other  neat  be  elf  shot,^

results.title_inferred: Leechdoms for Disorders of the Stomach: Table of Contents (Sections V–IX)
results.original_text:
V. Leechdoms for puffing up of the maw, and what the patient shall partake of in this disorder.

vi. Leechdoms for want of appetite and for nausea, which cometh of the maw, and what the patient shall eat; four crafts, or skilful recipes.

vii. Leechdoms for deadened maw, and if it have bad lymph, and tokens of deadened maw, how that digests not, which it eateth; six leechdoms.

viii. Leechdoms for sore and want of appetite of the maw, which may be cured neither with meat nor drink, and suffereth bitter risings in the throat; four receipts.

ix. Leechdoms for an inward wound of the maw.
results.translation:
V. Remedies [leechdoms] for bloating [puffing up] of the stomach [maw], and what the patient shall consume in this condition.

VI. Remedies for loss of appetite and for nausea arising from the stomach, and what the patient shall eat; four skilled recipes [crafts].

VII. Remedies for an atonic [deadened] stomach, and if it contain bad fluid [lymph], and the signs [tokens] of an atonic stomach — namely, that it does not digest what is eaten; six remedies.

VIII. Remedies for pain and loss of appetite of the stomach, which can be relieved neither by food nor drink, and which is accompanied by bitter regurgitation [risings] in the throat; four recipes.

IX. Remedies for an internal wound [ulcer] of the stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: maw
entities_mentioned.notes: Old/Middle English term 'maw' consistently refers to the stomach throughout this text. 'Deadened maw' likely refers to atony or hypomotility of the stomach. 'Inward wound of the maw' may correspond to gastric ulcer.

entities_mentioned.name: lymph (bad lymph)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: pathological fluid or gastric secretion (humoral concept)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'lymph' here likely reflects a humoral medicine concept of corrupt fluid or phlegm accumulating in the stomach, not lymph in the modern anatomical sense.

entities_mentioned.name: bitter risings in the throat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: acid reflux / gastroesophageal reflux (GERD) or regurgitation
entities_mentioned.notes: The symptom description of bitter material rising into the throat is consistent with acid reflux or regurgitation.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a table of contents or chapter-heading list rather than a full recipe text; no specific plant names, drugs, or preparation methods are described here. The word 'leechdoms' is an Old English term for remedies or medical treatments. 'Crafts' in this context means skilled methods or recipes. The passage is from Cockayne's edition of Anglo-Saxon medical texts (Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft), Volume 2. Section numbering shifts inconsistently between Roman numerals uppercase (V) and lowercase (vi, vii, viii, ix), reflecting the original source formatting.

results.title_inferred: Leech Book II: Table of Contents, Chapters XX–XXIX — Liver and Abdominal Disorders
results.original_text:
XX. Leeches teach this for wound of the liver, when the swelling or matter bursteth forth.

XXI. Leechdoms and tokens of a hardened and puffed up liver in manifold wise, either in the lobes, or in the margins, or in the membranes, or in the hollows, of the liver.

XXII. Leechdoms for the sense of hardness of the liver, and salves, and wort drinks, or if it burst and descend downwards or mounteth up upwards, or if the insensibility and hardness of the liver become too prolonged.

.XXIII. Læcebomaf hyvet him fie to popjanne on lipep able hpaet him fie co healbanne je on læcebomum je on mete · j tacn þ þe fpile þpinan ne mæj ne utypnan on þæpe hppe.

.XXIIII. Læcebomaf · j pyptbjiencaf piþ eallum hpep pæpcum ealjia þpeotyne j pf Lfep peaxe.

.XXV. Læcap eac be eallum pambe coþum j abltim fpeocol tacn funbon j læcebomap j hu mon þa yjelan pætan þæpe pambe lacnian fcyle j þonne abl to þæpe pambe pile pop þæpe yjelan omihtan pætan cneop hatiaþ lenbenu heperaþ jajiaþ þupa lenbena lipan · toje otep betpeox fculdpium utjonj jemenjeb.

.XXVI. Læcebomaf jip fio pamb punb biþ hu f mon onjitan mæje j jelacnian · v. cpæftaf.

.XXVII. Læcebomaf be pambe mipþenlicpe jecynbo oþþe mijfbypbo hu p mon mæje onjitan j jelacnian j be pambe hattpe jecynbo · j be cealbje j pætpe jecynbo j be hattje j bpijje jecynbo j f hæmed þinj ne buje · þyppum lichoman j ne fcejjeþ hatum ne palatum · feopon cpæptap j þæte hæmed þinj fpiþoft eþlaþ þam þe hopn able habbaþ.

.XXVIII. Læcebomaf piþ þon þe monnej fupepipe hpup fie jepylleb piþ yjelpe pætan j be pinbijpe pambe.

.XXVIIII. Læcebomaf piþ þon þe mete untela mylte j cijjie on pule j yple pætan oþþe pcittan.
results.translation:
XX. Leeches [physicians] teach this for wound of the liver, when the swelling or matter bursteth forth.

XXI. Leechdoms [medical remedies] and tokens [signs/symptoms] of a hardened and puffed-up liver in manifold ways, whether in the lobes, or in the margins, or in the membranes, or in the hollows of the liver.

XXII. Leechdoms for the sensation of hardness of the liver, and salves, and wort drinks [herbal beverages], or if it burst and descend downwards or mount up upwards, or if the insensibility and hardness of the liver become too prolonged.

XXIII. Leechdoms telling what the sick man hath to forego in liver disorder, and what he hath to hold by, either in leechdoms or in diet [meat], and tokens that the swelling may not dwindle nor run off in the liver.

XXIV. Leechdoms and wort drinks for all liver pains, thirteen in all, and if the liver wax [grow enlarged].

XXV. The Leech Book treats also of all belly [abdominal] complaints and distensions; clear tokens have been found, and leechdoms, and how one must treat the evil humours [ill fluids] of the belly; and when disease settles in the belly on account of the evil, noxious humour, the knees grow hot, the loins oppress and make sore the sides of the loins; [the humour] spreads between the shoulders, going out mixed.

XXVI. Leechdoms for when the belly is wounded — how one may recognise it and heal it; five crafts [methods/chapters].

XXVII. Leechdoms concerning the belly's manifold nature or disorder, how one may recognise and heal it; and concerning the belly's hot nature, and of cold and wet nature, and of hot and dry nature; and that the act of intercourse [sexual thing] may not harm a thin body, and does not injure the hot nor the cold; seven crafts; and that sexual intercourse is most troublesome to those who have a horn complaint [perhaps a hardening or calculus condition].

XXVIII. Leechdoms for when a man's upper gut [upper digestive tract] is filled with evil humour, and concerning a windy belly.

XXIX. Leechdoms for when food is ill digested and turns foul [putrid] and produces evil humour or loose flux [diarrhoea].

entities_mentioned.name: liver (liþer / lifer)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: hepatic organ; conditions described suggest hepatomegaly, hepatic abscess, or cirrhosis
entities_mentioned.notes: Multiple chapters address liver hardening, swelling, and rupture, consistent with ancient humoral diagnoses of hepatic disease.

entities_mentioned.name: wort drinks (wyrtbrencas)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Old English term for medicinal herbal beverages; specific herbs not identified in this table-of-contents passage.

entities_mentioned.name: salves
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied externally in conjunction with internal wort drinks for liver conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: evil humour / noxious fluid (yfel wætan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: pathological bodily fluid; humoral medicine concept
entities_mentioned.notes: Reflects classical Galenic humoral theory transmitted through Anglo-Saxon medical texts.

entities_mentioned.name: horn complaint (horn adl)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: possibly urinary calculi, gall stones, or a hardening condition; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: The Old English 'horn adle' is a disputed term; some scholars associate it with a calculus or horn-like growth. Sexual activity said to aggravate it.

entities_mentioned.name: windy belly (windig womb)
entities_mentioned.notes: Common Old English medical complaint category.

results.translator_notes: This segment is primarily a table of contents (chapter headings) for Book II of the Old English Leech Book (Lǣce Bōc), with the Old English chapter summaries in chapters XXIII–XXIX. The Old English text is heavily damaged or poorly typeset in the source (OCR artefacts are significant); the translation of the Old English chapters relies on Cockayne's own facing-page translation as a guide, cross-checked against the Old English. The footnote 'All the viscera were supposed to get out of place' refers to the ancient/medieval belief in organ displacement. The term 'five crafts' and 'seven crafts' refer to numbered sub-chapters or methods within each section. 'Meat' in XXIII means food/diet generally, not only flesh, following Old English usage. The passage 'hæmed þing' (sexual intercourse) in XXVII is a standard Old English euphemism.

results.title_inferred: Table of Contents: Chapters XXX–XXXVI of the Leechbook, covering disorders of the belly, intestines, rectum, spleen, and related conditions
results.original_text:
XXX. Leechdoms if thou will that thy wamb be always sound, and of disease and sore; and of disease of the wamb and sore of the intestines, and for the moderation of the wamb; sixteen receipts.

XXXI. Leechdoms and symptoms marking of the rope gut and small gut, and of the fæcal discharge; how they suffer unbounded thirst and loss of appetite; and of their complexion or hue, and of the navel, and the dorsal muscles, and rectum, and pit of the belly, and milt, and share or pubes, and how bad leeches ween that that is loin disease or milt wark, and where the wambsick suffer the disorder, and how it is with them, and how a man shall treat them: four methods.

XXXII. Leechdoms how a man shall cure one so afflicted, whether with bloodletting, and salve, and baths, and how to send curatives into the belly. And these leechdoms are efficacious against loin ache, if a man use sand, for dysentery, for diseases of the maw, and gripings, and womens tendernesses, and of the disease where a man would evacuate and is not able (tenesmus), and if the discharge be windy, and watery, and bloody. Twelve methods.

XXXIII. Leechdoms for the perilous disease in which a man casteth from him and speweth, as they say, his excrement through the mouth; and for wounds of the inwards, and sore of the small guts, and for laceration of the inwards, and for inward spasm; and for the wamb which digests late, and the man who is not affected by the leechdoms; there cometh on him dropsy, pain in liver, sore of spleen, retention of urine, inflation of belly, pain in loins, sand and stones wax in the bladder. Thirteen receipts.

[Old English, fol. 61b]
XXXIIII. Leechdoms and by what virtues a man shall give the leechdoms when they are needed for the lungs, and the heart, and the wamb [belly], and the bladder, and the intestines; and how ready [prepared] it be concerning weakness and cold and this late digestion, or if the wamb be overgrown and wounded, and if a man be inwardly bloated, and against inward spasms and itching from below.

XXXV. Leechdoms concerning over-fullness of children and the wamb, and if food digest not well in them, and they spit over [vomit], and if the navel be foul.

XXXVI. Leechdoms concerning milt-wark [spleen disease], and that it be on the left side, and signs of the disease, how uncertain they are, and how long the milt [spleen] be, and of the membrane of the milt on the left side, of the laughter that cometh from the milt; how the milt...
results.translation:
XXX. Leechdoms [medical remedies] for those who wish that their belly [wamb] be always sound and free of disease and soreness; and for disease of the belly and soreness of the intestines, and for the regulation [tempering] of the belly: sixteen receipts.

XXXI. Leechdoms and diagnostic signs pertaining to the large intestine [rope gut] and small intestine, and of fæcal discharge; how patients suffer extreme thirst and loss of appetite; and concerning their complexion or color, and the navel, and the dorsal muscles, and the rectum, and the pit of the belly, and the spleen [milt], and the groin or pubes; and how incompetent physicians suppose these to be loin disease or spleen-ache; and where those sick in the belly suffer the disorder, and how it manifests in them, and how a man shall treat them: four methods.

XXXII. Leechdoms showing how a man shall cure one so afflicted, whether by bloodletting, salve, or baths, and how to administer curatives internally into the belly. These leechdoms are also efficacious against loin-ache, if a man uses grit [sand passed in urine], for dysentery, for diseases of the stomach [maw], and for gripes, and women's tenderness [pelvic pain], and for the disease in which a man would evacuate but cannot (tenesmus), and if the discharge be windy, watery, or bloody: twelve methods.

XXXIII. Leechdoms for the perilous disease in which a man expels and vomits, as it is called, his excrement through the mouth [intestinal obstruction with feculent vomiting]; and for wounds of the internal organs, and soreness of the small intestines, and for laceration of the internal organs, and for internal spasm; and for a belly that digests slowly; and for the man who is not helped by the leechdoms—upon him cometh dropsy, pain in the liver, soreness of the spleen, retention of urine, inflation of the belly, pain in the loins, and gravel and stones that grow in the bladder: thirteen receipts.

XXXIIII. Leechdoms and by what virtues [properties] a man shall administer the remedies that are needed for the lungs, the heart, the belly, the bladder, and the intestines; and how one should be prepared concerning weakness and cold, and this condition of slow digestion; or if the belly be distended and wounded; and if a man be inwardly bloated; and against internal cramps and itching from below.

XXXV. Leechdoms for over-fullness [overfeeding or indigestion] in children, and of the belly; and if food does not digest properly in them, and they vomit; and if the navel be foul [infected or ulcerated].

XXXVI. Leechdoms concerning spleen-ache [milt-wark], and that it is located on the left side; and the signs of the disease, how uncertain they are; and how large the spleen may be; and concerning the membrane of the spleen on the left side; of the laughter that arises from the spleen [a classical notion that the spleen caused laughter]; how the spleen... [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: wamb
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English term used throughout to denote the belly or abdomen generally, sometimes more specifically the stomach or womb depending on context. Here consistently means abdomen/digestive tract.

entities_mentioned.name: rope gut
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English anatomical term for the large intestine, so called from its rope-like appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: milt / milt wark
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'milte' = spleen. 'Milt wark' = spleen-ache or spleen disease. Classical humoral theory associated the spleen with black bile and with laughter.

entities_mentioned.name: tenesmus
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly glossed as such in the source text: 'the disease where a man would evacuate and is not able (tenesmus)'.

entities_mentioned.name: disease in which a man speweth excrement through the mouth
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely describes advanced intestinal obstruction; the vomiting of fecal matter (feculent vomiting) is a recognized late sign of complete bowel obstruction.

entities_mentioned.name: dropsy
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical term for fluid accumulation; in the context of liver and spleen disease, likely refers to ascites.

entities_mentioned.name: sand and stones in the bladder
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Sand' refers to gravel (small calculi); 'stones' to larger calculi.

entities_mentioned.name: laughter that cometh from the milt
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical and medieval notion, derived from Hippocratic and Galenic tradition, that the spleen was the seat of laughter or mirth; also possibly referencing a symptom of splenomegaly interpreted humorally.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a table of contents passage from the Læce Boc (Leechbook), an Old English medical compilation. Chapters XXX–XXXIII are given in English translation by Cockayne; Chapters XXXIIII–XXXVI are in the original Old English (fol. 61b) and have been translated here from the Old English. The footnote clarifies that 'moderation of the wamb' renders Latin 'temperies' and 'commoderatio ventris', meaning the belly should be neither too hot nor too cold — a humoral concept. The phrase 'how bad leeches ween' means 'how incompetent physicians mistakenly suppose.' The source text shows OCR artifacts throughout (e.g., 'tliou', 'tby', 'Ix;', 'ftocal', 'mie') which have been silently corrected where unambiguous. The Old English text of chapters XXXIIII–XXXVI is fragmentary as the segment ends mid-sentence.

results.title_inferred: Table of Contents: Leechdoms for Children's Surfeit, Spleen Complaints, Humours, and Related Ailments
results.original_text:
XXXV. Leechdoms for the overfilling or surfeit of children, and for their wamb, and if their meat digest not well, and if sweat pass from them and stink foully.

XXXVI. Leechdoms of pain in the milt, and that the milt is on the left side, and tokens of the disease, how reckless the sick are, and how long the milt is, and of the film or membrane of the milt on the left side, and of splenetic laughter, which cometh of the milt, how the milt suffereth everything of that which other limbs suffer either hot or cold; and of the bath, and of sexual commerce, and whence the heat cometh and the cold of the milt: eight receipts.

XXXVII. Leechdoms how a man shall tend the man within and without with cold and hot leechdoms, and what meat he is to take, and what he is to forego.

XXXVIII. Leechdoms how a man shall cure the humours and the livid complexion by external applications, and of the evil humours of the milt, and of the lubricity of the humours of the milt.

XXXIX. A leechdom for a windy swollen state of the milt, which cometh of eating of apples, and of nuts, and of peas, and of honey, and which puffeth up throughout the rope gut, and the intestines, and the gastric juice.

[Old English text:]
.XL. Læcedomas be ablawunge and aliehardunge þæs blodes on þam milte.

.XLI. Læcedomas wið þære heardnesse and sare miltes and hu mon niwes spines blædran mid ecede gewylbre gelmescan þa heardnesse and wið eallum mablum þrys criuptas.

.XLII. Læcedomas gif omihtre blod and yfele wætan on þam milte syn þinbende þonne sceal him mon blod læran on þis wisan þe þeos læceboc geþ and be þæs blodes hive.

.XLIII. Læcedomas hwæt him on þære adle to picgeanne sie hwæt to forleanne.

.XLIIII. Læcebom eft þe þe þ yfel uttiht of þam milte swiðe æþele, and þe eac þeah wiþ magan ablawunge and innorþ innescep þa wambe þynnaþ þa oman, bitere hritecetunje awej beþ and breost coþe, and wiþ wæstc, and lifer adle and milte sæstc, and wambe wind eal þa liht.

.XLV. Læcedomas and wiðdrinc wiþ ansvollenum.

.XLVI. Læcedomas wiþ gelipeþerrine sidan wara and tacn wundorlic hwanan he cume and hu sio abl toweard lie, and hu mon þara tilian scyle.

.XLVII. Læcedomas þa þe þynnunge hæbben and fualinge mægen, þam lichoman þe þa hæto medmicle oþþe [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off]
results.translation:
XXXV. Leechdoms [medical remedies] for the overfilling or surfeit of children, and for their belly [wamb], and if their food does not digest well, and if sweat passes from them and stinks foully.

XXXVI. Leechdoms for pain in the milt [spleen], and [a note] that the milt is on the left side, and tokens [signs/symptoms] of the disease, how heedless the sick are, and how long the milt is, and of the film or membrane of the milt on the left side, and of splenetic laughter, which cometh of the milt, how the milt suffereth everything that other limbs suffer whether hot or cold; and of the bath, and of sexual intercourse, and whence the heat cometh and the cold of the milt: eight receipts [recipes/prescriptions].

XXXVII. Leechdoms [explaining] how a man shall tend the patient within and without with cold and hot leechdoms, and what food he is to take, and what he is to forgo.

XXXVIII. Leechdoms [explaining] how a man shall cure the humours and the livid complexion by external applications, and of the evil humours of the milt, and of the fluidity [lubricity] of the humours of the milt.

XXXIX. A leechdom for a windy, swollen state of the milt, which cometh of eating apples, and nuts, and peas, and honey, and which puffeth up throughout the rope gut [large intestine/colon] and the intestines and the gastric juice.

XL. Leechdoms concerning the inflation and hardening of the blood in the milt.

XLI. Leechdoms against the hardness and soreness of the milt, and how one shall apply a freshly prepared pig's bladder [softened] with heated vinegar to ease the hardness, and against all the cramps [pains] of this affliction.

XLII. Leechdoms [for when] impure blood and evil humours are pressing upon the milt — then one shall let blood [perform phlebotomy] from the patient in the manner that this leech-book describes, and concerning the condition of the blood.

XLIII. Leechdoms [advising] what is to be consumed and what is to be avoided during the disease.

XLIV. A leechdom, again, that is very excellent for driving the evil out of the milt, and which is also good against swelling of the stomach; and inwardly it smooths [the gut], thins the belly humours, drives away bitter retching, and [treats] chest disease, and wasting [fever], and liver disease and disease of the milt, and wind of the belly — all these it lightens.

XLV. Leechdoms and a counter-drink [antidote/remedy potion] against swellings.

XLVI. Leechdoms against pain in the side with a slippery [pleuritic?] quality, and a wondrous token [sign] of whence it cometh and how the disease is to be expected, and how one shall treat it.

XLVII. Leechdoms for those who have wasting and the power of putrefaction, for the body wherein the heat is moderate or [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: milt
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'milte'; consistently used throughout to denote the spleen. The text notes it is on the left side, consistent with anatomical knowledge.

entities_mentioned.name: wamb
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English term for the belly or womb/stomach region; here used in the context of digestive complaints in children.

entities_mentioned.name: rope gut
entities_mentioned.notes: A vernacular anatomical term likely referring to the large intestine; possibly the colon.

entities_mentioned.name: splenetic laughter
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: risus sardonicus or involuntary laughter attributed to splenic disease
entities_mentioned.notes: A humoral concept that the spleen produced melancholic or aberrant laughter; not a precise modern clinical entity.

entities_mentioned.name: apples, nuts, peas, honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: dietary items (Malus domestica, various nuts, Pisum sativum, honey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as dietary causes of windy swelling of the milt/spleen; reflect humoral-dietary theory of flatulent foods.

entities_mentioned.name: pig's bladder with heated vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an external application to soften hardness of the spleen; a common early medieval therapeutic technique.

entities_mentioned.name: wiðdrinc (counter-drink)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'wiðdrinc' — a drink taken against a specific ailment; here prescribed for swellings.

results.translator_notes: This segment is primarily a table of contents (chapters XXXV–XLVII) from the Old English medical compilation known as the Lacnunga or a related leech-book (Læce Boc), followed by the corresponding Old English chapter headings. The Old English text is partly corrupt or damaged (possible OCR artifacts from a 19th-century edition, likely Cockayne's 'Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England', vol. 2). Several Old English passages contain likely scribal or OCR corruptions (e.g., 'jelmescan', 'criuptas', 'hritecetunje', 'sæstc') which have been rendered as best guesses in the translation. The final entry (XLVII) breaks off mid-sentence. The marginal note 'Gastric juice' appears to be an editorial annotation from Cockayne or the printer, not original to the Old English manuscript. The 'lif' in some passages likely refers to 'liver' (Old English: lifer). Blood-letting (phlebotomy) is described in chapter XLII as a therapeutic intervention.

results.title_inferred: On Disorders of the Stomach: Causes, Symptoms, and Dietary Treatment (Leechbook Book II)
results.original_text:
LEECE BOC.

jecynbon ond on magan ond lila[h]jinn ond on unmobe ond on unsemerpteccunge ond ungepitlico word; þe magas bið neali wiþe heortan ond þære geloðre ond geaborlenge þam brifsigene; of þam cumaþ þa abla swiðolu of þær magan inbyrjan ond on uplum searum pretan atterberende; þonne ga þa wætan þa ypelan weoðrað gegadepode on þone magan ond þær rixiaþ ond scearpunja nimaþ; swiðost on þam monnum þe habbaþ swiþe gewelne ond sarcijenne majan, swa þ he sume fomnunja swelleþ; ne magon abejian þa scarranjan scearpunja þoera ætena sætena; hwilum pyrmas of þam miþerran ond belcum gesecaþ þa neoþerran dælas to þam magan ond eac heortcoþe þyrceað ond angnyssa ond geswoþunga, swa þe hwilum sume men fram þara wyrma plitunje swellaþ ond forweoþrað; for þon þam mannum beah þ him mon on fruman þa mettas giefe þe celunje ond surunje magen habben; swa sindon seppla naes to swete ealles ac surmelsde ond peru ond persucas ond hlaf gedon on ceald water oþþe on wut be þære gelicunje þes magan þe þa ypelan metan sceorpende ond scearpan hæfð; wiþ beah eac on fruman þam þe þa heortcoþe ond sceorp cæpoliaþ selcra georrisc þ him mon lytlum þa mettas selle þa þe late melten; leax ond þa fixas þa þe late meltan, gose, mæple ond swines fet þa þe mægen wiþ habban þam yfelum wætan; ond þonne him wel sie þonne þicge he swetran mettas; ne biþ him nanwuht werse þonne he þa þicge þa þe late melten.
results.translation:
LEECHBOOK.

[Concerning] natural causes, and [those arising] in the stomach, and lassitude [lit. 'body-aching'], and in despondency [low spirits], and in immoderate excitement of speech; the stomach lies near to the heart and to the gullet [or: the pleasure of it], and [there is] an enlargement toward the diaphragm; from this arise the evil vapours [ill humours] of the stomach, bursting upward and in swelling eruptions bearing poison [noxious matter] upward; then when the evil humours are gathered together in the stomach and hold dominion there, and sharpnesses [acrid sensations] take hold — most severely in those men who have a very moist [humid] and sore stomach, so that it swells up at times — they cannot endure the biting sharpnesses of those foods that have been eaten. At times worms from the middle parts and from the bowels seek out the lower parts toward the stomach, and also cause heartburn [heartache], and oppression and faintings, so that sometimes some men swell up from the stirring of those worms and perish. Therefore it is beneficial for those persons that in the beginning one give them foods that can have a cooling and souring [astringent] quality; such are apples, not altogether too sweet but somewhat sour, and pears, and peaches, and bread put into cold water or into [warm] water according to the disposition of the stomach which has those evil sharp-biting and acrid qualities. It is also beneficial in the beginning for those who suffer heartburn and acrid eructations [sharp belching], that for each meal one give them sparingly of foods that digest slowly: salmon and those fish that digest slowly, goose, sorrel [or: maple-tree related preparation — possibly sorrel], and pig's trotters, which can withstand [resist being broken down by] the evil humour; and when he is well, then let him eat sweeter foods. It will not be at all worse for him when he eats those [foods] that digest slowly.

entities_mentioned.name: seppla (apples)
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended as cooling and mildly sour/astringent; not too sweet. Used for stomach disorders with excess evil humours.

entities_mentioned.name: peru (pears)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed among cooling, astringent foods for stomach treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: persucas (peaches)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed among cooling foods for stomach disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: leax (salmon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Editor's note confirms the OE interpreter rendered the Greek 'ιχθύς' forms as salmon. Recommended for slow digestion to resist evil humour.

entities_mentioned.name: gose (goose)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as a slow-digesting food beneficial against evil humours in the stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: mæple / mueple
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rumex acetosa (sorrel) — uncertain; possibly a fish type
entities_mentioned.notes: OCR and manuscript difficult; editor suggests reading 'mæcle' or similar. The Greek source lists various shellfish and sea creatures here which the OE translator omitted or replaced. May refer to sorrel plant used as a souring agent.

entities_mentioned.name: swines fet (pig's trotters)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as a slow-digesting food recommended to resist evil humour in the stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: pyrmas (worms)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a cause of swelling, heartburn, oppression, and fainting; worms ascending from the lower bowel toward the stomach. Pathological entity, not a drug.

entities_mentioned.name: heortcoþe (heartburn / heart-disease)
entities_mentioned.notes: OE term literally 'heart-suffering'; context strongly suggests gastric acidity or reflux rather than cardiac disease.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Cockayne's edition of the Old English Leechbook (Bald's Leechbook), Book II, folios 65b–66a. The source is a partial Old English adaptation of a Greek/Latin medical text (likely derived from Alexander of Tralles or a related Galenic tradition). The editor's footnotes indicate: (1) the OE construction is grammatically faulty in places; (2) the interpreter skips seven folio pages of the Greek source at one point; (3) Greek botanical terms including pomegranate seeds (ῥοὰς κόκκοι), nectarines, and dry/cold-flavoured grapes are omitted by the OE translator; (4) various Greek shellfish terms (βούλβα, στρύφνιον, ἄσταικοι, κτένια, κήρυκια) are omitted; (5) the interpreter diverges from the printed Greek text in recommending larger infrequent meals rather than frequent small ones. The OCR is heavily corrupted throughout; this translation is based on best reconstruction of the Old English text. Illegible or ambiguous words have been rendered by context. Some lines remain partially conjectural.

results.title_inferred: On wamb [stomach/gut] disease and treatment by bloodletting and herbal drinks
results.original_text: 2. Of wamb disease, or if the evil humours come from the wamb alone and do not overrun the whole body, that case shall be treated with healing meats alone. If moreover the evil humour from the wamb overrunneth the whole body, this shall be dealt with by means of the stronger remedies: at whiles one shall let him blood from a vein, if there seems to be too much of the blood and of the evil humour, and also give a wort drink; but he shall first be let blood and after that have the wort drink given him.
results.translation: 2. Concerning disease of the wamb [stomach/belly/gut]: if the evil humours [morbid bodily fluids] arise from the wamb alone and do not spread throughout the whole body, that condition shall be treated with healing foods alone. If, however, the evil humour from the wamb spreads throughout the entire body, this shall be addressed by means of stronger remedies: at times one shall let blood from a vein, if there appears to be an excess of blood and of the evil humour, and shall also administer a wort drink [herbal medicinal beverage]; but the patient shall first be bled and only afterwards have the wort drink administered to him.

entities_mentioned.name: wamb
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: stomach, abdomen, or gut (Old English term)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'wamb' (also 'womb' in broader archaic sense) refers to the belly, stomach, or intestines. Here used in the context of gastrointestinal disease or disorders of the digestive tract.

entities_mentioned.name: evil humours
entities_mentioned.notes: Reflects classical Galenic humoural theory, in which disease results from imbalance or corruption of the four bodily humours (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile). No single modern pathological equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: healing meats
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'mete' broadly means food, not exclusively flesh. 'Healing meats' likely refers to specially selected foods considered medicinally beneficial, akin to dietary therapy.

entities_mentioned.name: bloodletting (let him blood from a vein)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard humoural medical procedure to reduce excess blood or corrupt humours. Prescribed here when the evil humour has spread systemically. Condition: systemic spread of morbid humour from the gut. Preparation/method: venipuncture.

entities_mentioned.name: wort drink
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'wyrt' means plant or herb. A 'wort drink' is a liquid preparation made from one or more medicinal plants. The specific plant(s) are not named in this passage. Condition treated: wamb disease with systemic humoral spread. Preparation: liquid/drink form. Administered after bloodletting.

results.translator_notes: This passage is from a medieval Old English medical text (likely Cockayne's 'Leechdoms, Wortcunning and Starcraft of Early England'). The text reflects humoural medicine as filtered through Anglo-Saxon tradition. The therapeutic sequence prescribed—bloodletting before the herbal drink—is a notable clinical protocol worth flagging for comparative study. The term 'wamb' is retained in brackets in the translation as a period-specific anatomical term with broader semantic range than modern 'stomach.'

results.title_inferred: Remedies for a Sore and Swollen Maw (Stomach)
results.original_text:
1. For a sore and swollen maw; take oil, and put mastic, and dill, and southern wormwood into the oil, let it stand three nights, and arrange that the worts be sodden in the oil, then put that upon raw fresh wool, smear the maw therewith. Again, for that ilk; take old lard, triturate it in a treen mortar, mingle therewith the white of an egg, put on a cloth and lay on. For a sore maw, again; put the wort into warm oil, which hight fenugreek, and bunches of laurel flowers, and dill; smear the maw with that.

2. For a sore maw; put on a cloth juice of [watercress] and oil. Again, if the maw be swollen or distended; take of Welsh wine and fennel oil, each half, seethe wormwood tops, put into warm oil, smear therewith. Give him then flesh to eat of small creatures, of small birds, roasted and broiled, and manifold apple-kinds, fermented beverages against those that are raw [i.e., undigested], and sodden in vinegar and in grease and in wine well sharp. For a sore maw; rose leaves five, or seven, or nine, and pepper corns the same number, grind small and in warm water give to drink. Again for the same; take of field-wormwood twenty clustered corns and of cummin as much as thou canst take with three forward fingers, crumble then a bowl-full, pound in a mortar, add cold water two good bowls full, give then first the half to drink.

3. This is a fomentation to strengthen the maw and to comfort after purging or after a wortdrench [medicinal drink]; heat thinly sliced bread on old lard if thou have it; if it be summer, to wormwood seed-burrs seethe together, put on a cloth, anoint with oil, lay on the maw; if it be winter thou needest not put the wormwood to it.

[Section III.] For the swelling and soreness of the maw; if the person have the strength, let him blood, then smear him with the oil...
results.translation:
1. For a sore and swollen maw [stomach]: take oil, and put mastic, and dill, and southern wormwood into the oil; let it stand three nights, and arrange that the herbs be steeped [sodden] in the oil; then apply that upon raw fresh wool and smear the maw therewith. Again, for the same ailment: take old lard, triturate it in a wooden mortar, mix therewith the white of an egg, put it on a cloth and lay it on. For a sore maw, again: put into warm oil the herb called fenugreek, and bunches of laurel flowers, and dill; smear the maw with that.

2. For a sore maw: put upon a cloth the juice of [watercress] and oil, and lay it on. Again, if the maw be swollen or distended: take Welsh wine and fennel oil, each in equal half measure; seethe wormwood tops, put into warm oil, smear therewith. Then give the patient flesh to eat of small creatures, of small birds, roasted and broiled, and various kinds of apples; fermented [acid] drinks against those [foods] that lie undigested, and things sodden in vinegar and in grease and in well-sharp wine. For a sore maw: take five, or seven, or nine rose leaves, and an equal number of pepper corns, grind small, and give to drink in warm water. Again, for the same: take twenty clustered corns of field-wormwood and of cummin as much as thou canst take between three forward fingers; crumble a bowlful, pound in a mortar, add cold water to make two good bowls full, and then give the first half to drink.

3. This is a fomentation to strengthen the maw and to comfort it after a purge or after a medicinal drink [wortdrench]: heat thinly sliced bread in old lard if thou have it; if it be summer, seethe wormwood seed-burrs together with it, put on a cloth, anoint with oil, lay it on the maw; if it be winter, thou needest not add the wormwood.

[Section III.] For the swelling and soreness of the maw: if the person have the strength, let blood from him, then smear him with the oil [recipe follows].

entities_mentioned.name: mastic
entities_mentioned.notes: A resinous gum widely used in ancient and medieval medicine for digestive complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: dill
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a carminative and digestive herb.

entities_mentioned.name: southern wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from common wormwood (Artemisia absinthium); used for stomach ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: fenugreek
entities_mentioned.notes: Named explicitly in the text as 'hight fenugreek'; used for sore stomach as a poultice in warm oil.

entities_mentioned.name: laurel flowers (bunches of)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified as 'laurel flower bunches'; bay laurel is the most likely candidate in this context.

entities_mentioned.name: watercress (pelejcan / welisce)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'welisce' or similar term; context suggests watercress or a related cress; juice applied to cloth as poultice.

entities_mentioned.name: fennel oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with Welsh wine as a topical remedy for swollen stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood (tops)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tops (flower/seed clusters) steeped in warm oil for topical application to swollen maw.

entities_mentioned.name: rose leaves
entities_mentioned.notes: Five, seven, or nine leaves ground with pepper and taken in warm water for sore maw; the odd numbers may reflect a ritual/magical element.

entities_mentioned.name: pepper
entities_mentioned.notes: Ground with rose leaves and taken in warm water for sore maw.

entities_mentioned.name: field-wormwood (of puihnyrc / wormnyst)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'wuhnyrst' or similar; likely field wormwood, distinct from common wormwood; twenty clustered corns used.

entities_mentioned.name: cummin
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with field-wormwood, pounded in mortar, taken as a drink for stomach ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: old lard
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as vehicle for egg-white poultice and for heating bread as a fomentation.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Old English Leechbook II (Bald's Leechbook), a 10th-century Anglo-Saxon medical manuscript. The word 'maw' refers to the stomach. 'Wortdrench' (wyrðdrenc) is a medicinal herbal drink. 'Treen mortar' means a wooden mortar. Welsh wine ('Welisce win') likely refers to a wine imported from or associated with the Welsh/British regions or possibly a variety of grape wine. The odd numbers (5, 7, 9) for rose leaves may reflect numerological or magical tradition common in Anglo-Saxon medicine. The Old English text contains OCR artifacts and manuscript abbreviations that make precise rendering difficult in places; the folio references (fol. 67a, 67b) are preserved from the manuscript. Section numbering (1, 2, 3/III) follows the source. The phrase 'against those that are raw' likely refers to aiding digestion of uncooked or poorly digested foods.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Hard Swelling of the Maw and Loss of Appetite
results.original_text:
IV.

For a hard swelling of the maw; give the sick salt meats, and hares and boars flesh, roots of rue, and cresses, and sheer (clear) wine, and easily digested meats, and applications drawing out the hard swelling, and baths; work moist smearings, that is, lotions, of oil and wormwood, and of mastic and wine; bathe him, then smear with that, then overlay with ewes wool, and swathe up; take also mild apples, put them for the space of a night into wine and then seethe them; then sweeten the wash or infusion with virgin honey, and pepper it with twenty peppercorns; then give him in the morning a little bowl full or a spoon full of the thus wrought potion to drink.

A leechdom for swelling of the maw; one shall in the morning hours squeeze hard the mans feet and hands, and one shall bid him cry or sing very loud.

[Old English sections follow, treating nausea/disgust of food coming from the stomach, including remedies with wormwood, threebread, sharp wine, salt meats with vinegar, roasted senna, radish, hot-natured foods, good clear wine, and further remedies using southern cumin dried and ground with vinegar in a mortar, fennel seed, dill, pepper, rue leaves, strained honey, compounded with vinegar to the consistency of prepared electuary, placed in a glass vessel and taken with bread or any food desired, also taken at the lupine and vesper hours; noted to benefit not only loss of appetite but the whole body. Against loss of appetite of food: dissolve asafoetida in water, grind with vinegar, give to drink with flatbread.]
results.translation:
IV.

For a hard swelling of the stomach [maw]: give the sick person salt meats, and flesh of hare and boar, roots of rue, and cresses, and clear wine, and easily digested foods, and poultices drawing out the hard swelling, and baths. Prepare moist ointments, that is, lotions, of oil and wormwood, and of mastic and wine; bathe him, then anoint him with that, then lay ewes' wool over it and bind it up. Take also mild apples, steep them overnight in wine and then boil them; then sweeten the liquid or infusion with virgin honey, and add twenty peppercorns; then give him in the morning a small bowlful or a spoonful of this prepared potion to drink.

A remedy for swelling of the stomach: in the morning hours one shall squeeze the man's feet and hands firmly, and bid him cry out or sing very loudly.

[Section VI — Against nausea and disgust of food coming from the stomach and thereabouts:] Give him on an empty stomach [at night-fasting] wormwood or threebread [a medicinal bread preparation] steeped in sharp wine; give on an empty stomach; and after that give salt meats prepared with vinegar, and roasted senna and radish to eat, and all foods that have a hot quality, and give whey to drink; and take care that they suffer no irregularity; and give good heated and clear wine to drink at night fasting; and teach him to fast at night on honey; and seek for him a brook to ride along, and on a horse or whatever they can endure.

Again, against loss of appetite for food: take southern cumin, dry it with vinegar, then grind it in a mortar; and fennel seed, and dill — three spoonfuls — grind all together; add three spoonfuls of pepper and seven spoonfuls of rue leaves, and a pound of strained finest honey; mix all thoroughly together; then add vinegar as seems fitting so that it is made to the consistency of a preparation that is compounded to swallow; then put it in a glass vessel, and then with bread or with whatever food you wish teach him to take it and use it — though you may with a spoon take the juice that helps; use it at the lupine hour as well as at the vesper hour. This is not good against loss of appetite for the stomach alone, but for the whole body it is beneficial.

Against loss of appetite for food: dissolve asafoetida in water with pressure [by rubbing]; grind with vinegar; give to drink with flatbread. This [remedy follows].

entities_mentioned.name: salt meats
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed as dietary treatment for hard stomach swelling.

entities_mentioned.name: hares and boars flesh
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: hare (Lepus spp.) and wild boar (Sus scrofa)
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended as easily digested or stimulating meats.

entities_mentioned.name: rue (roots of)
entities_mentioned.notes: Roots used; rue was a common medicinal herb in Anglo-Saxon medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: cresses
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nasturtium officinale (watercress) or Lepidium sativum (garden cress)
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely watercress or garden cress; used as dietary remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both as an external lotion (with oil) and as an internal remedy at night-fasting.

entities_mentioned.name: mastic
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a lotion with wine for external application over the stomach swelling.

entities_mentioned.name: ewes wool
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an overlying dressing to retain the poultice/lotion.

entities_mentioned.name: mild apples
entities_mentioned.notes: Steeped overnight in wine, boiled, then sweetened with honey and peppered.

entities_mentioned.name: virgin honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to sweeten the apple-wine infusion and separately as a night-fasting remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: pepper
entities_mentioned.notes: Twenty peppercorns added to the apple-wine potion; also three spoonfuls in the compound electuary.

entities_mentioned.name: threebread (þreobread)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a medicinal bread or a compound preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon term; exact nature unclear, possibly bread compounded with herbs, given at night-fasting in sharp wine.

entities_mentioned.name: senna (roasted)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'senep' sometimes means mustard; however contextual use as a digestive/laxative food suggests senna or mustard.

entities_mentioned.name: radish
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed among hot-natured foods recommended for loss of appetite.

entities_mentioned.name: whey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquid byproduct of cheese-making (bovine milk whey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Given as a drink alongside hot-natured foods.

entities_mentioned.name: southern cumin
entities_mentioned.notes: Dried with vinegar and ground as part of compound electuary for loss of appetite.

entities_mentioned.name: fennel seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Three spoonfuls used in the compound electuary.

entities_mentioned.name: dill
entities_mentioned.notes: Three spoonfuls used in the compound electuary.

entities_mentioned.name: rue leaves
entities_mentioned.notes: Seven spoonfuls of leaves used in the compound electuary.

entities_mentioned.name: strained honey
entities_mentioned.notes: One pound used as the base binder for the electuary.

entities_mentioned.name: vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used throughout as a solvent, drying agent, and to adjust electuary consistency.

entities_mentioned.name: asafoetida (bræwce / droahta?)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Old English 'breowje broptum' dissolved in water and ground with vinegar; interpretation as asafoetida is plausible given the context; editor's footnote references Greek term (possibly ἡγεμονή or similar).

entities_mentioned.name: flatbread
entities_mentioned.notes: Given with the asafoetida-vinegar preparation.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Cockayne's edition of the Old English medical compendium 'Læce Boc' (Leech Book), Volume 2, Chapter IV and continuing into Chapter VI. The Latin footnote reference ('ἡγεμονή, I suppose') is the editor Cockayne's gloss, attempting to identify a Greek source term. The Old English sections (in runic-approximated typeface) have been translated from the body of the text and paraphrased where OCR rendering is highly degraded. 'Maw' is the period term for stomach. 'Threebread' (þreobread) remains of uncertain identification. 'Southern cumin' refers to Cuminum cyminum as distinct from caraway. The passage prescribing crying or loud singing as a remedy likely reflects a mechanical theory of loosening internal blockages. The electuary described in Section VI is a sophisticated polypharmaceutical preparation consistent with late classical/early medieval compound medicine.
