results.title_inferred: Leech Book II: Remedies for a Deadened or Troubled Stomach (Maw)
results.original_text:
1548. [For the maw / stomach.] Give him also after his nights fast [nihtnescigum] this: take vinegar mingled with somewhat of gladden [gladene / iris] and of long pepper ten corns or clusters, and mustard; mingle all together and triturate; give after a nights fasting one spoon measure. Then consider thou, notwithstanding, that all the aforenamed leechdoms and the after-written ones shall not be done at one too long season, but must have space and rest between them, whilom two days, whilom three; and when one lets him blood on a vein, on those days let none other leechdom be done to him, except about five days later or more. For a stomach troubled with hiccup [forsogenum magan] or puffed up, take beeves flesh sodden in vinegar and with oil, prepared with salt, and dill, and porrum [leek]; let the sick diet on that for seven days; then that relieves the labouring maw. These are tokens of a deadened maw: what he taketh, that melteth or digesteth not, but the undigested meat passes through the womb [bowels] outward.

[Section VIII.]
For soreness and aversion [unlust] of the maw, of him who can be cured neither with meat nor with drink, and [for] bitter hiccuping: Take centaurian [centaury], that is of a wild sort, some call it hyrde wyrt [shepherd's herb], some earth-gall [earthgall]; scrape a pound [of it].
results.translation:
1548. [Remedy for the stomach / maw.] Give him also after his night's fast the following: take vinegar mingled with some gladden [iris root] and ten corns or clusters of long pepper, and mustard; mingle all together and triturate; give him, after a night's fasting, a spoon's measure. Bear in mind, however, that all the above-named remedies and the ones written hereafter should not be carried out continuously for too long a period at once, but must have intervals of space and rest between them—sometimes two days, sometimes three. And when one lets him blood from a vein, on those days no other remedy should be applied to him, except about five days later or more.

For a stomach troubled with hiccup or puffed up: take beef-flesh boiled in vinegar and prepared with oil and salt, and dill, and leek; let the sick man diet on that for seven days; that then relieves the labouring stomach. These are the signs of a deadened [paralysed] stomach: what he takes in neither melts [digests] nor is retained, but the undigested food passes downward through the bowels outward.

[Section VIII.]
For soreness and loathing of the stomach in one who can be cured neither with food nor with drink, and for bitter hiccuping: Take centaury—the wild sort; some call it hyrde wyrt [shepherd's herb], some call it earthgall [earth-bile]; scrape a pound of it.

entities_mentioned.name: gladden (gladene)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yellow flag iris / Iris pseudacorus or Iris foetidissima (gladdon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'gladene' commonly refers to the gladdon or flag iris; the root was used medicinally.

entities_mentioned.name: long pepper (langer pipor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from common black pepper; used as a digestive stimulant in medieval European and Anglo-Saxon medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: mustard (senep)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used internally as a digestive stimulant and externally as a rubefacient at the bath.

entities_mentioned.name: vinegar (eceb / aeceb)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard vehicle for medicinal preparations in Leech Book recipes.

entities_mentioned.name: dill (bile)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'bile'; used here as a flavoring and carminative in the dietary preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: porrum / leek (por)
entities_mentioned.notes: Leeks appear frequently in Anglo-Saxon medicinal dietaries.

entities_mentioned.name: beeves flesh (hryðeren flæsc)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed as a dietary food boiled in vinegar with oil and salt for stomach complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: centaurian / centaury (centaurian)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centaurium erythraea (common centaury) or possibly Centaurium minus
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'hyrde wyrt' (shepherd's herb) and 'eorð-gealla' (earthgall) in Old English; a bitter tonic plant used for digestive complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: earthgall (eorðgealla)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English synonym for centaury-type bitter herbs; 'earth-bile' refers to the plant's extreme bitterness.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Old English Leech Book II (Bald's Leechbook or Leechbook III corpus), a 10th-century Anglo-Saxon medical compilation. The source text is heavily abbreviated and shows significant OCR degradation of the Old English characters (thorn, wynn, eth rendered as 'p', 'f', etc.). The facing English gloss printed alongside (from Cockayne's 1864–1866 edition) has been used to verify the translation where the Old English was damaged beyond recovery. 'Maw' is the period term for stomach. 'Nihtnescigum' / 'nihtnescig' means 'night-fasting' (i.e., before breakfast). 'Forsogenum magan' is rendered as 'hicket' (hiccup) in Cockayne's gloss but more literally means 'choked' or 'constricted' stomach. Bloodletting spacing instructions are notable: the text cautions a minimum five-day gap between phlebotomy and other remedies. Section VIII begins a new recipe for stomach soreness and loathing using centaury.
results._label: cockayne_v2::1548.  fmeppe.     Sele  hmi  eac  neahcnepcijum

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Sore of Maw (Stomach) and Inward Wound of the Maw
results.original_text:
LEECH BOOK. II. 189

wort, some earth gall, rub small a pound of it, and smoke; apply thereto four bowls full of hot water; give it to the sick to drink for three days after his nights fasting. Again, take the upper part of the red nettle, while having seed, wash it clean, and work it up to sup. Again, administer to drink juice of green marche triturated and wrung out, and in the same wise, give him to drink juice of horehound. Again, for sore of maw; rue and mint, dill, dwarf dwosle, agrimony, some call it garcliff, and cress, pound them all in wine or in ale, give of this each day to drink.

ix.

For an inward wound of the maw; take goats milk just when it is milked, administer to be drunk. Some drink for sore of maw warm ewe milk, some the best oil warmed, some mingle that with the goats milk till they spew, that they may spew the more easily.
results.translation:
LEECH BOOK. II. 189

wort, some earth gall — rub small [i.e., grind fine] a pound of it, and smoke [fumigate]; apply thereto four bowls full of hot water; give it to the sick to drink for three days after his nightly fasting. Again, take the upper part of the red nettle while it is in seed, wash it clean, and work it up into a soup [or decoction]. Again, administer to drink the juice of green marche [wild celery or marsh parsley], triturated and wrung out; and in the same manner give him to drink juice of horehound. Again, for soreness of the maw [stomach]: rue and mint, dill, dwarf dwosle [dwarf elder / danewort], agrimony (some call it garcliff), and cress — pound them all in wine or in ale, and give of this to drink each day.

ix.

For an inward wound of the maw: take goat's milk just as it is milked, and administer it to be drunk. Some drink warm ewe's milk for soreness of the maw; some [drink] the best oil warmed; some mingle that [oil] with the goat's milk until they vomit, so that they may vomit the more easily.

entities_mentioned.name: earth gall
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centaurium erythraea (common centaury) or Gentiana amarella (felwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon 'eorþgalla' is typically identified as centaury or a related bitter herb.

entities_mentioned.name: red nettle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urtica dioica (stinging nettle) or Lamium purpureum (red dead-nettle)
entities_mentioned.notes: The qualifier 'while having seed' suggests a specific harvesting stage. Both candidates are plausible in the Leech Book context.

entities_mentioned.name: marche (green marche)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens (wild celery / smallage) or Apium nodiflorum (fool's watercress)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'merece' typically refers to wild celery or marsh parsley.

entities_mentioned.name: horehound
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-attested Anglo-Saxon medicinal herb for digestive and respiratory complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: rue
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard classical and medieval herb for digestive ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: dwarf dwosle
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'dwosle' is debated; sometimes identified as pennyroyal (Mentha pulegium), but 'dwarf dwosle' more likely points to dwarf elder.

entities_mentioned.name: agrimony (also called garcliff)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'garclife' is the standard term for agrimony in the Leech Books.

entities_mentioned.name: cress
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lepidium sativum or Nasturtium officinale (garden cress or watercress)

entities_mentioned.name: goat's milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Used fresh and warm as a therapeutic drink for internal stomach wounds.

entities_mentioned.name: ewe's milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Warm sheep's milk administered for stomach soreness.

entities_mentioned.name: oil (best oil warmed)
entities_mentioned.notes: In Anglo-Saxon medical texts 'best oil' often refers to olive oil, though other oils were also used.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Leech Book of Bald (Bald's Leechbook), Book II, chapter viii–ix, as edited and translated by Cockayne (Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England, Vol. II). The OCR is heavily corrupted in the header and opening lines; 'ral>' is read as 'rub', 'poun<l' as 'pound', 'HiiokJi.' as 'smoke' (fumigation), and '*  ^"''' as a corrupted page-reference marker. 'Sore of maw' consistently refers to stomach ailment or gastric disorder. The emetic use of oil mixed with goat's milk (section ix) is notable as an intentional purgative/emetic therapy for internal wounds of the stomach. 'Dwosle' remains a contested identification in Anglo-Saxon botanical scholarship.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Nausea, Stomach Ailments, Bloating, and Vomiting
results.original_text:
X.
For nausea and to heat the maw; water sodden on wormwood and on dill, down to the third part, give the man that to drink; it warmeth and hardeneth the maw.

XI.
For puffing up and blowing of the maw; overpour roots of fennel and marehe with clear old wine, and of that give the sick to drink after his nights fast two little bowls full. For a windy puffing up of the maw, to warm the maw, rue and dill, mint and marehe; seethe bundles of them separate in three jugs full of water, and continue seething so that there be only one cup; then administer the water to be drunk.

XII.
For spewing, and in case that a man's meat will not keep down; take sinfulle, rub it fine into sharp wine, give the man a bowl full to drink after evening work. Take, for that ilk, two parts of juice of fennel, one of honey, seethe or boil down till the mixture have the thickness of honey, then give after a night's fast a spoon measure full; that restraineth nausea, that bettereth the lungs, that healeth the liver. For mickle spewing, and in case a man may keep in no meat; take one ounce of dill seed, four [parts] of pepper, three of cumin, rub very fine; then put into water in which mint has been sodden and sour apples or verjuice, upper measure [i.e., heaped], if the man be not in a warm season, with wine, and give to drink when he will not go to fast; and lay outwardly on the maw sodden wood-apple and crumbs of bread and such-like poultices.

XIII.
For warmth/heating of the maw: there is a certain herb kind called lenticulae [lentils], a hundred and twenty times. Again, three spoon measures of sharp sheep's vinegar when he will sleep in the evening.

XIV.
For all ailments of the maw; take the outer roots of fennel, so that there be the most of them; take off from the fennel so much as may be one and a half pound; pour [over them]...
results.translation:
X.
For nausea and to warm the stomach: boil water with wormwood and dill down to one-third its original volume; give this liquid to the man to drink. It warms and strengthens the stomach.

XI.
For bloating and flatulent distension of the stomach: steep roots of fennel and marsh-mallow [marehe] in clear, aged wine, and give the sick person two small bowlfuls to drink after his overnight fast. For a windy bloating of the stomach, to warm the stomach: take rue, dill, mint, and marsh-mallow; boil bundles of each separately in three jugs of water, continuing to boil until only one cup of liquid remains; then administer this water as a drink.

XII.
For vomiting, and when a man's food will not stay down: take houseleek [sinfulle], rub it fine into sharp [acidic] wine, and give the man a bowlful to drink after the evening's labor. For the same complaint: take two parts of fennel juice and one part of honey; boil these down until the mixture reaches the consistency of honey; then give a spoonful after an overnight fast. This restrains nausea, improves the lungs, and heals the liver. For excessive vomiting, when a man cannot retain any food: take one ounce of dill seed, four [parts] of pepper, three of cumin; grind very fine; then put into water in which mint has been boiled, along with sour apples or verjuice, heaped measure — if the man is not in a warm season [i.e., not feverish], add wine — and give to drink when he is about to break his fast. Also apply externally to the stomach: boiled crab-apple [wood-apple], bread crumbs, and similar poultices.

XIII.
For heating/fever of the stomach: there is an herb called lenticulae [lentils], to be used one hundred and twenty times [i.e., as a frequently repeated remedy]. Also, three spoonfuls of sharp sheep's vinegar taken in the evening when he wishes to sleep.

XIV.
For all ailments of the stomach: take the outer roots of fennel, the most plentiful available; remove from the fennel plant so much as amounts to one and a half pounds; pour [over them — text continues]...

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in decoction with dill for nausea and stomach-warming.

entities_mentioned.name: dill
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in multiple preparations: decoction for nausea, juice reduced with honey for vomiting, and powdered seed for excessive vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: fennel (roots and juice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Roots steeped in old wine for bloating; juice reduced with honey for vomiting; outer roots used for general stomach ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: marehe
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'mersc-mealwe' or similar; used with fennel in wine infusion and with rue, dill, and mint in decoction for stomach bloating.

entities_mentioned.name: rue
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in decoction with dill, mint, and marsh-mallow for flatulent stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: mint
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in decoction for bloating and as a water base for the anti-emetic powder preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: sinfulle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sempervivum tectorum (houseleek) or possibly Sedum acre (stonecrop)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'sinfulle'; traditionally identified as houseleek. Rubbed into sharp wine for vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a thickening agent with fennel juice; the mixture was boiled to honey consistency and given for vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: pepper
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of the powdered anti-emetic compound for excessive vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: cumin
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of the powdered anti-emetic compound for excessive vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: sour apples / verjuice
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malus spp. (sour apples); verjuice from unripe grapes or apples
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as acidic liquid base for the anti-emetic powder; 'vingeard twig ufeweard' may refer to verjuice from vine shoots.

entities_mentioned.name: wood-apple (pudu aeppel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled and applied as a poultice to the stomach externally.

entities_mentioned.name: lenticulae
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin term retained in Old English text; described as an herb used repeatedly (120 times) for stomach heating/fever. The '120 times' reference is obscure and may indicate dosage repetition or a scribal note.

entities_mentioned.name: sheep's vinegar (scearpes eceð)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Scearpes eceð' literally 'sharp vinegar'; the adjective 'scearp' may not mean sheep-derived but rather 'sharp/strong.' Three spoonfuls taken at bedtime for stomach fever.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Maw' is the period term for stomach; retained in translation with modern equivalent provided. 2. 'Marehe' is likely Old English for marsh-mallow (Althaea officinalis), though some editors suggest other marsh plants. 3. 'Sinfulle' is a notoriously debated Old English plant name; houseleek (Sempervivum tectorum) is the standard scholarly identification. 4. The reference in chapter XIII to using lenticulae 'one hundred and twenty times' is unclear; it may be a garbled dosage instruction or a scribal interpolation. 5. The method attributed in the footnote to Alexander Trallianus (lib. vii, cap. 9) confirms the classical Greek medical lineage of these recipes. 6. Section XIV is incomplete as the source text is cut off mid-sentence. 7. OCR errors in the Old English interlinear text (e.g., 'pyle' for 'wile', 'jentm' for 'genim') have been silently normalized in the original_text field where they represent the Latin-script printed English translation rather than the manuscript Old English.

results.title_inferred: Old English remedies for stomach ailments: gastric burning, bloating, and heat of the stomach (Leechbook II, Chapters XIV–XVI)
results.original_text:
1556. Properly περιφαρισμός is of the wamb, or venter, not of the maw; and Aretaeus says as much, Chron. lib. ii., cap. 6. But other authors have the same expression as Alex. Trail.; for instance Caelius Aurelianus, Chron. lib. iii., cap. 2.

[Ch. XIV.]
For that [ailment] pour on [the] open wound/sore: prepare it hot; pound [ingredients] finely with vinegar and seethe; then take out the roots somewhat in the vinegar and wring out of the vinegar cleanly; put then in the vinegar honey with vinegar; put then away [a] good portion thereof into [the] weight of an ounce, more and other such of white pepper and of ameos [Ammi] and of southern wort [southernwood], other [such] portion; mix these lighter ones all together, and then give him three spoonfuls. Do this against stomach burning and thirst. Afterwards mix with the lesser oil [olive oil] and give to drink; that [benefits] the thirsty.

[Ch. XV.]
For that stomach ailment when the womb/belly mounts with bitter belching, or he belches, or there seethes in him in the stomach: take of pepper such as one coin's weight; of hyssop seed such as four coins' weight; other such of cumin; grind all and give in wine [a] spoonful's measure when he will go to sleep. The distension of the maw and the swelling thus comes from the black humors; but take then springs [i.e., cold-spring water or sponges], put in sharp vinegar, season strongly, lay over the stomach when it is such. After that, if there be need, lay other cloths on [in] stronger [preparation] and remove, as is said: one mixed with honey and then make [as] the physicians know [how].

[Ch. XVI.]
This is a recipe for the hot stomach's immoderate leanness/wasting and the over-cooling thereof; [for] the hot stomach's im-[moderation]...

[Scholarly note from editor:] a pound and a half, then pour on of vinegar as much as be a sextarius and a half, then let these stand thus together for three nights; after that seethe the roots somewhat in the vinegar, and [...]
results.translation:
1556. [Editorial note:] Properly, περιφαρισμός [peripharismos] pertains to the womb [venter/abdomen], not to the stomach [maw]; and Aretaeus says as much, Chronic Diseases, book ii, chapter 6. But other authors use the same expression as Alexander of Tralles; for instance Caelius Aurelianus, Chronic Diseases, book iii, chapter 2.

[Chapter XIV.]
For this [condition, of stomach burning and thirst]: pour [the preparation] onto the open [wound/affected area]; prepare it hot; pound [the ingredients] finely with vinegar, and then take the roots out of the vinegar and wring them out of the vinegar cleanly; then put into the vinegar honey with vinegar; then put away a good portion thereof equal in weight to an ounce, and more, and an equal portion of white pepper and of ameos [bishop's weed / Ammi majus] and of southern wort [southernwood / Artemisia abrotanum], another equal part; mix all these lighter [ingredients] together, and then give him three spoonfuls. Do this for stomach burning and thirst. Afterwards mix with the lesser [i.e., olive] oil and give to drink; this aids the thirsty.

[Chapter XV.]
For that stomach complaint when the belly swells with bitter belching, or he belches, or there is a seething in him in the stomach: take pepper of the weight of one coin; hyssop seed of the weight of four coins; an equal amount of cumin; grind all together and give in wine [by] a spoonful's measure when he wishes to go to sleep. The distension of the stomach and the swelling thus comes from the black humors [black bile]; but take then sponges [or: springs], put them in sharp vinegar, season strongly, and lay them over the stomach when it is in such a state. After that, if there be need, lay other cloths on [the area] in a stronger [preparation] and remove them, as is customary: one [cloth] mixed with honey; and then treat as physicians know how.

[Chapter XVI.]
This is a remedy for the immoderate wasting caused by the hot stomach [i.e., excessive heat of the stomach], and [for] its over-cooling; [for] the hot stomach's im-[moderate condition...]

[Editor's note, continued:] a pound and a half, then pour on of vinegar as much as a sextarius and a half, then let these stand together thus for three nights; after that seethe the roots somewhat in the vinegar, and [text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: περιφαρισμός (peripharismos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek medical term discussed by the editor; said to refer to the venter/abdomen rather than the stomach (maw). Cited from Aretaeus and Caelius Aurelianus.

entities_mentioned.name: Aretaeus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aretaeus of Cappadocia, Greek physician, c. 1st–2nd century CE
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as Chron. lib. ii, cap. 6 (Chronic Diseases, Book II, Chapter 6).

entities_mentioned.name: Alexander of Tralles (Alex. Trail.)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alexander of Tralles, Greek physician, c. 525–605 CE
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as lib. vii, cap. 8, p. 110. Source text for the dietary/therapeutic regimen.

entities_mentioned.name: Caelius Aurelianus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caelius Aurelianus, Latin medical writer, c. 5th century CE
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as Chron. lib. iii, cap. 2 (Chronic Diseases, Book III, Chapter 2).

entities_mentioned.name: pipor (pepper)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Piper nigrum (black pepper) or Piper longum (long pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for stomach ailments; dose of one coin's weight (approx. one scruple or denarius weight).

entities_mentioned.name: bile sæd (hyssop seed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for stomach bloating and belching; dose of four coins' weight.

entities_mentioned.name: cymen (cumin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ground and combined with pepper and hyssop; given in wine before sleep for stomach ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: ameos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ammi majus or Ammi visnaga (bishop's weed / toothpick weed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed among herbs for stomach burning; Old English 'ameos' is a Latinized Greek form.

entities_mentioned.name: suþerne wyrt (southern wort / southernwood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with white pepper and ameos for stomach burning.

entities_mentioned.name: hwit pipor (white pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Specified as 'white pepper' in the recipe for stomach burning.

entities_mentioned.name: eceb (vinegar)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a solvent and preparation medium throughout; also applied externally on cloths/sponges to the stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: hunig (honey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed with vinegar for internal remedy; also used on cloths applied externally.

entities_mentioned.name: ele (oil)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to as 'the lesser oil'; mixed with preparation and given to drink.

entities_mentioned.name: blace omum (black humors)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Black bile (atra bilis) in humoral medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as the cause of gastric distension and swelling in Chapter XV.

entities_mentioned.name: spungean (sponges)
entities_mentioned.notes: Soaked in vinegar and applied externally over the stomach; from Hellenic medical practice per the editor's footnote.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Cockayne's edition of the Old English Leechbooks, Volume II, folio 71b–75a, comprising Chapters XIV–XVI of Leechbook II. The Old English text is heavily abbreviated and contracted, with significant OCR degradation. The editor's footnotes identify the source of the regimen as Alexander of Tralles, lib. vii, cap. 8, and note that 'spungean' derives from the Greek. The Greek term θεραπεία τῆς διὰ θερμὴν ἀσθενούσης δυνάμεως ('treatment of [a condition] weakened by heat') is cited as a chapter heading in the source. The editor also notes that 'ungemetfæstlice' and 'ungemetsæstlic' are opposites of καθεκτικός, correcting Somner's interpretation. The trailing text about 'a pound and a half ... sextarius and a half ... three nights ... seethe the roots in the vinegar' appears to be a continuation of a recipe running across the page break, likely belonging to an adjacent chapter. Chapter XVI is only partially preserved in this segment.

results.title_inferred: Leech Book II: On Disorders of the Maw (Stomach) — Symptoms, Diet, and Remedies for Hot and Cold Stomach Ailments
results.original_text:
1556. I cap. 5; p. 105, ed. 1548; p. 319, ed.

Head [S]iege. 1556, for a few lines only.

Gr. εἰς ἄκρατον, dipped in wine unmixed with water, (as if brandy). Read fpripban?

LEECH BOOK II.

Maw are tokens, when it is vexed with inflammations, Book II. thirst is incident to the man, and oppression, and swoonings, and vacillation of mind, and loss of appetite, and nausea. It is beneficial for him that he should eat bread in cold water or in vinegar, and eggs very hard boiled or roasted, (at nine o'clock in the morning,) and worts, and lactucas, that is lettuces, and mallow, and hens flesh not much sodden, and the extremest parts of the limbs of goose, that is giblets, and fishes which have hard flesh, and periwinkles, and oysters, and others; various sorts of peas, and mild apples, and a bath of sweet fresh waters shall be wrought; a hot bath will not suit him. Tokens of the overcold maw, that the men feel no thirst nor burning heat in the maw, nor is there any warm symptom incident upon them. But they yearn for meats more strongly than is proper, and if in their inwards there lodges any cold humour, then they spew up the filth and are not able to retain the meats which they swallow; and after the spewing soon they pray that somewhat be given them to eat. Those men thou shalt smear with the oil on which wormwood has been sodden. And the thick coagulated and the viscid humours in the maw, and the chilled humours, and the intractable thick viscid foulness, thou shalt warm and thin with the afore named leechdoms. Work then for the sick man a wort drink of the rind of the root of fennel, and let it be very tender, and such that it may weigh six ounces, and one sextarius of vinegar, and three ounces of aloes; then seethe the fennel in the vinegar till it be well sodden, then wring the worts off the vinegar, then add to the vinegar a pound of clean honey, then seethe these together, till it be as thick as honey, then shed the aloes into it, well rubbed up, and give three spoon measures with water; that is good for heart ache and for epilepsy.
results.translation:
LEECH BOOK II [an Old English medical compilation].

[Chapter 5: On Ailments of the Maw (Stomach)]

[Editorial note: Reference to edition of 1556, cap. 5; p. 105, ed. 1548; p. 319, ed. (1556). The heading 'Siege' appears in the 1556 edition for a few lines only. The Greek phrase εἰς ἄκρατον means 'dipped in wine unmixed with water' (i.e., as if in brandy). Read [fpripban?].]

The signs [tokens] that the maw [stomach] is afflicted with inflammations are: thirst comes upon the man, and oppression, and swoonings [fainting], and vacillation [wandering] of mind, and loss of appetite, and nausea. It is beneficial for him that he should eat bread soaked in cold water or in vinegar, and eggs very hard-boiled or roasted (at the third hour [nine o'clock in the morning]), and pot-herbs [worts], and lactucas — that is, lettuces — and mallow, and hen's flesh not much boiled [sodden], and the outermost parts of the limbs of goose — that is, giblets — and fishes with firm flesh, and periwinkles, and oysters, and others; various sorts of peas, and mild apples. A bath of sweet fresh water shall be prepared; a hot bath will not suit him.

The signs of the over-cold maw: the men feel no thirst nor burning heat in the stomach, nor is there any warm symptom upon them. But they crave food more strongly than is proper, and if in their inward parts there lodges any cold humour, then they vomit up foul matter and are unable to retain the food they have swallowed; and soon after vomiting they beg that something be given them to eat.

Those men you shall anoint with the oil in which wormwood has been boiled. The thick, coagulated, and viscid [sticky] humours in the stomach, the chilled humours, and the intractable thick viscid foulness, you shall warm and thin with the aforementioned leechdoms [remedies].

Then prepare for the sick man a medicinal herb-drink [wort drink] made from the bark of the root of fennel — let it be very fresh and tender, weighing six ounces — and one sextarius [approximately one pint] of vinegar, and three ounces of aloes. Then boil the fennel in the vinegar until it is thoroughly cooked; then strain the herbs from the vinegar; then add to the vinegar a pound of pure honey; then boil these together until the mixture is as thick as honey; then stir in the aloes, well ground [rubbed up], and administer three spoonfuls with water. This is good for heartache [stomach pain / cardiac ailment] and for epilepsy.

entities_mentioned.name: lactucas / lettuces
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly glossed in the text: 'lactucas, that is lettuces.' Recommended as food for inflamed stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: mallow
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malva sylvestris or related Malva spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended as dietary food for hot/inflamed stomach condition.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an oil preparation (wormwood boiled in oil) applied topically by anointing for the over-cold stomach; condition: cold humour in the maw causing vomiting and excessive appetite.

entities_mentioned.name: fennel (rind of the root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a wort drink: 6 oz of fennel root bark boiled in vinegar, then combined with honey and aloes. Indicated for heartache and epilepsy.

entities_mentioned.name: aloes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera / Aloe spp. (dried resinous extract)
entities_mentioned.notes: 3 oz of aloes, ground and added to the fennel-vinegar-honey preparation. Indicated for heartache and epilepsy.

entities_mentioned.name: honey
entities_mentioned.notes: 1 pound of clean (pure) honey combined with vinegar and aloes as a thickening and medicinal agent.

entities_mentioned.name: periwinkles
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Littorina spp. or similar edible marine gastropods
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as dietary food recommended for inflamed stomach condition.

entities_mentioned.name: wort drink (medicinal herb drink)
entities_mentioned.notes: General Old English term for a medicinal drink made from plant materials; here specifically fennel root bark decocted in vinegar with honey and aloes.

results.translator_notes: This passage is from the Old English Leech Book (Bald's Leechbook or related compilation), Book II, Chapter 5, preserved in multiple editions (1548, 1556). The editorial apparatus refers to variant readings and Greek source text (εἰς ἄκρατον = 'in unmixed wine'). The word 'maw' throughout translates the OE term for stomach. 'Sodden' means boiled. 'Leechdom' is the OE term for remedy or medicament. 'Sextarius' is a Roman liquid measure, approximately one pint. 'Heart ache' may refer to epigastric/stomach pain (as the heart and stomach were not always clearly distinguished in medieval medicine) or to cardiac pain; 'epilepsy' renders the OE term for falling sickness. The preparation is a decocted electuary-like syrup. The page break 'N 2' and page number '196' are printer's marks from the modern edition and have been excluded from the translation proper. The reading '[fpripban?]' in the editorial note is an uncertain Old English word and has been left with the query mark as in the original.

results.title_inferred: On Excessive Appetite, Extreme Hunger, Digestion, and Liver Diseases
results.original_text:
2. Of the overmickle appetite, when from the same Book II. cold disease of the maw it cometh that the overmickle appetite and greediness ariseth from the foul humour, which cometh from the maw, and the sick are spewing, and, as it were a hound, again soon seek the meats: to them thou shalt give clean and clear wine, and red, much heated; let it not be too sharp; nor let the meat be too sharp, nor too sour, which thou mayst give them, but smooth and fat. If extreme hunger cometh from immoderate heat and tenderness of the maw, so that they are soon in a swoon, if they have not the meat; then, for extreme hunger thou shalt soon treat the man; bind the extremities of his limbs with ligatures, pull his locks for him, and wring his ears, and twitch his whisker, when he is better, give him soon some bread broken in wine, before he take other meats. Give him the meats which are not too soon digested. Beeves flesh, and goats, and harts digests late: bucks is worst, and rams, and bulls, and those of four footed neat which are very old, and fowls which have hard flesh; peacock, swan, duck. To those that have a cold wamb thou shalt give well digesting meats, shell fishes, and young of culvers, hens flesh, and gooses wings; they are the better as they are fatter and fresher. The extremities of the limbs of swine [i.e., pigs' trotters] are easy of digestion, and young beeves, and kids: and sweet wine digests better than the rough.

XVII. For all liver diseases, and of its nature, and increment, and of the six things which work the liver pain, and curing of all these, and plain tokens, either by the urine, or by the loss of appetite, or by the hue of the [skin].

[Old English text:] V nepefeoþan þo htas pp lagppan helc þa lenbenbpaðan · þo is blodes cimber · and blodes hus · and foscor · þonne þara metta meltung biþ and þynnes þa becumaþ on þæb hyger þonne wendaþ hie hiora hiw and cegiaþ on blod · and þa unyefernessa þe þær beoþ hio awyrrþ ut and þæt claene blod gesamnaþ and þurh feower æðra swiðost onsenc to þære heortan and eac geond ealne þone lichoman oþ þa ytmestan limo. Be sex þingum þe þone liferseoc wyrceaþ: ærest geswel þæt is aþundenes þære hyerre; oþer is þies geswelles coberseung; þridde is wund þære lifre; feorþe is welmes hæto mid geselnysse and mid sare geswelle; sixt [...]
results.translation:
2. On excessive appetite [overmickle appetite]: When, from the same cold disease of the stomach [maw], it comes about that excessive appetite and greediness arise from the foul humour [corrupt bodily fluid] coming from the stomach, and the sick are vomiting and, like a dog, soon seek food again — to such patients you shall give clean, clear wine, red and well heated; let it not be too sharp [acidic]; nor let the food you give them be too sharp or too sour, but smooth and fatty. If extreme hunger comes from immoderate heat and tenderness of the stomach, such that the patient soon falls into a swoon if he lacks food — then for extreme hunger you shall treat the man promptly: bind the extremities of his limbs with ligatures, pull his hair, wring his ears, and twitch his beard/whiskers. When he recovers, give him soon some bread broken in wine before he takes other foods. Give him foods that are not digested too quickly. Beef, goat, and hart [red deer] digest slowly; buck [male deer] is the worst, as are ram, bull, old four-footed cattle, and fowl with hard flesh — peacock, swan, duck. To those with a cold stomach [wamb] you shall give easily digesting foods: shellfish, young pigeons [culvers], hen's flesh, and goose wings; these are better the fatter and fresher they are. The extremities of the limbs of swine [pigs' trotters] are easy of digestion, as are young cattle and kids. Sweet wine digests better than rough [astringent] wine.

XVII. On all liver diseases, their nature, growth, the six things that cause liver pain, their cures, and the plain signs — either by the urine, by loss of appetite, or by the complexion [hue of the skin].

[Old English section, translated:] ...the kidneys [lendenbrædan]: it [the liver] is the vessel of blood, and the house of blood, and the filter [foscor]. When the digestion of foods and their refinement are complete, they come into the liver, and there they change their form and are transformed into blood; and the impurities [uncleannesses] that are there it drives out, and the pure blood it gathers, and sends it most strongly through four veins to the heart, and also throughout the whole body to the outermost limbs. Concerning the six things that cause liver disease: the first is swelling, that is, distension of the liver; the second is the bursting [rupture] of that swelling; the third is a wound of the liver; the fourth is the heat of inflammation [welm] with tenderness and painful swelling; the sixth [sic] [...]

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Cockayne's edition of the Old English 'Læce Boc' (Leechbook), Book II, which is largely a translation and adaptation of Graeco-Roman medical texts, including Trallianus (Alexander of Tralles). The translator's footnote notes that the physical resuscitation measures (binding limbs, pulling hair, wringing ears, twitching beard) are intended for the fainting episode (lipothymia, Greek: λειποθυμία) just mentioned, as per Trallianus. The Old English section beginning 'V nepefeoþan...' introduces the chapter on liver physiology and disease; the transcription in the source is heavily corrupted by OCR and manuscript abbreviation. 'Wamb' = stomach/womb (here stomach). 'Maw' = stomach. 'Overmickle' = excessive/too great (Old English). 'Culvers' = pigeons/doves. 'Neat' = cattle. 'Wring his ears' and 'twitch his whisker' are stimulation techniques to revive a fainting patient. The Old English liver physiology passage describes the liver as the 'vessel of blood,' 'house of blood,' and filter — consistent with Galenic humoral theory. The six causes of liver disease are enumerated but the sixth item is cut off in the source text (OCR/manuscript lacuna). The chapter heading 'XVII' in Roman numerals appears mid-segment.

results.title_inferred: On the Swollen and Wounded Liver: Tokens, Leechdoms, and Hardening
results.original_text:
XIX.

Tokens of a swollen and wounded liver; leechdoms for that; and of the hardening of the liver. He who is wounded in the liver, if he be not sooner cured, then arriveth at the disorder in which a man speweth purulent matter. If the man swollen in the liver, or the bloated one, abideth so swollen until the five and twentieth day, so as that the swelling bursteth not, then beginneth the liver to harden; if it bursteth, then is there a wound in the liver.

Tokens of the wound are these; when the wound is bursten out then the outrunning through the wamb [belly/intestines] is as it were bloody water, and his face is red and swollen; and when thou settest thy hand upon the liver then he feeleth very great pain, and the man is very tender, and from that disease cometh full oft water-blisters. Against a swollen sore: at first with poultices and salves shall one treat it; the salve shall be of barley groats sodden in lye and of dove's dung wrought with honey; and then lay the salve on a hot cloth or skin, or smear a blanket therewith; then the swelling will soften straightway and ripen within. Let him drink mulsum [hydromel/mead], that is a mingled drink, every day; and goat's milk sodden, and water in which good herbs have been sodden.

XX.

Leechdoms for the wound of the liver when the swelling is ripened and bursteth. Take goat's milk, as warm as newly milked, give to drink. Add also to the drink viper prepared as physicians know; and when he shall be willing to drink of anything let them call it not water, but [let it be] previously sodden from herbs—in wormwood and in other such things—and such poultices as we wrote before. But one shall first bathe and wash the sore places with warm springs and hot water, and in that water shall be sodden laurel berries and shepherd's purse and earthgall [centaury] and wormwood; with that thou shalt long first heat the sore places and let it steam in. If then the wound of the liver festereth greatly so that he hawks that foul matter out of the mouth, make for him a mingled drink [mulsum]; that is a great deal of boiled water in a good deal of honey.
results.translation:
XIX.

Signs of a swollen and wounded liver; remedies for that; and concerning the hardening of the liver. He who has a wound in the liver, if he is not cured sooner, then arrives at the condition in which a man vomits purulent matter. If the man who is swollen in the liver, or the bloated one, remains so swollen until the twenty-fifth day without the swelling bursting, then the liver begins to harden; if it bursts, then there is a wound in the liver.

The signs of the wound are these: when the wound has burst outward, then the discharge through the belly runs like bloody water, and his face is red and swollen; and when you place your hand upon the liver then he feels very great pain, and the man is very tender, and from that disease very often come water-blisters. Against a swollen sore: one shall treat it at first with poultices and salves; the salve shall be made of barley groats sodden in lye and of dove's dung worked with honey; then one shall lay the salve on a hot cloth or skin, or smear a blanket with it; then the swelling will soften immediately and ripen within. Let him drink mulsum [honey-water], that is a mingled drink, every day; and goat's milk that has been boiled, and water in which good herbs have been boiled.

XX.

Remedies for the wound of the liver when the swelling has ripened and bursts. Take goat's milk, as warm as newly milked, and give it to drink. Add also to the drink viper prepared as physicians know how; and when he is willing to drink anything, let them not call it water, but [let it be] previously boiled from herbs—in wormwood and in other such things—and use such poultices as we wrote before. But one must first bathe and wash the sore places with warm springs and hot water, and in that water shall be boiled laurel berries, and shepherd's purse, and earthgall [centaury], and wormwood; with that thou shalt long first heat the sore places and let the steam penetrate. If then the wound of the liver festers greatly, so that he coughs that foul matter out of the mouth, prepare for him a mingled drink [mulsum]; that is a large portion of boiled water with a good portion of honey.

entities_mentioned.name: barley groats sodden in lye
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare (barley) prepared with wood-ash lye
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base for a poultice/salve applied to the swollen liver region.

entities_mentioned.name: dove's dung (culppena scearp)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed with honey to form a salve; use of bird dung in poultices is attested in classical and medieval medicine, possibly also a folk name for a plant (e.g., Ornithogalum), but literal interpretation is more likely here given context.

entities_mentioned.name: honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both as an ingredient in the salve and as a component of mulsum (honey-water drink).

entities_mentioned.name: mulsum (gemilscede drincan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mulsum — a Roman/early medieval drink of boiled water and honey (a form of hydromel/mead)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed as a daily drink for liver patients; the Old English gloss explains it as a 'mingled drink.'

entities_mentioned.name: goat's milk (gate meoluc)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed both boiled (as a daily drink for the swollen liver) and warm and freshly milked (for the burst/festering wound stage).

entities_mentioned.name: water sodden with good herbs
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to generally here; specific herbs named in the bathing decoction below.

entities_mentioned.name: viper (naedran geworthce)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vipera berus or related viper species, prepared medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Added to a drink; the text notes it should be prepared 'as physicians know how.' Use of viper flesh/broth in medicine derives from classical (Galen, Alexander of Tralles) tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood (wermod)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both in an herbal decoction for drinking and in the hot bathing preparation for the sore liver region.

entities_mentioned.name: laurel berries (laures croppan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in water for a hot fomentation/bath applied to the sore liver area.

entities_mentioned.name: shepherd's purse (hierde-wyrt)
entities_mentioned.notes: Included in the hot bathing decoction. Identification as shepherd's purse is conventional but not fully certain in all Old English contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: earthgall (eorðgealla)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centaurium erythraea (common centaury) or Blackstonia perfoliata (yellow-wort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'eorðgealla' (earth-gall) is typically identified as centaury, a bitter herb used in liver complaints. Included in the hot bathing decoction.

results.translator_notes:
1. This segment is drawn from Cockayne's edition of the Old English Leech Book (Bald's Leechbook), Book II, chapters XIX–XX, ff. 75b–76a. The source text interleaves Cockayne's English paraphrase/translation of the Old English with the Old English manuscript text itself.
2. The footnote in the source identifies Alexander of Tralles (Byzantine physician, 6th century) as the classical source underlying this chapter, noting that 'se utgans' (the outgoing) should be understood as the outflow of bile from the liver rather than intestinal discharge.
3. The Old English manuscript text contains scribal abbreviations and ligatures; some characters render as HTML entities (e.g., '>' for '>') due to OCR of the printed edition.
4. 'Wamb' is an archaic/Old English term for belly or womb; here it means belly/gut.
5. The preparation of viper as a medicinal drink ingredient is a direct inheritance from classical Greco-Roman pharmacy (theriac tradition).
6. 'Mulsum' is retained in brackets as a Latin period term; the Old English gloss 'gemilscede drincan' means 'mingled/mixed drink.'
results._label: cockayne_v2::XIX.   Tokens  of  a  swollen  and  wounded  liver

results.title_inferred: Leechdoms for Abscess of the Liver (Chapter XX–XXI)
results.original_text:
XX.

Leechdoms for the abscess of the liver, when the purulent swelling bursteth; take goats milk so warm, newly milked, give the patient that to drink. Form also into a potion an adder, wrought so as leeches ken how to work it, and when the sick will to drink anything, let them drink nothing but water previously sodden with worts, on wormwood and on other such, and such onlayings as we before wrote of. But one shall previously bathe and wash the places with warm squirtings and with hot water, and on the water let there be sodden bunches of laurel berries or flowers, and herdwort, that is, earth gall, and wormwood; with these do thou long previously foment the sore places, and make the reek smoke them. If further the wound of the liver be very rotten, so much as that the man breaketh the rotten matter from his mouth, let him work himself a mulled drink, that is, a mickle deal of boiled water in a good deal of honey; from it shall the scum [be frequently skimmed off before one puts it away, until there be none; let it then cool and be drunk].

XXI.

[Old English text — Signs of abscess of the liver, whether in the lobes and sides and hollows. The hardening is made in two ways. One is at first, before any other ailment comes to the liver more easily; the other comes after other ailments of the liver — that is without pain, and when the man eats anything he vomits again and turns his body and has uncontrolled vomiting and the hiccups; and when thou settest thy hands straight upon the liver they are as heavy as stone and it is not sore. If it be thus for a long time, then it changes into unnatural water-collections, all the bloatings and the fluids which lie distributed throughout the body, and they come from hot blood and boiling. So also must one observe in the liver whether the heat and the bloating is in the liver itself, in the hollows, and in the things that are around the liver, and whether it is in the cartilages and lobes that are in the round parts and sides that are in the two halves. When the physician understands that, he may then the more readily find the remedy. These are the signs: if the bloating is so hot in the liver's other [lobe] or hollow, then there is great swelling and fever with choking of the throat and shining [acute] sore to the collarbones, to the shoulder and coughing and narrowness of the chest, and more heaviness than soreness.]
results.translation:
XX. Remedies for abscess of the liver, when the purulent swelling bursts: take goat's milk still warm, freshly milked, and give the patient that to drink. Also prepare a potion from an adder, prepared as physicians know how to make it. When the sick person wishes to drink anything, let them drink nothing but water previously boiled with herbs — with wormwood and other such plants — and use such poultices [onlayings] as we described before. But one should first bathe and wash the affected places with warm irrigations [squirtings] and with hot water, in which bunches of laurel berries or flowers, and herdwort — that is, earth gall — and wormwood have been boiled; with these foment the sore places at length beforehand, and make the steam/smoke [reek] fumigate them. If furthermore the wound of the liver is very putrid, to such a degree that the man brings up the putrid matter from his mouth, let him prepare for himself a mulled drink — that is, a great quantity of boiled water in a good measure of honey — from which the scum must be frequently skimmed off before one puts it away, until there is none; then let it cool and be drunk.

XXI. [Old English, translated:] Signs of abscess of the liver, whether in the lobes, sides, and hollows. The hardening arises in two ways. One kind occurs at the outset, before any other ailment more easily comes to the liver; the other follows after other ailments of the liver — it is without pain, and when the man eats anything he vomits again, tosses his body, and has uncontrolled vomiting and hiccups; and when thou settest thy hands directly upon the liver, they feel as heavy as stone and it is not sore. If it remains thus for a long time, it then turns into unnatural accumulations of fluid [water-collections], all the bloatings and fluids that lie spread throughout the body coming from hot and boiling blood. So also must one determine in the liver whether the heat and the bloating is in the liver itself, in the hollows, and in the structures surrounding the liver, and whether it is in the cartilages and lobes of the rounded parts and sides, in both halves. When the physician understands this, he may the more readily find the appropriate remedy. These are the signs: if the bloating is intensely hot in the other lobe or hollow of the liver, then there is great swelling and fever with choking of the throat, and sharp [shining/acute] pain extending to the collarbones and to the shoulder, and coughing and tightness of the chest, and more heaviness than soreness.

entities_mentioned.name: goat's milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed warm and freshly milked as a drink for liver abscess when the purulent swelling ruptures.

entities_mentioned.name: adder (in potion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared as an internal potion by physicians ('leeches') according to craft knowledge; exact preparation not described here. Likely a theriac-type preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both as a decoction for drinking (boiled in water) and as a fomentation/fumigation for the sore liver area.

entities_mentioned.name: laurel berries or flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in hot water and used as a warm fomentation/wash over the liver area.

entities_mentioned.name: herdwort / earth gall
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centaurium erythraea (common centaury) or possibly Gentiana spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'eorþgalla' (earth gall) is traditionally equated with centaury or felwort. Used in fomentation decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: honey (mulled drink / hydromel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mel (honey); preparation is a form of hydromel or oxymel
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled water mixed with a good quantity of honey, skimmed until clear, then cooled and drunk; prescribed for advanced putrid liver disease with expectoration of rotten matter.

results.translator_notes: The source text is from Cockayne's edition of the Old English Leechbook (Lǣce Bōc), Vol. 2, Chapters XX–XXI. The English-language portion of Chapter XX is from Cockayne's 19th-century translation and contains period vocabulary preserved: 'onlayings' (poultices), 'squirtings' (irrigations/injections of fluid), 'sodden' (boiled), 'reek' (steam/smoke), 'mickle deal' (great quantity), 'ratteny'/'ratten' (putrid/rotten matter). The Chapter XXI material is in the original Old English (fol. 76b of the manuscript) and has been translated from the Old English here. Several OCR corruptions in the Old English are present (e.g. 'ot' for 'of' in the title, 'WjOjU' for 'patient/wone', 'floivers' for 'flowers', 'ratteny'/'ratten' for 'rotten'/'rotten matter', 'hreaketh' for 'breaketh'); these have been silently corrected where unambiguous. The Old English for XXI contains a detailed semeiology (sign-based diagnosis) of liver abscess with differentiation of anatomical location — an unusually sophisticated clinical passage.
results._label: cockayne_v2::XX.   Leechdoms  for  the  abscess  ot  the  liver

results.title_inferred: Treatment of Abscess with Suppuration: Surgical Incision, Drainage, and Diuretic Remedies (Leech Book II, Chapter XXII–XXIII)
results.original_text:
XXII.

LEECH BOOK. II. 209

every day to drink the wort parsley, and dill, and seed of marche or its roots with honey: if he hath no fever eke that with wine. After that other wort drinks are proper, when the swelling is become an abscess and bursteth, and is becoming more free from soreness, and is passing off downwards through the wamb, and the man pisseth ratten, reckoneth that he then may be hole; then must be given him principally the diuretic drinks, in order that all the mischief through the wamb and through the mie may be done away, lest the man should take to spewing ratten through the mouth; and let him withhold himself somewhat from the bath and from green apples. If however the swelling and the ratten mounteth up to that degree that it seem to thee that a man may cut into it and let it out, then work him a salve first of culvers sharn and the like of that, and previously bathe the places with sousings, with the water, and with the worts which are wrote of before. When thou understandeth that the swelling is growing nesh and mild, then touch thou it with the cutting iron, and cut in a little, and cleverly, even that the blood may come out, lest an evil sinus or pouch descend in thither. Do not let too much blood at one time, lest the sick man become too languid or die; but when thou dost prick or cut it, then have for thyself a linen cloth ready that therewith thou mayst soon bind up the cut; and when thou wilt again let more blood draw the cloth off, let it run by a little at a time till it gets dry; and when the wound is clean, then enlarge it that the thirl or aperture may not be too narrow; but do thou every day syringe through it with a tube,

[Old English, fol. 78b:]
sum þiddan oþlege þe þa wunde clænsien · gif hio wiþon mynstre weorþe clænsian mid hunige · 7 gelæc erc togædere. Gif þonne seo ungefelede aheardung þære liþe to langlum wyrþ · þonne wyrþeþ hio stæcer bollan þone þe mon gelacman ne mæg. 7 him sceal sona on fruman þa ær genemnedun bestungan · ne drince he nipæs naht · 7 gif se þrefsoca mon blodes to fela lifebbe þonne sceal him mon ær eallum oþrum læcedomum blod læcan of þam swiþran earme on þære niþerran æbre · gif þa mon ne mæge þare gerædan þonne sceal mon on þære middel æbre blod lætan · þa þe þær ne boþ on micel eaþerum becumaþ.

.XXIII.
Dyrst him ne to wyrcanne on lifer adle hwæt him ne to healdanne.
results.translation:
XXII.

LEECH BOOK. II. 209

[Instruct the patient] every day to drink a preparation of the herb parsley, and dill, and the seed of marche [alexanders] or its roots with honey; if he hath no fever, augment that with wine. After that, other herbal drinks are appropriate, when the swelling has become an abscess and bursteth, and is becoming more free from soreness, and is passing downwards through the belly [wamb], and the man passeth matter [ratten = pus/putrid matter], reckon then that he may become whole; then must be given to him principally the diuretic drinks, in order that all the morbid matter through the belly and through the kidneys [mie] may be expelled, lest the man should begin to vomit pus through the mouth; and let him abstain somewhat from the bath and from green apples. If however the swelling and the pus mount up to such a degree that it seem to thee that a man may cut into it and let it out, then prepare for him a salve first of pigeon dung [culvers sharn] and the like thereof, and previously bathe the places with fomentations [sousings], with the water, and with the herbs that are written of before. When thou perceivest that the swelling is growing soft and mild, then touch thou it with the cutting iron [lance/scalpel], and cut in a little, and skilfully, just enough that the blood may come out, lest an evil sinus or pouch form inward. Do not let too much blood at one time, lest the sick man become too faint or die; but when thou dost prick or cut it, then have for thyself a linen cloth ready that therewith thou mayst soon bind up the cut; and when thou wilt again let more blood, draw the cloth off and let it run a little at a time until it runs dry; and when the wound is clean, then enlarge it so that the opening or aperture may not be too narrow; but do thou every day syringe through it with a tube,

[Continuing from Old English:]
and apply some poultices thereupon that shall cleanse the wound; if it be within a smaller space, cleanse it with honey and draw it together again. If however the unfelt [insensible] hardening of the limb be of too long standing, then it becometh a fixed swelling [induration] that one cannot treat medically. And one must from the outset apply the stinging remedies mentioned before; let him drink nothing raw [unprepared]; and if the afflicted man have too much blood, then one must before all other remedies let blood from the right arm in the lower vein; if that cannot be reached, then one must let blood in the median vein, those which are not easily accessible there.

XXIII.
What is not to be done in liver disease; what is to be maintained [i.e., what the patient must observe/avoid].

entities_mentioned.name: parsley (wort parsley)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a daily herbal drink; diuretic and cleansing properties cited.

entities_mentioned.name: dill
entities_mentioned.notes: Included in the daily herbal drink preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: marche (seed or roots)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Smyrnium olusatrum (alexanders) or possibly Apium graveolens (wild celery)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'merce'; commonly identified as alexanders in Anglo-Saxon herbal contexts; roots or seed used with honey or wine.

entities_mentioned.name: honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle/solvent for the herbal drink and as a wound cleanser applied internally to the abscess cavity.

entities_mentioned.name: wine
entities_mentioned.notes: Added to the herbal drink if the patient has no fever.

entities_mentioned.name: culvers sharn (pigeon dung)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the primary ingredient in a ripening/maturing salve applied to the abscess before incision; a common Anglo-Saxon poultice ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: cutting iron
entities_mentioned.notes: Instrument used to incise the mature abscess; procedure described with careful instructions to avoid creating a sinus tract.

entities_mentioned.name: linen cloth
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to bind the incised wound and control blood flow after drainage.

entities_mentioned.name: tube (for syringing)
entities_mentioned.notes: A hollow tube used to irrigate the abscess cavity daily; an early description of wound irrigation.

entities_mentioned.name: green apples
entities_mentioned.notes: Patient instructed to avoid green apples, likely due to their astringent or indigestible qualities.

entities_mentioned.name: diuretic drinks (wort drinks)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various herbal decoctions with diuretic action
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed to expel morbid matter (pus) through the kidneys and bowel, preventing upward movement of pus to the mouth.

entities_mentioned.name: mie
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English term for kidneys; referenced as route of excretion for morbid matter.

entities_mentioned.name: wamb
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English term for the belly/gut; route of downward passage of pus.

entities_mentioned.name: ratten
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English term for rotten/putrid matter, i.e., purulent discharge; passage in urine or vomit noted as a prognostic sign.

results.translator_notes: This passage is from the Old English Leech Book (Bald's Leechbook), Book II, Chapter XXII, as translated and edited by Thomas Oswald Cockayne (Leechdoms, Wortcunning and Starcraft of Early England, Vol. II). The segment describes the management of an internal suppurating swelling (abscess), likely pleuritis, empyema, or hepatic abscess given the mention of a Chapter XXIII on liver disease immediately following. The treatment sequence is: (1) herbal diuretic drinks, (2) ripening salve of pigeon dung, (3) fomentation, (4) surgical incision with careful blood-letting and irrigation, (5) dietary restrictions. The Old English text on fol. 78b is the source for the English translation on the facing page. The term 'thirl' (Old English þyrel) means a hole or aperture. 'Nesh' means soft or tender. 'Sousings' refers to fomentations or soaking washes. The instruction to syringe daily with a tube through the wound is a notably sophisticated surgical procedure. Chapter XXIII heading at the end introduces liver disease management ('Dyrst him ne to wyrcanne on lifer adle') which is not yet translated in this segment.
results._label: cockayne_v2::XXII.    LEECH   BOOK.    TI.  209   every  day  t

results.title_inferred: Leechdoms for Liver Disease (Leech Book I, Chapter 215–216)
results.original_text:
1548. [Some word, perhaps wyn, is omitted by MS.]

LEECH BOOK I [p. 215]

again in the evening. Let him withhold himself also from vinegar, from the bath, from peas, and beans, and navews, and from the things which work in a man a windy vapour. Again, beat or rub up and sift costmary, fenugreek, pepper, hares treadles, equal quantities of all; put a spoon full of this into wine, and give it to him who is without fever, to drink. To him who hath fever, that is mickle heat and fire, give it in warm water; then let him lie on the right side and lay his right hand stretched out under his head, for half an hour. Again, wortdrinks for liver disease: to two little bowls full of juice of clover mingled with a little honey, add a bowl full of heated wine; give this to be drunk for three days, if anything of evil be on the liver, the drink will cure it. Again, give to drink for four days, three little bowls full of the juice of wild mallow, mingled with two such of water; and if fever disease be on him, the wort drink driveth it away. Again, rub together wine, cummin, and honey, give him this to drink. Again, five and twenty bunches of ivy berries, gathered in the month which we hight in Latin Januarius, and in English the second Yule, and of pepper as much, rub these up with the best wine, and heat it; give it to the sick man, after his nights fasting, to drink. A leechdom again for liver disease: dry clean some twigs or stalks of colewort with the flower heads, burn them to ashes, store the ashes, and when occasion is, put a spoon full of the ashes with eleven ground pepper corns into old very clear wine, then heat it, give to be drunk; the next time nine corns, the third time seven. A leechdom again for liver disease: rub small a bunch of bay berries and twenty pepper corns, put them into a bowl full of old wine, and mingle them together with a glowing [iron].

[Old English:]

1548. [Some word, perhaps 'wine' (OE: wyn), is omitted by the manuscript.]

LEECH BOOK I [p. 215]

...again in the evening. Let him also abstain from vinegar, from the bath, from peas, beans, and navews [turnip-like roots, Brassica rapa], and from those things which produce a windy [flatulent] vapour in a man. Again, beat or grind up and sift costmary, fenugreek, pepper, and hare's dung [hares treadles], in equal quantities of all; put a spoonful of this into wine and give it to him who is without fever to drink. To him who hath fever — that is, great heat and fire — give it in warm water; then let him lie on the right side and lay his right hand stretched out under his head for half an hour.

Again, herbal drinks [wortdrinks] for liver disease: to two small bowls full of juice of clover mingled with a little honey, add a bowl full of heated wine; give this to be drunk for three days — if anything evil be upon the liver, the drink will cure it.

Again, give to drink for four days three small bowls full of the juice of wild mallow, mingled with two such measures of water; and if fever disease be upon him, the herbal drink will drive it away.

Again, rub together wine, cumin, and honey, and give him this to drink.

Again, five and twenty clusters of ivy berries, gathered in the month which we call in Latin Januarius [January], and in English the second Yule, and of pepper an equal amount — rub these up with the best wine and heat it; give it to the sick man to drink after his night's fasting.

A leechdom again for liver disease: dry and clean some twigs or stalks of colewort [cabbage/kale] with the flower heads, burn them to ashes, store the ashes, and when occasion arises, put a spoonful of the ashes with eleven ground peppercorns into old, very clear wine, then heat it and give to be drunk; the next time use nine corns, the third time seven.

A leechdom again for liver disease: grind small a bunch of bay berries and twenty peppercorns, put them into a bowl full of old wine, and stir them together with a glowing [iron implement]; give [this] to drink and let [the patient] lie quietly.

Again, woodbine [or vine-creeper]: the green twigs wound upward around [...]

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Old English Leech Book (Bald's Leechbook), a 10th-century Anglo-Saxon medical manuscript. The passage contains multiple distinct remedies for liver disease ('lifire adle'), dietary restrictions, and fever management. The footnote indicates the Saxon author interprets 'fever' (a Latinism) with native English descriptors ('mickle heat and fire'). The Old English continuation at the bottom of the page is partially legible due to OCR degradation of the manuscript facsimile; the translation of that section is tentative. 'Hares treadles' (animal dung) was a documented ingredient in early medieval pharmacy. The technique of stirring with a 'glowing iron' to heat and possibly sterilize the preparation is noteworthy. The decreasing-dose regimen for peppercorns (11→9→7) reflects a structured therapeutic protocol. The second Yule = January in the Anglo-Saxon calendar.

results.title_inferred: Signs and Remedies for Disorders of the Stomach (Wamb), Leech Book II, Chapter XXV
results.original_text:
XXV.

Here are plain tokens of disorders and sicknesses of the wamb, and how a man shall cure the evil humours of the wamb. When wamb disease is present then the tokens are; the wamb turneth itself, and is fevered, and feeleth sore when the man eateth meat, and prickings, and loss of appetite for meat. The knees are slow, the loins are heavy, and there are spasms between the shoulders, and all the body by piece meal is heavy, and the feet are tardy, and the muscles of the loins are sore; when a man observes these tokens, then the first leechdom is a day's fasting, that with that he may cleanse the wamb, that it may be the lighter. Well, if the disease be still on the increase, let him fast for two days together if his strength allow; if he cannot, then let one give him some lighter food to taste, such as thin broths and the like. Some, for cleansing of the wamb, seethe nettles in water and in wine and in oil; some use the red nettle twice or thrice; some seethe bear or dock in sweetened wine and give to drink; and if the disease increase and the sick man by morning be touched thereby, then seethe for him stronger herbs and add somewhat of pepper thereto. Let one carefully observe what the outgoing [bowel movement] is — whether much or little or whether there be none at all — and let the leech judge thereby how it seemeth to him what should be done. If it be undigested matter within, as yet unburnt [unconsumed], let one draw it out with certain sinking [downward-acting, laxative] foods, and let it not stay settled within the body; and through gathered undigested matter in the wamb or in the small intestine, there is then no outgoing of the stool, but the stool is corrupted and the stomach is turned and broken, and the head is swollen and sore, and the flanks are blown up, and heat is lost, and great thirst, and all bodily strength is weakened and prostrated. One shall treat such a disease, if there be no fever, with cow's milk or goat's, freshly milked, as a drink. It also helpeth if one heat the milk with river-stones that have been burned [heated in fire], or with hot iron, and give it to drink; and if the patient be a young man and it be the right time and he have strength, one should draw blood copiously from the arm, and after three nights let him drink the milk again.
results.translation:
Chapter XXV.

Here are clear signs of disorders and sicknesses of the stomach [wamb], and how a man shall cure the evil humours of the stomach. When stomach disease is present, the signs are these: the stomach turns itself, is fevered, and feels sore when the man eats food; there are prickings [sharp pains] and loss of appetite. The knees are sluggish, the loins are heavy, there are spasms between the shoulders, and the whole body is gradually heavy; the feet are slow and the muscles of the loins are sore. When a man observes these signs, the first remedy [leechdom] is a day's fasting, so that by it he may cleanse the stomach and make it lighter. Well, if the disease is still increasing, let him fast for two days together if his strength permits; if he cannot do that, then let someone give him a little light food to taste, such as thin broths and the like. Some, for the cleansing of the stomach, seethe nettles in water and in wine and in oil; some use the red nettle two or three times; some seethe bear's wort [or dock] in sweetened wine and give it to drink. And if the disease increases and the sick man is affected by morning, then seethe for him stronger herbs and add some pepper to it. Let one carefully observe what the evacuations are — whether much or little, or whether there are none at all — and let the physician judge thereby what he thinks should be done. If there is undigested matter within, not yet consumed [broken down], let one draw it out with certain downward-acting [laxative] foods, and let it not settle within the body; for through gathered, undigested matter in the stomach or in the small intestine, there is then no passing of stool, but the stool is putrefied and the stomach is disordered and distraught, and the head is swollen and sore, and the flanks are bloated, and vital heat is lost, and there is great thirst, and the strength of the whole body is weakened and laid low. One should treat such a disease, if there is no fever, with cow's milk or goat's milk drunk freshly milked. It also helps if one heats the milk with river-stones burned [fire-heated] or with hot iron, and gives it to drink. And if the patient is a young man, and the time is right, and he has strength, one should draw blood copiously from the arm, and after three nights let him drink the milk again.

entities_mentioned.name: nettles (nettle, seethed in water, wine, and oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urtica dioica (stinging nettle) or related Urtica spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for cleansing the stomach; red nettle variant also mentioned, possibly Urtica pilulifera or simply the common nettle at a different growth stage.

entities_mentioned.name: red nettle (readan netlan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Urtica pilulifera (Roman nettle) or Lamium purpureum (red dead-nettle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from the plain nettle; given two or three times for stomach cleansing.

entities_mentioned.name: bear (beran) or dock (boccan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Meum athamanticum (bear's wort / baldmoney) for 'beran'; Rumex spp. (dock) for 'boccan'
entities_mentioned.notes: Seethed in sweetened wine and given to drink for stomach disorders. The Old English 'beran' is ambiguous — it may refer to bear's wort or another plant associated with bears.

entities_mentioned.name: pepper (pipor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Added to stronger herb decoctions when disease increases; used as a stimulant/carminative.

entities_mentioned.name: Jack-in-the-hedge / Erysimum alliaria
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced in the editor's footnote 1 as a plant associated with this passage; modern name Erysimum alliaria is a synonym of Alliaria petiolata.

entities_mentioned.name: cow's milk or goat's milk (cu meolcum oþþe gate, freshly milked)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bos taurus / Capra aegagrus hircus milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed for stomach disorders in the absence of fever; may be heated with hot stones or hot iron before drinking.

entities_mentioned.name: bloodletting from the arm
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended for young, strong patients; blood drawn copiously from the arm, followed three nights later by a milk drink.

results.translator_notes: The source text is Old English (Anglo-Saxon) from the Leech Book (Læce Boc), Book II, Chapter XXV, as edited and partially translated by Cockayne. The printed English at the beginning and end of the segment is Cockayne's own translation; the middle section is the Old English manuscript text (with some MS. variants noted). This translation renders the full chapter from both portions. The word 'wamb' (OE womb) here clearly means the stomach/digestive tract rather than the uterus (another common meaning). The phrase 'sinking foods' refers to laxative or downward-acting foods. The footnote reference to Paul of Aegina ('Paul, Ægin.') suggests this chapter derives partly from the Byzantine medical tradition of Paul of Aegina, a common source for Anglo-Saxon medical texts. The MS. variants noted by Cockayne (e.g., 'don' for 'bon', 'clænsunge' for 'clæmpunja') are minor scribal differences. 'Omihte pæte' is interpreted as undigested or unconsumed matter — literally 'unburnt food' — consistent with humoral theory of crudity. 'Sinfel-þearm' is rendered 'small intestine' (literally 'sin-gut' or 'winding gut').

  "original_text": "XXVI.

Of sickness of the wamb, and if it be wounded within, how a man may understand that and cure it. First if there be a wound upon it within, then is there sore, and grumblings, and irritation; and when they take meat and drink, then they have nausea, and their mouth is foul, and they are fevered, and their discharge is bloody and stinketh foully: to those men shall be given eggs to sup up, barley bread, clean new butter, and new barley meal or groats made into a brewit together, as cooks ken to do; let it be administered to them after their nights fast. Again for that, let one apply good salves, and external applications, such as may draw out that evil, also easily digested meats, and sheer and smooth wine.

XXVII.

Of the various nature of the wamb or of its caprice, how a man may understand that. When it is of a hot temper and nature, then a little drink may soon help it. If the drink be more powerful soon the wamb is oppressed and palpitates, as if in cold it were beating, and it rejoiceth in dry meats. When the wamb is moist it doth not suffer thirst, and it is of a very moist nature; it doth not suffer thirst nor heaviness from meats, and it rejoiceth in moist meats. Of the hot nature of the wamb. The wamb, that namely which is of a hot nature, digests meats well, especially those which be hard and of difficult digestion, and rejoices in warm meats and drinks, and it is not harmed by cold meats, taken with moderation. That which is of a watery nature hath a good appetite for meat; it hath not a good digestion, chiefly of the meats which be of difficult digestion, it rejoices in cold meats.
