results.title_inferred: On Poor Digestion, Bad Humours, and Their Treatment by Emesis and Diet
results.original_text:
XXIX.

In case a "mans" meat doth not well digest, and turneth to evil humour and to excrement, it is good for those "men" that "they" should spew, if it be not too uneasy to "him," irritate him to spew by a wort drink. If there be extreme need that he may be able to spew before meat, let him manage that with sweetened wine. Let him flee the meats which work him mucus, and burnings, and heat in his inside, and which too readily digest: let him take those which work a good juice, and make the wamb nesh. At whiles it is good for him that one should give him light wort drinks, such as are aloes well prepared. The humour, if one doth not get rid of it, worketh not easily cured diseases, that is to say, foot pain, joint pain, loins pain; and often a strong fever cometh on the men who have that disease.
results.translation:
XXIX.

In the case where a man's food does not digest well, and turns to bad humour [corrupt bodily fluid] and to waste matter, it is beneficial for such men that they should vomit; if this is not too difficult for him, induce him to vomit by means of a herbal [wort] drink. If there is urgent need for him to be able to vomit before eating, let him accomplish this with sweetened wine. Let him avoid the foods which produce in him mucus, burning sensations, and heat in his belly, and those which digest too quickly; let him take instead those which produce a good juice [chyme] and make the stomach [wamb] soft [nesh]. At times it is good for him that one should give him light herbal drinks, such as well-prepared aloes. The humour [corrupt fluid], if one does not purge it, produces diseases not easily cured — namely, pain in the feet, pain in the joints, and pain in the loins; and often a severe fever comes upon the men who suffer from this condition.

entities_mentioned.name: wort drink
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: herbal decoction (general term; specific herb unspecified)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'wyrt' denotes a herb or plant; used here as an emetic preparation. The specific plant is not identified in the text.

entities_mentioned.name: sweetened wine
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an emetic agent taken before meals. Likely reflects classical Galenic dietary medicine transmitted through Anglo-Saxon sources.

entities_mentioned.name: aloes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera / Aloe barbadensis or related Aloe spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared aloes were a well-known laxative and digestive remedy in classical and medieval medicine; described here as a 'light wort drink' to help clear bad humours.

results.translator_notes: This passage is from Cockayne's edition of an Anglo-Saxon medical text (likely Bald's Leechbook or a related Leechbook), Volume 2, Chapter XXIX. The archaic pronouns ('mans', 'men', 'him' in quotation marks) reflect Cockayne's effort to render Old English third-person constructions into Victorian English. 'Wamb' is Old English for stomach or belly; 'nesh' means soft or tender. The conditions described — foot pain, joint pain, loin pain, and fever arising from unresolved bad humour — are consistent with a Galenic interpretation of gout, arthritis, and related febrile conditions caused by retained corrupt matter. The treatment paradigm (emesis, dietary regulation, herbal purgatives) is classically Galenic and likely mediated through late antique sources such as Alexander of Tralles or Isidore of Seville.

results.title_inferred: On the Health of the Belly: Remedies for Digestive and Urinary Complaints
results.original_text: XXX. If thou wish that thy wamb be always sound, then shalt thou thus treat it, if thou wilt. Look to it every day that thy fæcal discharge, and thy mie, be of sound aspect as right is. If the mie be little, seethe marche and fennel, work a good broth, or seethe juice of . . . and of other sweet worts. If the fæcal discharge be too little, take the wort which in southern lands hight turpentine tree, as much of it as the size of an olive; give it the sick when he will go to bed. These worts are also very good for that, and more: mealpe and brassica and the like sodden [boiled] with young swine's flesh; take that broth; and also dead nettle sodden in water and pressed to drink; and also elder's leaf and the broth in the same wise. Some drip leaves when one will go to sleep, such as the size of three beans, to swallow each day; and brews like these, that strengthen — if there be need, they are to be given — chiefly in early spring before the evil humour that is gathered in winter pour itself throughout the other limbs. Many men care not then nor heed what comes from those evil humours: either the half-dead disease [palsy], or wild madness, or the painful disorder that in southern tongue is called tetanus, or head-ailment, or erysipelas [St. Anthony's fire]. Therefore one must first cleanse the evil humours away before the evils come and grow in winter and spread through the limbs. For belly-ache and pain: smear or beat hemlock [?] a bowl-full, and two cups of vinegar, boil together, give to drink fasting to the sick man. Again, lay bruised brooklime chosen upon the navel; it soon stills it. Again, a little dill seed rubbed in water, give to drink. For belly-ache and for inner pain: when for great cold the belly be unruled [disordered], do those things to it that are written above. If there be then a turning of crudity [undigested matter] or a cutting pain, take three drops of laurel berries rubbed, and of cumin, and of wild parsley separate spoonsful, and twenty corns of pepper; rub all together and dry three times in a warm belly; after that take water, rub dill in it, and give those things warm to drink, until the pain be stilled. For the same, take lily sodden in goat's milk; apply [foment] in the southern [manner].
results.translation: Chapter XXX. If you wish that your belly [wamb] be always sound, you shall treat it thus, if you will. See to it every day that your fæcal discharge and your urine [mie] are of sound appearance, as is proper. If the urine be scanty, boil smallage [marche] and fennel, make a good broth, or boil the juice of [lacuna] and of other sweet herbs [worts]. If the fæcal discharge be too scanty, take the herb which in southern lands is called the turpentine tree, as much as the size of an olive, and give it to the sick person when he wishes to go to bed. These herbs are also very good for that purpose, and more: mercury-herb [mealpe] and brassica [cabbage/kale] and the like, boiled together with the flesh of a young pig; let the patient drink that broth; and also dead nettle boiled in water and pressed out to drink; and also elder leaves and the broth prepared in the same manner. Some drip leaves when one wishes to go to sleep — of such a quantity as three beans — to be swallowed each day; and drinks similar to these, which strengthen the body — if there be need, they are to be given — especially in early spring before the evil humour gathered in winter pours itself throughout the other limbs. Many men of former times did not care nor do they now heed what comes of those evil humours: either the half-death disease [palsy/hemiplegia], or raving madness, or the painful disorder that in the southern tongue is called tetanus, or head-ailment, or erysipelas. Therefore one must first cleanse away the evil humours before the evils come and grow in winter and spread through the limbs. For belly-ache and pain: smear [or beat] hemlock [?] a bowl-full, and two cups of vinegar, boil together, give to the sick man to drink fasting. Again, lay bruised brooklime, well chosen, upon the navel; it soon stills the pain. Again, a little dill seed rubbed [ground] into water, give to drink. For belly-ache and for inner pain: when from great cold the belly is disordered [unruled], do those things to it that are written above. If there be then a churning of undigested matter or a sharp cutting pain, take three drops of laurel berries rubbed, and of cumin, and of wild parsley, each a separate spoonful, and twenty grains of pepper; rub all together, and let it warm three times in a warm belly [i.e., apply with warmth]; after that, take water, rub dill into it, and give those things warm to drink until the pain is stilled. For the same ailment, take lily boiled in goat's milk and apply it in the southern fashion.

entities_mentioned.name: wamb
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English term for belly or womb; here used in the digestive sense.

entities_mentioned.name: mie
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'mige' meaning urine.

entities_mentioned.name: marche
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'merce'; used to promote urination; boiled into broth.

entities_mentioned.name: fennel
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled with smallage into a diuretic broth.

entities_mentioned.name: turpentine tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a laxative; dose the size of an olive, given at bedtime. Called 'turpentine tree' in 'southern lands.'

entities_mentioned.name: mealpe

entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin version of the text substitutes 'mercurialis' for this term; used as a laxative herb boiled with pork.

entities_mentioned.name: brassica
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled with young pork as a laxative broth.

entities_mentioned.name: dead nettle
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in water and the liquid drunk; laxative use. The Latin version substitutes 'mercurialis' here, suggesting some textual variation.

entities_mentioned.name: elder leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaves and broth used similarly to the other herbs above as a laxative.

entities_mentioned.name: brooklime
entities_mentioned.notes: Bruised and laid on the navel to relieve belly-ache.

entities_mentioned.name: dill seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Ground into water and drunk for belly-ache; also used warm in drink for cutting abdominal pain.

entities_mentioned.name: laurel berries
entities_mentioned.notes: Three drops rubbed with cumin and wild parsley for colic/cutting pain.

entities_mentioned.name: cumin
entities_mentioned.notes: A spoonful rubbed with laurel and wild parsley for abdominal pain.

entities_mentioned.name: wild parsley (seterwilian / peterwilian)
entities_mentioned.notes: A separate spoonful combined with laurel and cumin for colic.

entities_mentioned.name: pepper
entities_mentioned.notes: Twenty grains ground with the other spices for abdominal pain.

entities_mentioned.name: lily
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in goat's milk and applied ('in the southern fashion') for belly-ache and inner pain.

entities_mentioned.name: half-dead disease (healfdeade adl)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English medical term for a paralytic condition; attributed to evil humours accumulating in winter.

entities_mentioned.name: tetanus / hrice wierd (hrîce wierdo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tetanus or a severe spasmodic disorder
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a painful disorder named in the southern (Latin) tongue as 'tetanus'; caused by uncleansed evil humours.

results.translator_notes: This chapter is from the Old English Leechbook (Lǣcebōc), likely Bald's Leechbook, Book II. The source text interleaves a printed English translation (Victorian, by Cockayne) with the underlying Old English manuscript text (fols. 85b–86a), apparently from a facsimile or diplomatic edition. The lacuna ('juice of . . .') corresponds to an illegible or omitted plant name in the manuscript. The footnote indicates that a related Latin version substitutes 'mercurialis' for both 'mealpe' and 'nettle,' suggesting scribal conflation. The phrase 'in the southern fashion' (on sutherne) likely refers to Mediterranean/Classical medical practice. 'Mie' (mige) is consistently rendered as urine. The medical theory here is explicitly humoral: winter accumulates evil humours that must be purged in early spring to prevent serious disease. The preparation methods include: decoction/broth, topical poultice (on navel), ground seeds in water, and compound spice preparations taken warm.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Stomach Sickness and Bowel Complaints
results.original_text: 2. For wamb sickness seethe rue in oil, and let the sick swallow it in oil. Again, give him to eat a wild pigeon sodden in vinegar and in water. For wamb sickness, again, let him chew leaves of laurel, and swallow the juice, and let him lay the leaves on his navel. Again, give melted harts marrow in hot water to drink. For moderating the action of the wamb; take beet, delve it up and shake the mould off, do not wash it, but seethe and boil it in a kettle so long, that it be all sodden to pieces, and run thick, then add a little salt, and of honey five spoon measures, of oil one spoon measure, give the man a bowl full. Again, give to the sick to eat, separate, the top of a sodden leek, having a head to it. Again, give him to eat some seed of the red nettle on bread. Again, give him to drink juice of mulberries. Again, let him eat after his night's fasting plum fruits. Again, give him to drink elder rind beaten, as much as may weigh a penny, in a bowl full of cold water.
results.translation: 2. For stomach [wamb] sickness: boil [seethe] rue in oil, and let the sick person swallow it in the oil. Again, give him to eat a wild pigeon boiled [sodden] in vinegar and water. For stomach sickness, again, let him chew leaves of laurel and swallow the juice, and let him lay the leaves upon his navel. Again, give melted hart's [male deer's] marrow dissolved in hot water to drink. For regulating [moderating] the action of the stomach [wamb]: take beet, dig it up and shake the earth off, do not wash it, but boil it in a kettle for so long that it is entirely dissolved to pieces and becomes thick, then add a little salt, five spoon-measures of honey, and one spoon-measure of oil; give the man a bowl full. Again, give the sick person to eat, separately, the top of a boiled leek that has a head on it. Again, give him to eat some seed of the red nettle on bread. Again, give him to drink juice of mulberries. Again, let him eat plum fruits after his overnight fast. Again, give him to drink beaten elder rind, as much as weighs a penny [a small unit of weight], in a bowl full of cold water.

entities_mentioned.name: rue
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled in oil and swallowed for stomach sickness. Rue was a common Old English and medieval herbal remedy for digestive ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: wild pigeon
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Columba livia or related Columbidae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Boiled [sodden] in vinegar and water; consumed as food remedy for stomach sickness.

entities_mentioned.name: laurel
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaves chewed and juice swallowed; leaves also applied topically to the navel for stomach sickness.

entities_mentioned.name: harts marrow
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bone marrow of red deer (Cervus elaphus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Melted and dissolved in hot water; drunk for stomach sickness.

entities_mentioned.name: beet
entities_mentioned.notes: Dug up unwashed, boiled to a thick consistency, combined with salt, honey, and oil; given as a bowl-full to regulate bowel action. Preparation notably specifies not washing the root before cooking.

entities_mentioned.name: leek
entities_mentioned.notes: The top of a boiled leek with its head, given separately to the sick.

entities_mentioned.name: red nettle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lamium purpureum (red dead-nettle) or possibly Urtica dioica var.
entities_mentioned.notes: Seeds eaten on bread for stomach complaint. 'Red nettle' in Old English sources most commonly refers to Lamium purpureum.

entities_mentioned.name: mulberries
entities_mentioned.notes: Juice given to drink for stomach sickness.

entities_mentioned.name: plum fruits
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus domestica or related Prunus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Eaten after overnight fasting for stomach/bowel complaints; likely exploiting laxative properties.

entities_mentioned.name: elder rind
entities_mentioned.notes: Beaten elder rind in a penny-weight dose, dissolved in a bowl of cold water and drunk. Elder bark was traditionally used as a purgative.

results.translator_notes: The term 'wamb' (Old English: wamb/womb) refers here to the stomach or abdomen generally, not specifically the uterus. 'Sodden' means boiled. 'Seethe' means to boil or simmer. 'Moderating the action of the wamb' likely refers to regulating bowel function, either as a laxative or antidiarrheal depending on context; the note reference (p. 165) suggests the editor had additional commentary. 'A penny' as a weight measure refers to a pennyweight (approximately 1.56 grams in later usage). The instruction not to wash the beet before boiling is a noteworthy and specific procedural detail. Some OCR artifacts in the original ('ilw. ^]^^ ^^■', 'to})') have been silently corrected based on context.

results.title_inferred: On diseases of the belly, colon, and small intestines: symptoms and early treatment
results.original_text:
XXXI. Of wamb sicknesses, and of tokens in the colon and in the small guts. There is a kind of that ilk disease in the wamb, and in the colon, and small guts, of which this will be for a token; that the sick suffer immoderate thirst and loss of appetite for meat, and often they have a flux with a mingled faecal discharge, at whiles hard, at whiles white, at whiles they discharge often in the day and then little at a time, at whiles once and then much; at whiles a desire is upon them to go to stool and to cast the burthen from them, and gladly would they attend to it, but they are not able, they have not the power of digestion, and they drop blood, very much like a broken vessel. Of their hue, or complexion, and of the navel, and of the dorsal muscles, and of the back gut or rectum, and of the lower belly, and the milt, and the share; they are [horr-]

[Old English source, fol. 87a–87b:]
neofan · ⁊ bæcþearme · ⁊ neþereoþan · ⁊ milte þare · beoð æblæce ⁊ eal se lichoma ascimod · ⁊ yfel scenc naþ his selfes geweald · ⁊ biþ þæt sar on þa swiþran sidan · healfe · on þa sceare · ⁊ þa wambe swiþe geneappod · ⁊ eac fram þam nafolan oþ þone milce · ⁊ on þa winestran rigeþreofan ⁊ gecymþ æt þam bæcþearme ⁊ eac þam neþereoþan · ⁊ þa lendenu beoþ mid micle sare begyrdede. Wenað unwispe læcas þ þæt fie lenben adl oþþe miltre wærc · ac hit ne biþ swa · lenben seoke men migas blode ⁊ sande þonne þam þe milte wærc biþ · þrideþ him se milr ⁊ biþ aheardod on þam winestran dæle þære sidan. Þa wambseocan men þropiaþ on þam bæcþearme ⁊ on þam niþerran hwrife ⁊ losað him sona sio scern ⁊ cele þropiaþ ⁊ slæp oftogen ⁊ imho ⁊ tiþð innan þone for ⁊ on þæt smæl þearm.

XXXII. Þisse adle fruman mon mæg yfelice gelacnian · on þa ilcan wisan þe þa utyrnendan · ⁊ æfter uneaþ · gif hio biþ unwislice to lange forlæten. On fruman mon sceal dæg oþþe II. togeadere gewæscan ⁊ beþan þa breost mid wine · ⁊ mid ele ⁊ wyrcean onlegenan of rosan ⁊ berenum melpe wiþ win gemengeb ⁊ on hunige gesoben ⁊ mid ele on mortere gesamnod lege ofer þa sceare of þone nafolan ⁊ ofer þa lendeno oþ þone bæcþearm ⁊ þær hit sar lie · læt him blod ryne ⁊ þæt sete þær on oþþe horn ⁊ teo þæt blod ut · ⁊ smere mid ele fol. 87b. ⁊ bewreoh hine wearme for þon þe cile biþ swæðe adle
results.translation:
XXXI. On diseases of the belly [wamb], and on symptoms in the colon and in the small intestines. There is a certain type of disease of this kind in the belly, and in the colon, and in the small intestines, of which the token [sign/symptom] shall be this: that the sick suffer immoderate thirst and loss of appetite for food, and often they have a flux [diarrhoea] with a mixed faecal discharge — sometimes hard, sometimes white; sometimes they discharge frequently during the day but only a little at a time, sometimes once only but then a great deal; sometimes they have an urge to go to stool and to expel the burden, and they would gladly do so, but they are unable — they have no power of digestion — and they pass blood, very much as from a broken vessel.

As to their colour [complexion], and the navel, and the dorsal muscles, and the back gut [rectum], and the lower belly, and the spleen [milt], and the groin [share]: they become pallid [horribly discoloured], and the whole body wastes; and the sick person has no control over foul odour; and the pain is on the right side, half on the groin, and the belly is much constricted; and also from the navel as far as the spleen, and on the left rib-cartilage; and it reaches the rectum and also the lower gut; and the loins are girt about with great pain. Unwise physicians think it to be a kidney disease [lenben adl] or spleen-ache [miltre wærc], but it is not so. Those who are sick in the kidneys pass blood and gravel in urine, whereas those who have spleen-ache: the spleen presses upon them and becomes hardened on the left side of the flank. Those who are belly-sick suffer in the rectum and in the lower abdomen, and quickly lose control of their bowels, and suffer cold, and sleep is drawn away from them, and they have hiccup, and wind gathers inwardly — in the stomach and in the small intestine.

XXXII. At the beginning this disease may be treated with difficulty in the same manner as dysentery [the outward-running], and afterwards with effort, if it has been unwisely left too long. At the outset one should, for a day or two together, wash and foment the chest with wine and with oil; and make a poultice of roses and barley meal mixed with wine and boiled in honey, and gathered together with oil in a mortar; lay it over the groin from the navel, and over the loins as far as the rectum, and wherever there is pain. Let blood flow from him, and place a cupping vessel [sete] thereon, or a horn, and draw the blood out; and anoint with oil, and wrap him up warmly, for cold is a great aggravator of the disease.

entities_mentioned.name: wamb (belly/stomach)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'wamb' refers broadly to the belly or womb; here used for the digestive abdomen.

entities_mentioned.name: colon
entities_mentioned.notes: Directly named 'colon' in the English gloss; Old English 'bæcþearm' (back-gut) also used.

entities_mentioned.name: small guts (smæl þearm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'smæl þearm' literally 'slender gut'.

entities_mentioned.name: milte / milt
entities_mentioned.notes: Old English 'milte'; the text explicitly distinguishes spleen-ache from the wamb disease being described.

entities_mentioned.name: lenben adl (kidney disease)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Renal/kidney disorder, possibly renal calculi given mention of blood and gravel in urine
entities_mentioned.notes: Text notes that kidney disease causes haematuria and gravel, distinguishing it from the intestinal condition.

entities_mentioned.name: rosan (roses)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a poultice preparation combined with barley meal, wine, honey, and oil applied to the abdomen and loins.

entities_mentioned.name: berene mele (barley meal)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingredient in the topical poultice; a common emollient base in medieval medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: win (wine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both as a fomentation for the chest and as a vehicle for the poultice mixture.

entities_mentioned.name: hunig (honey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to boil/bind the poultice ingredients.

entities_mentioned.name: ele (oil)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle in the poultice and for anointing the patient after bloodletting.

entities_mentioned.name: horn / sete (cupping horn / cupping vessel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for wet cupping to draw blood; both a horn and a 'sete' (cup/vessel) are mentioned as alternatives.

results.translator_notes: The segment is a bilingual source: an early Modern English translation (Cockayne's) is interleaved with the Old English manuscript text (from the Leech Book, Bald's Leechbook II, fols. 87a–87b). The Old English text has been lightly normalised for transcription here; editorial footnotes in the original indicate minor manuscript emendations (additions and omissions of the conjunction '⁊'). 'Tenesmus' is identified in a footnote by Cockayne as the condition of straining to defecate without being able to do so — the classical term fits the description given. The condition described overall resembles chronic inflammatory bowel disease, colitis, or severe dysentery with tenesmus, rectal bleeding, and altered bowel habits. Chapter XXXII begins immediately after and describes the initial treatment regimen, which includes fomentation, poulticing, and bloodletting by cupping. The word 'imho' in the Old English is interpreted as 'hiccup' (hiccough); it may also relate to nausea or belching. 'Share' (Old English 'sceare') refers to the pubic/groin region. 'Sete' is interpreted as a cupping vessel placed over the bloodletting site.
